ions and eco- nomic considerations (Whitfield et al., 2009). Overall, the support for further nuclear investments in the European Union is fairly weak (Eurobarometer, 2022). To successfully navigate a pathway toward a low-carbon and sus- tainable future, it is essential to understand how different social and value-based dimensions influence energy policies. This article aims to contribute to the literature by exploring factors that determine energy opinions, focusing on the polarization of wind

iterature by exploring factors that determine energy opinions, focusing on the polarization of wind and nuclear preferences in Sweden. Sweden is an interesting case study, as it is a country with a high level of both wind and nuclear energy in its energy mix, yet one where energy policies are marked by deep political tensions and polar- ization. The study draws conclusions from a large-scale survey con- ducted in Sweden during 2023, including over 5200 respondents, who were randomly selected and

y con- ducted in Sweden during 2023, including over 5200 respondents, who were randomly selected and representative of the wider Swedish population. The results show that low-carbon energy investments in Sweden are likely to encounter resistance due to a sizable antagonistic minority who are strongly opposed to either wind or nuclear energy. Interestingly, among those with traditional, nationalistic, and authoritarian values and right leaning political ideology, the enthusiasm for nuclear energy

ic, and authoritarian values and right leaning political ideology, the enthusiasm for nuclear energy seems to reduce the closer a new nuclear power plant would be to their own residences. The study highlights the importance of recognizing the sociopsychological dimensions within political frameworks aiming for a transition toward a low-carbon energy system. 2. Overview of relevant literature Attitudes to wind and nuclear power are divisive in many countries. Both of these energy technologies gen

udes to wind and nuclear power are divisive in many countries. Both of these energy technologies generate different social emotions in different societal groups, often related to beliefs about benefits and risks associated with the respective technology. Factors influencing attitudes to different energy options tend to be contextual and they vary across different countries (Arndt, 2023; Wang and Kim, 2018; Nguyen and Yim, 2018). Opinions about nuclear energy are particularly influenced by normat

im, 2018; Nguyen and Yim, 2018). Opinions about nuclear energy are particularly influenced by normative aspects related to the risk of accidents, uranium extraction, the treatment of radioactive waste, and its association with the military industry (Siegrist and Visschers, 2013; Keller et al., 2012; Groh and Mo¨llendorff, 2020; Siegrist et al., 2014). Support for nuclear energy has clearly been affected by the three major accidents worldwide—Three Mile Island, Chernobyl, and Fukushima—after whic

fected by the three major accidents worldwide—Three Mile Island, Chernobyl, and Fukushima—after which public attitudes turned more negative (Richard Eiser et al., 1990; Visschers and Siegrist, 2013). Nuclear power can, on the other hand, also be associated with energy security and economic prosperity (Slovic et al., 2000; Arndt, 2023), and social acceptance can, thus, be described as a trade-off be- tween risks and potential economic benefits (Finucane et al., 2000; Slovic et al., 2004). People’

e- tween risks and potential economic benefits (Finucane et al., 2000; Slovic et al., 2004). People’s perceptions of risks and benefits differ, however, depending on individual-level factors. Aspects that have been demonstrated to in- fluence opinions are political ideology, gender, income, and education (Costa-Font et al., 2008; Sundstro¨m and McCright, 2016; Gupta et al., 2019; Spence et al., 2010; Hazboun and Boudet, 2020), as well as institutional and country-level factors, such as the natio

10; Hazboun and Boudet, 2020), as well as institutional and country-level factors, such as the national energy mix and political aspects (Wang and Kim, 2018; Bohdanowicz et al., 2023). 2 E n e r g y P o l i c y 198 (2025) 114492 Renewable energy is not associated with similar risk perceptions, and in many countries, wind energy is among the most popular energy options (Arndt, 2023). Attitudes expressed at nation-level tend to diverge, however, from the views of the communities living in the prox

essed at nation-level tend to diverge, however, from the views of the communities living in the proximity of wind power investment (Krekel and Zerrahn, 2017; Dug- stad et al., 2020). Bell et al. (2005)expressed accordingly that a “social gap” tends to arise between the high level of public support at the na- tional level, and the low level of successful implementation of wind energy projects. The distance of wind energy sitings from peoples’ residence, the so- called proximity effect, has shown

distance of wind energy sitings from peoples’ residence, the so- called proximity effect, has shown to have a strong impact on opinions (Carley et al., 2020; Karasmanaki and Tsantopoulos, 2021). Early studies on social acceptance of wind power focused accordingly on the proximity effect or so-called NIMBY-ism (not-in-my-back-yard) (Batel et al., 2013; Fast, 2013). However, there is criticism of the “NIMBY-- term”, which is sometimes used as a method to discredit opponents (Horst, 2007). While no

e “NIMBY-- term”, which is sometimes used as a method to discredit opponents (Horst, 2007). While noise and visual intrusion of wind farms have shown to influence public attitudes (Carley et al., 2020; Krekel and Zerrahn, 2017), opposing responses are often based on more complex aspects (Dermont et al., 2017; Batel, 2020). Devine-Wright (2009), Horst (2007) and Dugstad et al. (2023) show that socio-cultural attachment to the place of living is of relevance, and that opposition can be seen as “pl

o-cultural attachment to the place of living is of relevance, and that opposition can be seen as “place-protective action”. Public acceptance of wind power is often influenced by the perceived environmental impacts of the wind farm location, and to what extent biodiversity in landscapes are affected (Dugstad et al., 2020). Biodiversity concerns differ, however, between different communities, and in Sweden, they are of particular relevance for Sami communities (Ramasar et al., 2022; Ek and Matti,

n Sweden, they are of particular relevance for Sami communities (Ramasar et al., 2022; Ek and Matti, 2015). Hence, careful analysis of the spatial allocation of wind farms is important when addressing inherent trade-offs between local dis- amenities, biodiversity conservation, and climate change considerations (Kati et al., 2021; Grimsrud et al., 2024). Geographic proximity does not have similar impacts on attitudes on nuclear energy, and studies show instead that people living close to nuclear

mpacts on attitudes on nuclear energy, and studies show instead that people living close to nuclear power plants are more positively inclined than those living further away (Greenberg, 2009; Uji et al., 2021; Venables et al., 2012; Cale and Kromer, 2015). However, few studies have captured attitudes in relation to proximity prior to the construction process. It is likely that new nuclear power plant establishments in landscapes, just like new wind-power plants, may evoke negative reactions motiv

nt establishments in landscapes, just like new wind-power plants, may evoke negative reactions motived by sense of place or concerns about their impacts on local wild life and biodiversity (Sovacool, 2012). Recent studies indicate, moreover, that opposition to wind power is associated with notions of being neglected or misrepresented (Walker and Devine-Wright, 2008). There are also research suggesting that perceptions of unfair distribution of the benefits and burdens of wind power influence acc

ting that perceptions of unfair distribution of the benefits and burdens of wind power influence acceptance (Huijts et al., 2012; De Luca et al., 2020). If profits from wind power generation is not redistributed locally, but transferred to the national level or to business corporations, individuals tend to see the construction of wind power as a kind of exploitation of the local landscape (Lindvall, 2023; Liebe et al., 2017; Knauf, 2022). The decision-making process and the ability of affected i

l, 2023; Liebe et al., 2017; Knauf, 2022). The decision-making process and the ability of affected individuals to participate in it, also impact acceptance of wind power (Walker and Baxter, 2017; Brennan and Rensburg, 2016; Le Maitre et al., 2023; Vuichard et al., 2022). Individuals who distrust public institutions, or who perceive the decision-making processes as unfair, non-transparent, or illegitimate, are also more likely to oppose wind farms (Gross, 2007; Liebe et al., 2017). Perceptions of

itimate, are also more likely to oppose wind farms (Gross, 2007; Liebe et al., 2017). Perceptions of procedural and distributional justice have also been shown to influence public attitudes to nuclear energy (Visschers and Siegrist, 2012; Besley, 2012), although the research on this matter is limited. Attitudes to different energy options are, however, not only deter- mined by aspects related to fairness and risks, but also by other types of personal norms, values, and ideological orientations.

airness and risks, but also by other types of personal norms, values, and ideological orientations. A particularly strong determinant for both wind and nuclear energy attitudes is envi- ronmental concern. Individuals who prioritize environmental protection 2 D. Lindvall et al. over economic aspects, are found to be more skeptical about nuclear energy, while those who perceive nuclear energy as economically beneficial are more prone to downplay environmental hazards (Pampel, 2011; Badora et al.,

nomically beneficial are more prone to downplay environmental hazards (Pampel, 2011; Badora et al., 2021; Besley and Oh, 2014; Corner et al., 2011). Individuals with pro-environmental attitudes tend to, likewise, be more positive to wind energy (Azarova et al., 2019; Knauf, 2022), although concerns of the local environmental impacts may also motivate resis- tance (Dugstad et al., 2020). Some studies indicate that climate change concern has become less relevant for nuclear power acceptance (Bohda

es indicate that climate change concern has become less relevant for nuclear power acceptance (Bohdanowicz et al., 2023), yet other studies have not found evidence of this trend (Sonnberger et al., 2021; Arndt, 2023). Several studies suggest, moreover, that attitudes toward nuclear energy are associated with political ideology. Individuals who define themselves politically to the left tend to be more positive to wind power (Karlstrøm and Ryghaug 2014; Clulow et al., 2021), while the opposite is

ore positive to wind power (Karlstrøm and Ryghaug 2014; Clulow et al., 2021), while the opposite is true for nuclear energy (Whitfield et al., 2009; Costa-Font et al., 2008; Pampel, 2011; Clulow et al., 2021). Worldviews are also relevant, and individuals who express individualistic or hierarchical values tend to be more supportive of nuclear energy (Van De Graaff, 2016; Whitfield et al., 2009), while wind power is preferred by individuals who express egalitarian and ecological values (Bidwell,

hile wind power is preferred by individuals who express egalitarian and ecological values (Bidwell, 2013). As a result, attitudes to wind and nuclear energy are prone to po- litical polarization (Perlaviciute and Steg, 2015; Roddis et al., 2019). The association between ideology and values, and public attitudes to different energy technologies, can therefore be influenced by political narratives or political cues (Latr´e et al., 2019), and media messaging and ˙ political debates (Ho and Kristian

r political cues (Latr´e et al., 2019), and media messaging and ˙ political debates (Ho and Kristiansen, 2019; Zuk, 2023; Kojo et al., 2020). Some studies have also shown that attitudes can be exploited by economic agents or interest groups advocating for or against the usage of renewable energy (Johnstone et al., 2017). 3. Energy policies in Sweden 3.1. The polarised debate on nuclear energy Sweden is a country where energy policies have been fraught with difficult political disputes for over f

en is a country where energy policies have been fraught with difficult political disputes for over five decades. In the 1970s, Sweden developed a nuclear energy program, commissioning 12 nuclear re- actors. These reactors were constructed between 1972 and 1980, mak- ing Sweden the country with the highest percentage of nuclear power usage per capita in the world (Kåberger, 2002). The construction of nuclear power plants was partly motivated by the needs and demands of the industry, but it was al

of nuclear power plants was partly motivated by the needs and demands of the industry, but it was also a response to the 1973 oil price shock and the decision to halt the expansion of hydropower to protect the few remaining unexploited rivers in Sweden (Vedung, 2002). Initially, there was strong interparty consensus for the nuclear energy program; how- ever, the expansion became an increasingly contentious political issue, partly spurred by the anti-nuclear stance taken by the Centre Party (Vedu

ntentious political issue, partly spurred by the anti-nuclear stance taken by the Centre Party (Vedung, 1979). From 1976, nuclear energy emerged as one of the most disruptive issues in Swedish politics, causing a government crisis in 1978 and profoundly affecting the 1979 election results (Holmberg, 2022). Following the accident at Three Mile Island, anti-nuclear senti- ments grew stronger, and to defuse the issue, a public referendum was held in 1980. A slight majority of voters supported a gra

defuse the issue, a public referendum was held in 1980. A slight majority of voters supported a gradual phase-out of nuclear power in the referendum; however, the process of phasing out nuclear energy did not begin until 1999, when the first reactor was decommissioned. Since then, another five reactors have been closed down (Holmberg, 2009). At the time of the referendum, opinions on nuclear energy were not initially influenced by ideological orientations but were determined by factors such as

gy were not initially influenced by ideological orientations but were determined by factors such as risk perception and environmental concern (Holmberg, 2018). Over time, the issue became increasingly politically polarized, and by 2021, right-leaning voters were, according to one survey, 58 percentage points more likely to favor nuclear power than left-leaning voters (Holmberg, 2022). In an attempt to depoliticize energy policies, an interparty energy agreement was negotiated by all major politi

t to depoliticize energy policies, an interparty energy agreement was negotiated by all major political 3 E n e r g y P o l i c y 198 (2025) 114492 parties in 2016. According to this agreement, the existing reactors were allowed to be replaced, but without any public subsidies. A long-term target of 100 percent renewable electricity production by 2040 was also adopted, and the so-called electricity certification system, benefiting renewable energy production, was prolonged and expanded (prop: 20

y certification system, benefiting renewable energy production, was prolonged and expanded (prop: 2017/18:228). In 2019, the agreement was dissolved following the departure of two conservative parties, which advocated for the continued operation of two reactors at the Ringhals power plant. The debate on nuclear energy then intensified, and in the 2022 election campaign, the pro-nuclear conservative parties ran on a proposal to construct new reactors, while the center-liberal, green, and left-lea

e parties ran on a proposal to construct new reactors, while the center-liberal, green, and left-leaning parties took neutral or skeptical stances (Holmberg, 2022). The debate was also influenced by the rising popularity of the Sweden Democrats, a nationalist and right-wing populist party, which strongly advocated for nuclear power and opposed wind power development. The conservative government that took power in 2022, with collaborative support from the Sweden Democrats, declared its intention

at took power in 2022, with collaborative support from the Sweden Democrats, declared its intention to restart one of the decommissioned reactors and grant financial subsidies to any private companies willing to construct new power plants. In 2023, the govern- ment accordingly appointed an inquiry to investigate the construction of new nuclear power plants in Sweden (Regeringen, 2023a). By the end of 2023, the government also presented its Climate Action Plan, in compliance with the Climate Act,

of 2023, the government also presented its Climate Action Plan, in compliance with the Climate Act, in which it claimed that “… in Sweden nuclear power is the single most important measure to reduce our emission nationally …” (Regeringen, 2023b, p. 10). 3.2. The divided support for wind power The expansion of wind power has also become an issue characterized by growing political tension. Wind power has developed rapidly in Sweden, and in between 2001 and 2021, the installed effect increased by

er has developed rapidly in Sweden, and in between 2001 and 2021, the installed effect increased by almost ten times (Swedish Energy Agency, 2021). The expansion of wind power has been widely supported in society, yet in recent years, atti- tudes has become increasingly polarized (Jo¨nsson, 2022). Just like the debate on nuclear power, political opinions are divided between conservative parties, which express anti-wind power rhetoric, and the more supportive center-liberal, green, and left-leani

hich express anti-wind power rhetoric, and the more supportive center-liberal, green, and left-leaning parties (Axelsson et al., 2023). At the local level, wind power has become a particularly divisive issue. The Environmental Act and the municipal planning monopoly grant municipalities the power to decline con- struction permits for wind power sites, colloquially referred to as the “municipal veto.” The number of municipal rejections has increased in recent years, and in 2021, around 80 percent

o.” The number of municipal rejections has increased in recent years, and in 2021, around 80 percent of all construction appli- cations were declined (Westander, 2022). A key reason for these re- jections is the lack of formal compensation for people living near wind power sites (Lindvall, 2023). As a result, the government commissioned a public inquiry in 2021 to present proposals for a more predictable municipal siting process (SOU, 2022:53), and in 2022, another inquiry was appointed to intro

ictable municipal siting process (SOU, 2022:53), and in 2022, another inquiry was appointed to introduce local incentives for wind power develop- ment (SOU, 2023:18). The new conservative government, elected in 2022, expressed a negative stance on wind power during the election campaign and has withdrawn public subsidies for the connection cost of offshore wind power to the power grid. In its Climate Action Plan, the government eased its negative policy stance towards wind power, although it did

ate Action Plan, the government eased its negative policy stance towards wind power, although it did not present any proposals for facilitating wind power development or overcoming acceptance barriers (Regeringen, 2023b). 4. Research hypotheses, methodology and data material 4.1. Hypothesizing determinants for acceptance of energy investment The aim of this article is to examine potential determinants of social attitudes to wind and nuclear energy, based on the results of an exploratory survey.

ants of social attitudes to wind and nuclear energy, based on the results of an exploratory survey. Previous studies have demonstrated that environ- mental concern is a key determinant for both wind and nuclear power, but also that political attitudes and values, such as ideology, 3 D. Lindvall et al. worldviews, and personal identity, influence attitudes to these energy options (Karlstrøm and Ryghaug 2014; Clulow et al., 2021; Pampel, 2011; Whitfield et al., 2009). Based on these previous findi

haug 2014; Clulow et al., 2021; Pampel, 2011; Whitfield et al., 2009). Based on these previous findings, we as- sume that individuals with right-leaning attitudes and low climate concern, express higher support to nuclear energy, while left-leaning individuals with high climate concern, express higher support for wind power. We also assume that worldviews, that correspond with traditional, authoritarian, and nationalistic (TAN), or green, alternative, and libertarian (GAL) values (Hooghe et al.,

tarian, and nationalistic (TAN), or green, alternative, and libertarian (GAL) values (Hooghe et al., 2002), predict energy preferences, as well as political and institutional trust. To explore these correlations, we will test the following hypothesis. H1. Value -based orientations influence peoples’ propensity to support nuclear and wind power. In a situation with a high level of political polarization between different energy policies, it is likely that energy preferences are deter- mined by th

ation between different energy policies, it is likely that energy preferences are deter- mined by the attitudes toward one technology. Such effects can indicate that attitudes are determined by political cues (Latr´e et al., 2019) or politically motivated reasoning (Kahan, 2013), rather than environ- mental concerns, risk perceptions, economic benefits, energy security or other factual considerations. There could, thus, be a correlation between support for nuclear power and negative attitudes to

ons. There could, thus, be a correlation between support for nuclear power and negative attitudes to wind power, while the correlation is reversed for support for wind power. Such a rela- tionship could indicate that polarization is a factor that can explain at- titudes. To explore this, we will test the following hypothesis. H2. The propensity to support nuclear power is determined by nega- tive attitudes toward wind power. Another factor that has been shown to strongly influence support for wi

attitudes toward wind power. Another factor that has been shown to strongly influence support for wind power is the location of the sitings (Batel et al., 2013; Carley et al., 2020). The closer a wind farm is constructed to where people live, the less likely they are to accept it. This proximity effect has not previously been shown to strongly affect people’s attitudes to nuclear power, yet it is uncertain whether people hold supportive attitudes prior to the con- struction process. It is, moreo

uncertain whether people hold supportive attitudes prior to the con- struction process. It is, moreover, not clear how value-based orientations interact with, or alleviate, the proximity effect, which could indicate how strongly attitudes are underpinned by cultural values. To explore these associations, we test the following three hypotheses. H3. The proximity of a wind power project predicts attitudes toward wind power development. H4. The proximity of a nuclear power project predicts attitude

itudes toward wind power development. H4. The proximity of a nuclear power project predicts attitudes toward nuclear power development. H5. Value-based orientations mediate the proximity effect on attitudes toward nuclear and wind power. 4.2. Survey design and data The study is based on data from a large-scale public opinion survey on attitudes to climate and energy policies in Sweden. The survey was conducted within the research project Fairtrans by the survey company Skop. It included question

was conducted within the research project Fairtrans by the survey company Skop. It included questions on both climate and energy policies, and a subset of the questions was analyzed for this article. The sample was drawn from the population register and made as a non-proportional stratified sample in three age categories and divisions between different types of municipality, in order to attain a proportional repre- sentation. It was sent out in postal format, with possibilities for digital respo

roportional repre- sentation. It was sent out in postal format, with possibilities for digital responses, to 33 432 respondents aged 18 to 84 in between 25 April and 23 August, 2023. A post-stratification methodology was applied to make the sample representative of the total population. After weighting the dataset on a number of core demographics to make it representative of the Swedish population, a total of 5280 respondents were included in the dataset. More information about the data collecti

, a total of 5280 respondents were included in the dataset. More information about the data collection and stratifica- tion methodology is provided in the supplementary material to this article. To capture both general attitudes and proximity effects, participants 4 E n e r g y P o l i c y 198 (2025) 114492 were asked to respond to three statements regarding each energy tech- nology. The statements were phrased; To speed up the transformation to a fossil free society, I support further construct

s were phrased; To speed up the transformation to a fossil free society, I support further construction of wind/nuclear power in Sweden. Thereafter, the respondents were asked if they support (1) construction of wind/nuclear power in their municipality and (2) construction of wind/nuclear power within 5 km from their home. Five kilometers was estimated to be considered as intrusive, realistic, and comparable between the two energy options. For each statement, the respondents were asked to indica

comparable between the two energy options. For each statement, the respondents were asked to indicate their level of agreement on a seven- point scale. The scale was anchored at 1 = Completely disagree,1 4 = Neither/nor, and 7 =Completely agree. We thereafter designed questions on value-based orientations, aspiring to both capture positions on the left/right political spectrum and social ideals and worldviews. On the later issue, we used a scale inspired by the GAL (Green/Alternative/Libertaria

nd worldviews. On the later issue, we used a scale inspired by the GAL (Green/Alternative/Libertarian) vs. TAN (Tradi- tional/Authoritarian/Nationalistic) framework (Hooghe et al., 2002). GAL/TAN values are typically assessed using a set of value judgments regarding aspects such as multiculturalism, international solidarity, gender equality on one end, and conservatism and national community on the other (Hobolt et al., 2021). GAL values were assessed on the re- sponses to the following two ques

e other (Hobolt et al., 2021). GAL values were assessed on the re- sponses to the following two questions: “Society is enriched by open bor- ders, global cooperation and international solidarity” and “It is important to promote equality between men and women”. TAN was assessed on the following two questions: “In a secure society, immigration is low and the majority of the inhabitants share the same culture” and “In a secure society, law and order is maintained by a strong police force and a judi

e culture” and “In a secure society, law and order is maintained by a strong police force and a judiciary that can sentence cognizable punishments for criminals”. GAL is the weighted re- sponses of the two first questions, and the opposite for TAN. Two questions aimed to measure trust were created, asking re- spondents to assess if they believed politicians to most often act in the public interests (political trust) and if public authorities (governmental trust) most often act competently, effic

s (political trust) and if public authorities (governmental trust) most often act competently, efficiently and in accordance with the rule of law. The same response scale as described above was used. To capture environmental concern, a subset of Schultz’s scale (Wesley schultz, 2001) was used. Respondents were asked to indicate their level of concern about environmental problems and their conse- quences to (1) myself, (2) all people, (3) my lifestyle, (4) people close to me, (5) animals and plan

uences to (1) myself, (2) all people, (3) my lifestyle, (4) people close to me, (5) animals and plants, (6) future generations, and (7) children, on a seven-point scale. The scale was anchored at 1 =Not at all concerned and 7 =Very concerned. The responses were collated into an index assessing environmental concern. Other individual characteristics gathered in the survey regarded in- come, education, place of living (rural/urban), and gender. By conducting a series of OLS regression models, we e

on, place of living (rural/urban), and gender. Discussion Our study reveals that individual values notably forecast attitudes towards both wind or nuclear power, aligning with our initial hypoth- esis. The transition to renewable energy sources is affected by dynamics that go beyond technical issues, being part of a political, social, cultural, and spatial transformation. To facilitate a successful transition from fossil fuels to renewables next to the strategic site planning of federal, state and/or local governments, it

els to renewables next to the strategic site planning of federal, state and/or local governments, it is important to obtain the acceptance of local communities (Huijts et al., 2012; Warren et al., 2005). To enable a socially just energy transition, a better understanding of the concerns and motivations of local communities is inevitable. These could generate valuable insights into how projects should be sited, designed, commu- nicated, and implemented (Olson-Hazboun et al., 2016). Understanding

uld be sited, designed, commu- nicated, and implemented (Olson-Hazboun et al., 2016). Therefore, we chose only one federal state (Bavaria) to ensure that the regulatory framework was the same. One common regulatory path for addressing acceptance-related problems is determining re- quirements for their proximity to residential areas (Masurowski et al., 2016; Watson et al., 2012). Bavaria has introduced a state regulation, the 10H rule, which defines the minimum dista

al., 2012). Bavaria has introduced a state regulation, the 10H rule, which defines the minimum distance between the wind turbine and residential areas as at least ten times the total height of the wind turbine (Baugesetzbuch, 2014). With wind turbines reaching overall heights of 250 m, the 10H rule increases the challenge for mu- nicipalities to find appropriate project sites. Even though proximity has some effect on the perceptions of wind farms, the intensity of this effect is shaped by the no

y has some effect on the perceptions of wind farms, the intensity of this effect is shaped by the norms and values of the affected communities (van der Horst, 2007). Political power can produce and promote certain norms in social systems (Fraser, 2014), and institutional norms have the potential to regulate market dynamics (Nyborg et al., 2016). Further, this paper focuses on wind energy projects in forest areas because some of the main concerns of those who oppose wind energy projects are relat

in forest areas because some of the main concerns of those who oppose wind energy projects are related to landscape protection and impacts on biodiversity, especially in forest areas (Dai et al., 2015). While deploying wind parks in forest areas is complex in terms of local acceptance in Germany, developing wind energy in such zones is necessary for achieving climate and renewable energy goals (FA Wind, 2021). Wind turbines in forested areas are situated almost exclusively in the southern federa

Wind, 2021). Wind turbines in forested areas are situated almost exclusively in the southern federal states, namely, Rhineland-Palatinate, Hessen, Bavaria, Baden-Württemberg, and North Rhine-Westphalia (Bunzel et al., 2019). In Bavaria, forests account for around 37% of the land area, making it the state with the greatest forest coverage among all 16 German states (FA Wind, 2021). Analysing local actors that influence community norms can increase understanding of conflicts and dynamics related

actors that influence community norms can increase understanding of conflicts and dynamics related to wind energy projects (Karakislak et al., 2021). This paper aims to fill the gaps in the literature by exploring the relationship between local political figures and social norms and examining their effect on local responses to wind energy projects. It does so by addressing the following research questions: 1) What is the role of elected mayors in wind energy projects? 2) What influence do their

questions: 1) What is the role of elected mayors in wind energy projects? 2) What influence do their opinions have on local responses? and 3) What actors and processes influence local responses over time? Section 5 illustrates the results of the four case studies. The paper concludes in section 6 by reviewing the main findings and suggesting implications for policymakers and practitioners. 2. Social norms, political agency and local responses This paper applies the conceptual framework described by Kar- akis

itical agency and local responses This paper applies the conceptual framework described by Kar- akislak et al. (2021). This two-dimensional framework specifies factors and variables for analysing the definitions, influencing factors, and impacts of social norms as well as perceived justice within communities. The first element of the framework proposes that three groups of in- dividuals are influential in terms of the norms that impact local re- sponses: community spokespersons, political figure

ntial in terms of the norms that impact local re- sponses: community spokespersons, political figures, and opposition groups. These actor groups come to power to influence public opinion in situations where there is uncertainty or conflicts, such as unequal dis- tribution of benefits. Community spokespersons have social influence and strong ties within their groups, individuals with resources and networks capable of impacting others (Karakislak et al., 2021). networks capable of impacting others (Karakislak et al., 2021). Social norms How members of the public respond to the social and environmental changes around them is widely linked to the expected reactions of others. These

These conditional expectations about how people will react, or how they should react, are conditional behavioural regularities - or so- cial norms (Bicchieri and Mercier, 2014). Overcoming conflicts associated with energy projects requires a deeper understanding of the embedded influences and values related to the social context (Upham and Johansen, 2020). There tends to be a gap between what others ty

ted to the social context (Upham and Johansen, 2020). There tends to be a gap between what others typically do (descriptive norms) and what ought to be done (injunctive norms) (White et al., 2009). The distinction between intention and behaviour is also relevant when estimating responses to wind energy projects (Sokoloski et al., 2018). of normative beliefs about renewable energy technologies in general (Hübner et al., 2023; Reyes-Mercado and Rajagopal, 2017). Moreover, social norms or pressure from family, friends, and neighbours, alongside political actors, have the potential to influence local responses in both directions (Huijts et al., 2012). The role of local political figures Local politicians are directly affected by public opinion, while in turn their engagement in wind energy projects affects local responses (Friedl and Reichl, 2016). ommunity acceptance of wind en- ergy projects. Active local support for community-led projects3 strongly shapes the distribution of power and the relationships between local actors (Bell et al., 2013). As key agents of societal inclusion and local policies, the needs and views of the mayors should be better understood (Gürtler and Herberg, 2021). om their case studies that the role of the mayor as a policy entrepreneur in implementing 100% renewable energy systems is crucial. become advocates or leaders of renewable energy projects that influence local citizens (Honva´ri and Kukorelli, 2018). The power of the local politicians may be applied in different ways. in environmental politics: “power with”, which includes learning and cooperation; “power to”, which involves resistance and empowerment; and “power over”, which refers to manipulation. Local responses to wind energy projects Local responses influence the outcome of wind energy projects directly (e.g., through referendums) but also indirectly through their influence on local politicians (Jolivet and Heiskanen, 2010). However, it is important to understand local responses not as an obstacle to the energy transiti

0). However, it is important to understand local responses not as an obstacle to the energy transition but rather as an aspiration to increase their under- standing (Devine-Wright, 2007). Local responses are multi-layered and dynamic and can range from support to opposition and indifference, resistance, tolerance, or accep- tance (Batel and Devine-Wright, 2015; Walker et al., 2018). Energy-related social science research has been developing a more nuanced understanding of local responses (Walker

social science research has been developing a more nuanced understanding of local responses (Walker et al., 2018) and moved away from the NIMBY (not-in-my-backyard concept), which has been criticised for labelling opposition groups as self-interested or irrational (Kempton et al., 2005; Wolsink, 2006). Discussions about individuals’ attitudes, behaviours, and responses are adapted to different concepts and theoretical frameworks (Fast and Mabee, 2015; Huijts et al., 2012). Estimating and unders

ncepts and theoretical frameworks (Fast and Mabee, 2015; Huijts et al., 2012). Estimating and understanding local responses also requires that local actors acknowledge and cope with the emotions associated with wind energy (Perlaviciute et al., 2018). How people feel about wind energy in general, and their environmental beliefs might not reflect how they respond to projects. Positive associations about wind power tend to be more abstract than negative ones, resulting in the opponents’ responses

about wind power tend to be more abstract than negative ones, resulting in the opponents’ responses being more clearly elaborated (Cousse et al., 2020). Being directly affected by a project tends to be a strong driver of people’s attitudes; thus, this requires better anticipation (Russell and Firestone, 2021). erally engage in local resistance. Warren et al. (2005) claim that local opposition involves a minority of people but that they receive more attention from the press. On the other hand, in locally rooted projects, some actors have the potential to foster positive emo- tions and opinions about wind energy (van der Schoor and Scholtens, 2015). resulted in project abandonment. However, more recently, decisions have tended to be pro-wind energy (Langer et al., 2016). A similar trend can be perceived in Bavaria. Research methodology The aim of this study is to investigate how local politicians and social norms influence local responses to wind energy projects. In order to do that, the paper analyses four case studies in Bavaria and examines the processes around these wind energy projects. In this section, we will explain our case study selection, present our methods, and conclude with

. In this section, we will explain our case study selection, present our methods, and conclude with the analysis. 3.1. Case studies Germany is a relevant context for examining the local dynamics of wind energy development for three reasons. First, Germany’s phase-out of nuclear and coal energy requires a substantial expansion of renewable energy. Second, Germany has great potential for wind energy expansion, and third, the deployment faces various challenges. The Bavarian state government introd

expansion, and third, the deployment faces various challenges. The Bavarian state government introduced the Bavarian Energy Ac- tion Program in 2019 to spur the deployment of 300 new wind turbines with a 1 GW newly installed capacity (StMWi, 2019). However, this significant goal of expanding wind energy and informational instruments has met regulatory setbacks. Since there is no national regulation about wind turbines in forest areas, each state employs restrictions on planning that steer wind

on about wind turbines in forest areas, each state employs restrictions on planning that steer wind energy’s expansion (Bunzel et al., 2019). Particularly in Bavaria, forest areas carry a large potential for wind power, but – as Ludwig and Bosch (2014) suggest – this requires alternative socio-ecological integration models. In November 2014, Bavaria intro- duced the 10H rule, which defines the minimum distance from the resi- dential areas to the closest wind turbine as ten times the turbine’s to

imum distance from the resi- dential areas to the closest wind turbine as ten times the turbine’s total height (Baugesetzbuch, 2014). There are recent changes to 10H that allow dropping the distance rules in priority and reserved areas for wind energy (e.g., motorways and forest areas). However, local municipalities can define exceptions to the 10H rule in their local setting through urban land use plans that allow wind energy projects within the 10H limits. Municipal actors also have to balance

e plans that allow wind energy projects within the 10H limits. Municipal actors also have to balance the local and national interests in situations when hierarchical interventions such as the 10H rule exist (Verhoeven et al., 2022). Despite the intention to avoid conflicts, local projects still face strong opposition in Bavaria (Langer et al., 2016). In practice, the 10H rule can put additional pressure on local municipalities. First, the 10H rule can take power away from municipalities, and sec

essure on local municipalities. First, the 10H rule can take power away from municipalities, and second, the exception to the rule puts pressure on them since they have to justify why a distance lower than 10H is allowed (Watson et al., 2012). This often results in considerable resistance from the local population but also from neighbouring municipalities (Langer et al., 2016). Bavaria is a particularly interesting area for wind energy due to its significant role in the German energy transition.

ularly interesting area for wind energy due to its significant role in the German energy transition. Bavaria ranks third on the list of German federal states in terms of its ambitions and imple- mentation targets of renewable energy to create socio-technical change (AEE, 2019). Thus, it is one of the states that prioritised the energy transition. However, the 10H rule has affected the expansion of wind energy and created further conflict. Most federal states in Germany have two types of mayors,

wind energy and created further conflict. Most federal states in Germany have two types of mayors, working voluntarily or full-time, depending on the municipalities’ size. There could be multiple mayors in Bavarian municipalities with more than 5000 inhabitants, the first mayor being the civil servant that the citizens directly elect. The first mayor is the chairperson of the council and head of administration. The local council elects the second or third mayor from their members. Their role is

administration. The local council elects the second or third mayor from their members. Their role is to represent their community similarly to other council members (GO, 1988). Three types of knowledge can be gathered through interviews: technical, process, and interpretative. First, facts and figures were explored which do not

rviews: technical, process, and interpretative. First, facts and figures were explored which do not depend on individuals, i.e. technical knowledge (Bogner and Menz, 2009). Here, we were interested in the project spe- cifics such as location, number of turbines, turbine height, MW capacity, ownership structures, and proximity to residential buildings. Process knowledge provides insight into processes and activities obtained through experience and direct involvement (Bogner and Menz, 2009). Here,

ses and activities obtained through experience and direct involvement (Bogner and Menz, 2009). Here, we were especially interested in participation structures, the ac- tors involved, communication strategies used, and the local responses to the projects. Karakis

context of thematic or qualitative content analysis (Gale et al., 2013; Ritchie et al., 4 I. Karakislak and N. Schneider 2003). It consists of organising the data to enable interpretation within and between cases to identify similarities and differences (Gale et al., 2013). This analysis was complemented by examining relevant docu- ments and reports to understand the Bavarian context better. In the first step, both authors reviewed all interview transcripts independently and inductively coded th

e first step, both authors reviewed all interview transcripts independently and inductively coded them by identifying themes. Case study descriptions This section describes the social and cultural context of the case studies and summarises the process of wind energy projects. The infor- mation presented here is collected from documents and interviews. Most of the landscape (80–90%) for all four locations was rur

llected from documents and interviews. Most of the landscape (80–90%) for all four locations was rural, divided between agriculture and forest areas. Their respective local economies depend on manufacturing, trading, transportation, and hos- pitality industries. According to the last federal and state elections, the towns supported political parties similarly. Germany’s Christian- democrat and conservative political party (CSU) received the most votes in all four towns. There are three male mayo

servative political party (CSU) received the most votes in all four towns. There are three male mayors (CS1, CS2 and CS4) and one female first mayor (CS3) in these municipalities. The gross annual household income of the residents in the towns was also similar (Bavarian State Office for Statistics, 2021). We, therefore, argue that the towns have a relatively similar socio-economic and cultural context (see Fig. 1). Fig. 1. Case study areas in Bavaria, Ge 5 E n e r g y P o l i c y 1 76(2023)11350

From the analysis of the case studies, we were able to identify four main factors that influence the dynamics of local responses: the assessment of the mayor and the local council; other relevant stake- holders, such as project developers and oppositional groups; project communication, transparency and the processes; and

oject developers and oppositional groups; project communication, transparency and the processes; and changes caused by external events. 5.1. The assessment of the mayor and local council Many of our interviewees mentioned the importance of the mayor and the local council’s support for implementing wind energy projects and local acceptance. Our case studies confirm the findings in the literature that the approval of the mayor and the local council for a project seems to be pivotal to success (Bus

at the approval of the mayor and the local council for a project seems to be pivotal to success (Busch and McCormick, 2014; Gürtler and Herberg, 2021). Approval of a project by the mayor is no guarantee of successful implementation, but disapproval of a project proposal seems to hinder project development in the first place. Local political actors (e.g., local council member 1 of CS1) and project de- velopers (e.g., project planner of CS2) from our case studies indicated that the mayor’s opennes

de- velopers (e.g., project planner of CS2) from our case studies indicated that the mayor’s openness to potential projects is essential for wind energy development. Furthermore, during scanning potential case studies, we could not identify any wind energy projects opposed by a mayor. Our inability to find such projects suggests that approval might be a prerequisite in Bavaria for initiating a project, which our in- terviewees also confirmed. As local political figures, mayors are representative

ject, which our in- terviewees also confirmed. As local political figures, mayors are representatives of their com- munities but also their political parties or groups. We found that the local leadership of the mayors tends to be independent of the national standpoint of the affiliated political parties regarding the energy tran- sition, similar to Adesanya et al. (2020). Even though some participants argued that the political parties of the mayors and council members could steer responses to pr

ants argued that the political parties of the mayors and council members could steer responses to projects, we found no correlation between these factors in the case studies. The influence of mayors on the project outcome is twofold. Firstly, they can stop projects directly since they have to adapt the land use plan. The project developer of CS3 stated that they do not start projects without the approval of the local council and the mayor since they need to change the land use plan. The project

pproval of the local council and the mayor since they need to change the land use plan. The project developer explained that many projects fail because the mayor says “no” thinking “why should they get themselves into trouble?“. Secondly, mayors can also have an influence on local responses. They can play a mediating role between project developers, planners, and the local population, providing them with a strategic position through encouraging and inviting the public to participate in projects,

em with a strategic position through encouraging and inviting the public to participate in projects, addressing concerns, or acting as intermediaries. Their vision of their town and their leadership could significantly impact 8 E n e r g y P o l i c y 1 76(2023)113509 the project’s development. Nonetheless, mayoral support does not guarantee local support. Thus, the mayor’s support is essential but not sufficient for local acceptance. Our findings also indicate that mayors need the support of th

t not sufficient for local acceptance. Our findings also indicate that mayors need the support of the local council. Especially in municipalities where the projects need to be approved locally, council members directly impact outcomes. In turn, mayors and local council members are also directly affected by the re- actions to projects of the local population. They can face pressure from the public, which may affect their attitudes towards project develop- ment. Thus, the mayors’ responses can cha

which may affect their attitudes towards project develop- ment. Thus, the mayors’ responses can change over time, but their in- fluence on the local population can also change and is highly influenced by social norms and trust. In our case studies, mayors were elected representatives by the local communities, which portrays them as trusted leaders. Trust in mayors as stakeholders played an important role in the distinctive outcomes of these three projects, confirming other studies (Dwyer and Bi

ant role in the distinctive outcomes of these three projects, confirming other studies (Dwyer and Bidwell, 2019; Fast and Mabee, 2015; Titov et al., 2021). However, trust is not the sole explanatory factor for acceptance; other factors impact the relationship between the community and mayors, such as providing a clear vision, project communication, and project characteristics. The current mayor of CS1 and the previous mayor of CS2 have been in office for over ten years and have had long-lasting

CS1 and the previous mayor of CS2 have been in office for over ten years and have had long-lasting relationships with their local commu- nities. Here, the mayors were supporting and initiating the projects, and the local population supported them as well, which resulted in the project implementation in the end. Whereas, in CS4, the mayor has been in office for over 20 years and would be arguably perceived as a trusted local leader. However, his initial attempt to launch a project was not succes

perceived as a trusted local leader. However, his initial attempt to launch a project was not successful. Moreover, mayors have administrative boundaries in wind energy planning as most projects are spread across multiple municipalities. In CS1, neighbouring communities shared different percentages in finan- cial participation in the project, and the town that received lower ben- efits objected to the extension proposal. Thus, a mayor’s leadership in their community may not be sufficient to impa

the extension proposal. Thus, a mayor’s leadership in their community may not be sufficient to impact the project’s outcome. 5.2. Process of communication Almost all interviewees agreed that project communication is an essential part. It is not only important what is communicated, but also how, when, and by whom. It is essential to inform the public early about projects to prevent trust-related problems and to avoid the impression that everything is being managed and decided behind closed doors

roblems and to avoid the impression that everything is being managed and decided behind closed doors. This conclusion confirms the findings of many studies (Dai et al., 2015; Dermont et al., 2017; Wolsink, 2007). However, our case studies suggest that the early provision of information is not enough. The “right timing” is critical for avoiding raising more questions than it settles. Many of our interviewees agreed that there needs to be a balance be- tween informing early and having enough infor

terviewees agreed that there needs to be a balance be- tween informing early and having enough information to share. When informing early, many factors will be unknown, not assessed, or unde- cided. This implies that residents will not receive answers to every question they have, which may result in the feeling that local decision makers or project developers are not being entirely open and trans- parent. The mayor in CS4 saw the timing as one of the reasons for the initial failure of the projec

parent. The mayor in CS4 saw the timing as one of the reasons for the initial failure of the project. He considered that they had informed the public too early, leaving many questions unanswered. Similarly, the project developer of CS1 described the balance between providing early information and having enough information as a “tightrope walk”. Project communication is a process, and alongside the importance of timing, our interviewees agreed on the relevance of how information is provided and

e importance of timing, our interviewees agreed on the relevance of how information is provided and how the events are set up. In CS4, one of the reasons for the strong opposition was believed to be the online context of the public information meeting. A positive example is the information event of CS2. Here, our interviewees agreed on the positive influence of the set- up on the constructive dialogue that was enabled. Panel discussions do not seem appropriate to inform the local population abou

ogue that was enabled. Panel discussions do not seem appropriate to inform the local population about a planned project. Moreover, personal discussions and raising awareness within 8 I. Karakislak and N. Schneider the community regarding alternatives to wind energy were described as helpful. Therefore, how and when the public is informed strongly influences the local responses and, in turn, the project outcome. In other words, information should be communicated when there is considerable room to

oject outcome. In other words, information should be communicated when there is considerable room to elaborate, not too early when there are no proposals for siting or project features. Moreover, the context of information sessions tends to impact how meetings proceed. Further, it is crucial that the whole process of the project is explained and that the local population is informed about when they can expect which decision and the respective information. Therefore, the information flow needs to

y can expect which decision and the respective information. Therefore, the information flow needs to be consistent and the whole planning process needs to be transparent. 5.3. Other stakeholders Next to the timing, also the people behind the project are highly relevant to local responses. Our interviewees identified project de- velopers and oppositional groups as having an influence on local re- sponses and project outcomes. We identified that it is essential whether the community trusts the pro

ponses and project outcomes. We identified that it is essential whether the community trusts the project developer, similarly to the findings of previous studies (Dwyer and Bidwell, 2019; Kalkbrenner and Roosen, 2016). Confirming findings from the literature (Goedkoop and Devine-Wright, 2016; Walter, 2014), regional project developers were trusted more than external project developers. Alongside their project role, we analysed how they perceive local responses. Project developers tend to make as

heir project role, we analysed how they perceive local responses. Project developers tend to make assumptions about emotional responses toward energy projects. Whether they frame the latter as NIMBY responses and try to solve conflicts through compensation (Perlaviciute et al., 2018) or take the raised concerns seriously affects local responses. Therefore, it is not only relevant who the project developer is but also how they interpret their role, how they interact with the local population and

eveloper is but also how they interpret their role, how they interact with the local population and how they set up the project. Regional project developers were favoured in the four case studies over external corporations. The former were perceived as caring about the region and its inhabitants and trying to ensure that added value stayed in the region. They were perceived as attempting to minimise negative impacts on the local population and the environment. In contrast, the latter is perceive

se negative impacts on the local population and the environment. In contrast, the latter is perceived as only interested in maximising profit and output without caring too much about environmental and social consequences. In CS2 and CS3, concerns were raised by the public. However, they were addressed differently by the project developers. The project planner of CS3 argued that the public was prejudiced against the project from the beginning. In contrast, in CS2, an external developer was associ

udiced against the project from the beginning. In contrast, in CS2, an external developer was associated with a failed project, which the current developer had taken over. Moreover, the external developer of CS3 explained the advantages of the project for the town in terms of siting and distance from the res- idential areas, whereas the regional developer of CS2 emphasised first the social, environmental, and economic advantages for the community and only afterwards the profits for their company

nmental, and economic advantages for the community and only afterwards the profits for their company. The communication between the local population, municipality and the developer of CS3 was also found to be problematic due to the absence of the provision of information and leadership. Particularly in CS3, the project developer failed to be transparent and informative about the process, leaving unanswered questions and eventually avoiding contact with the local community. Introduction The European Green Deal promises a “just and inclusive transition” to net-zero emissions by 2050, a target it reaffirmed

al promises a “just and inclusive transition” to net-zero emissions by 2050, a target it reaffirmed in the EU’s recent Clean Industrial Deal [1–3]. A critical step to reaching net-zero emis- sions is phasing out coal power, increasing power generation from re- newables, reducing energy demand, and shifting non-electric energy use to carbon-free alternatives. This transformation will impact the Euro- pean labour market, including the overall level and types of jobs in the energy sector [4–8]. The

First, employment impacts significantly influence public and political support for climate policies, with job losses potent

Second, quantifying where and what types of jobs are created or lost is essential for designing effective just transition policies that support affected workers and communities. ies and emerging renewable energy sectors is critical for ensuring adequate workforce development programs can prevent labor shortages that could impede the transition itself. Extensive literature has examined the impact of renewable energy on employment, revealing both opportunities and challenges for labour markets during energy transitions. Early studies established that renewable energy technologies are generally more labour-intensive than fossil fuel alternatives, with solar photovoltaics s

logies are generally more labour-intensive than fossil fuel alternatives, with solar photovoltaics showing particularly high employment factors during installation phases [4,13]. Cross- country analyses consistently find that renewable energy deployment creates more jobs per unit of investment or energy generated compared to conventional energy sources, though with significant variation across technologies and regions [14,15]. Wind energy studies highlight sub- stantial employment in manufacturi

hnologies and regions [14,15]. Wind energy studies highlight sub- stantial employment in manufacturing and maintenance, while biomass and biofuels are strongly tied to agricultural jobs. However, the litera- ture also identifies critical challenges, including spatial mismatches between job losses in fossil fuel regions and renewable energy job cre- ation, skills gaps requiring extensive retraining programs, and temporal misalignments between job destruction and creation [6,10]. Methodo- logical

programs, and temporal misalignments between job destruction and creation [6,10]. Methodo- logical approaches have evolved from simple employment factor cal- culations to sophisticated input-output and computable general equilibrium models that capture indirect and induced economic effects [16,17]. Regional studies consistently show heterogeneous impacts, with coal-dependent areas facing disproportionate adjustment chal- lenges while regions with renewable energy potential experience employment

rtionate adjustment chal- lenges while regions with renewable energy potential experience employment gains [9]. Recent literature emphasizes the importance of policy design in determining net employment outcomes, particularly regarding just transition mechanisms, skills development programs, and industrial policies that influence where renewable energy manufacturing occurs [7,8,12]. Despite this extensive research, gaps remain in comprehensive, technology-disaggregated analyses that combine deta

xtensive research, gaps remain in comprehensive, technology-disaggregated analyses that combine detailed energy system modeling with employment factors across multiple scenarios and timeframes, particularly for policy- relevant assessments of transition pathways. The literature on re- skilling and workforce transitions in energy sectors reveals opportu- nities and constraints for worker mobility between fossil fuel and renewable energy industries. Skills mapping studies demonstrate sig- nificant

etween fossil fuel and renewable energy industries. Skills mapping studies demonstrate sig- nificant overlap in core competencies, particularly for technical trades like electricians, welders, and heavy equipment operators, with trans- ferability rates ranging from 30 to 70 % depending on specific occupa- tions [18,19]. Research on coal miner transitions shows that workers with maintenance and electrical backgrounds adapt most readily to wind and solar technician roles, often requiring several m

ctrical backgrounds adapt most readily to wind and solar technician roles, often requiring several months or more of supplementary training rather than complete career changes. This paper addresses the following research questions: How will Europe’s transition to net-zero emissions affect energy sector employ- ment across different technologies and regions, and are current just transition policies adequate to address the challenges on the labour market? While the net-zero transition will generat

adequate to address the challenges on the labour market? While the net-zero transition will generate substantial net employment gains, significant skills, geography, and timing mismatches between job losses and creation require more targeted policy in- terventions than currently provided, particularly regarding workforce development for renewable energy sectors. This paper proceeds in four steps. First, using integrated assessment modeling, we quantify direct energy employment changes under Ref

First, using integrated assessment modeling, we quantify direct energy employment changes under Reference and Net Zero scenarios. Second, we examine the geographic and sectoral distribution of employment impacts across European regions. Third, we analyze cur- rent Just Transition Fund allocations to assess policy alignment with 2 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 127 (2025) 104292 employment needs. Methodology and data We use the World Induced Technical Change Hybrid (WITCH) inte- grated assessment model to assess employment implications of reaching net-zero emissions by 2050 for EU member states and neighbouring countries. We couple this model with a comprehensive global dataset of employment factors [20] to project the future energy

s model with a comprehensive global dataset of employment factors [20] to project the future energy jobs under a Reference (or current policies) scenario and Net Zero scenario. We then examine existing policy support for this transition, focusing on the EU’s Just Transition Fund (JTF), which was set up as part of the European Green Deal to support “workers and regions to develop new skills and thrive in the green economy”.1We compare existing policy support to our findings on employment effects

ive in the green economy”.1We compare existing policy support to our findings on employment effects to identify the most significant policy gaps within the European Green Deal. 2.1. Assessing the employment implications of Europe’s net-zero pathway We used a supply chain approach [20] to identify and quantify the core activities within each energy sector. direct job categories for each energy technology and calcu- lated “employment factors” that represent full-time jobs per unit of energy or capacity (jobs/GW or jobs/PJ). We converted data from job- years/GW (the original units) to jobs/GW based on typical construc- tion duration to account for the temporary nature of construction jobs. s per GW to jobs per GW by dividing by technology-specific construction durations (ranging from 1 to 10 years), since these represent temporary employment concentrated during project development phases [21]. ical progress raises the productivity of both factors. enario-specific tra- jectories, with accelerated deployment under Net Zero potentially driving faster labor productivity improvements [22]. The second important assumption is how regional job intensities vary or converge over time due to spill-overs and cross-regional learning. Here, we as- sume that regional jobs intensities converge to the regional minimum values entirely by 2050 linearly, capturing the strong international learning [23]. industries. Induced effects emerge from spending patterns of newly employed workers and regional economic multipliers. Studies using input-output and computable general equilibrium models suggest these multiplier effects typically range from 1.5 to 3.0 for renewable energy investments, meaning each direct job creates 0.5 to 2.0 additional indirect and induced jobs [16,17]. ntries, Northern Europe and Eastern Europe). ,12]. Discussion Our analysis demonstrates that Europe’s Net Zero transition offers significant employment opportunities while creating critical challenges in job displacement and skills development. The nearly doubling of energy employment, driven primarily b

ob displacement and skills development. The nearly doubling of energy employment, driven primarily by solar PV and wind deployment, indicates that decarbonization can be a net positive for European labour markets. However, the heterogeneous distribution of benefits raises equity concerns, as coal and oil-dependent regions will disproportion- ately bear transition costs. The “global pool” of renewable energy manufacturing jobs presents a critical policy opportunity. With 80 % of solar PV and wind

ble energy manufacturing jobs presents a critical policy opportunity. With 80 % of solar PV and wind manufacturing jobs currently overseas and China dominating produc- tion, establishing European manufacturing capacity could multiply direct energy jobs by up to fivefold. This result aligns with recent pol- icies like the Net Zero Industry Act but requires coordinated industrial policies, R&D investment, workforce training, and potentially protective trade measures. To prioritise community energy more, in 2011 the Scottish Government added a goal of 500 MW community and locally-owned RE for 2020, which was later

Scottish Government added a goal of 500 MW community and locally-owned RE for 2020, which was later increased to 1 GW (Scottish Government, n.d.). In substantiating this goal, the Scottish government stressed that carrying out a CRE project can lead to skills development, increase of community cohesion and confidence, and support for local economic regeneration (Scottish Government, 2014). These policies and incentives connect Scotland’s drive to replace fossil fuels and cut carbon emissions to

policies and incentives connect Scotland’s drive to replace fossil fuels and cut carbon emissions to a belief in RE technologies’ po- tential for playing a vital role in strengthening communities. Owning renewables is assumed to help building local capacity and give com- munities financial autonomy to prioritise and address their own needs (HIE, 2015a). According to the Scottish Minister for Business Energy and Tourism, community energy represents a ‘tremendous potential to empower people to mak

ess Energy and Tourism, community energy represents a ‘tremendous potential to empower people to make the most of their own local resources’, (Local Energy Scotland, 2015). In the ministerial foreword of the first national Community Energy Policy Statement, he even envisions a central role for community and local energy expressing that it needs to be brought in ‘from the margins of energy policy to make it the central tenet of our future energy systems, where it has the potential to transform lo

Through ‘building close links with energy consumers through community-based organisations’ there would be a potential to ‘maximise the value of energy generated and to create socio-economic benefits’ (Scottish Government, 2015, p.1). This vision

nergy generated and to create socio-economic benefits’ (Scottish Government, 2015, p.1). This vision is part of a liberalist understanding of the UK Big Society discourse, characterising it as a progressive response to state and market failure and a wider delegitimization of representative politics. A central tenet of this discourse is that ‘“communities” are better placed to serve their own needs and make informed choices which are situated in the “grounded” experiences of their lives’ (Catney

and make informed choices which are situated in the “grounded” experiences of their lives’ (Catney et al., 2014, p. 717). Especially for relatively undiversified rural economies participation in CRE is expected to be a source of empowerment, and simultaneously strengthen social and economic capital (Slee and Harnmeijer, 2017). Certainly, local ownership is much more beneficial to communities than hosting a commercial equivalent as it leads to more local and collective benefits, which is proven

hosting a commercial equivalent as it leads to more local and collective benefits, which is proven by several economic impact studies (see e.g. Allan et al., 2008; Slee, 2015). In concrete terms, a commercial windfarm would bring in a community benefit fund of around £3000–4000,-/MW to compensate the local community for negative externalities and about £10.000,-/MW rent for the landowner, whereas a community scheme can generate over £100.000–150.000,-/MW per annum (Slee and Harnmeijer, 2017, p.

a community scheme can generate over £100.000–150.000,-/MW per annum (Slee and Harnmeijer, 2017, p.16). This already vast disparity gets even larger when taking into account that local ownership creates revenue spendings in the local economy, while external ownership re- sults in a high share of the revenues leaking away. Such investment can take place in a broad range of areas including health and social care, local regeneration, culture and heritage, local services and amenities, poverty miti

nd social care, local regeneration, culture and heritage, local services and amenities, poverty mitigation, social inclusion, sport and recreation, energy and recycling (HIE, 2015b). The actual investment areas will depend on the particular needs of the local community. Therefore, the specific in- vestments and whether these meet the most pressing needs in the community will likely affect the extent to which community members feel empowered by the RE project. Furthermore, as well as to financial

nt to which community members feel empowered by the RE project. Furthermore, as well as to financial benefits for the local economy, community empowerment is also attributed to the development process of CRE projects. Communities, and notably the smaller group most active in the project development, would be empowered by acquiring project development and RE related knowledge, forming new collabo- rations strengthening the social fabric of the community, and not least by the very experience of ov

rations strengthening the social fabric of the community, and not least by the very experience of overcoming setbacks and finally accomplish- ing a challenging project with local volunteers (Callaghan and Williams, 2014; Cinderby et al., 2014; Parkhill et al., 2015). However, whereas the economic side of the empowering effect of CRE has been researched and substantiated in several studies, the un- countable, qualitative side has hardly been systematically assessed yet. It is not surprising that

countable, qualitative side has hardly been systematically assessed yet. It is not surprising that systematic and comprehensive assessment of the more subtle and qualitative aspects of local impact of CRE is in its in- fancy. To create the desired ‘sensitive and in-depth’ type of assessment, extended project-scale evaluation is required (Walker et al., 2007, p.78), and there are several hurdles to overcome before such an assessment can be done. The boundaries of local impact are unclear and sho

o overcome before such an assessment can be done. The boundaries of local impact are unclear and should be defined (Chadwick, 2002), and there is little conceptual clarity on def- initions of the impacts that are widely recognised within the CRE liter- ature. Also influence of external conditions should be taken into account as local impacts can hardly ever be attributed to one project alone (Ilsekog, 2008). Furthermore, impact pathways are rarely single cause-effect relationships (Ilsekog, 2008

kog, 2008). Furthermore, impact pathways are rarely single cause-effect relationships (Ilsekog, 2008). Finally, there are few exam- ples of holistic social impact assessment in other fields, as impact assessment has always had strong links to policy making and had to satisfy budgetary timescales and show tangible outcomes (Walker et al., 2007). Yet whilst measuring the growth in turnover and other economic analyses (see e.g. Allan, 2012; Allan et al., 2008; Entwistle et al., 2014; Okkonen and Le

economic analyses (see e.g. Allan, 2012; Allan et al., 2008; Entwistle et al., 2014; Okkonen and Lehtonen, 2015) can serve as a crude proxy, it cannot demonstrate the nature and extent of local impacts such as local 2 E n e r g y P o l i c y 138(2020)111193 economic development, social cohesion and capacity building (HIE, 2015b, p.2). cted (Hobson et al., 2016), as it is unlikely that current govern- ment support for CRE will continue just taking the acclaimed social outcomes of CRE as an ‘article of faith’ (Walker et al., 2007, 2007, p.78). Hence, this study aims to contribute methodologically to facilitate the generation of policy relevant evidence regarding the actual

ibute methodologically to facilitate the generation of policy relevant evidence regarding the actual local effects of CRE on their host communities so that government support can be evaluated based on evidence instead of acclaimed outcomes. There- fore, the research question is: How can the qualitative, local impacts of CRE be assessed systematically to provide policymakers evaluating policies supportive of CRE with evidence? To contribute to evaluation tools for the local effects of CRE, this research introduces intrchange mapping, an impact assessment meth- odology from the field of Social Impact Assessment, into the community energy literature. This methodology can be used to make explicit the mental models community members have regarding the changes a CRE project has brought for the community. ommunity. The article is concluded by reflecting on both the local impacts on Shapinsay and the methodology by outlining its potential and limitations for assessing local impacts of CRE projects.
