A need for more comprehensive evaluation frameworks Unlike the factors influencing the uptake and successful imple- mentation of CRE, its outcomes have not received much attention within academic literature (Berka and Creamer, 2018). A recent review on local outcomes of CRE shows that a great many studies merely refer to or loosely observe outcomes, but very few explicitly assess the local im- pacts with formal impact asses

oosely observe outcomes, but very few explicitly assess the local im- pacts with formal impact assessment approaches (Berka and Creamer, 2018). The lack of more comprehensive studies of local impact results in a scattered picture and severely limits the extent to which more detailed, generalisable explanations can be formulated about the circumstances under which different impacts come about. Existing literature is generally positive and uncritical about the real and expected outcomes of CRE. Co

ting literature is generally positive and uncritical about the real and expected outcomes of CRE. Community projects would enable cooperation, communication and commitment such that projects can be developed consensually, technologies can be installed in locally appro- priate ways, and revenues can be allocated to most pressing local needs within the community (Walker and Cass, 2007). As far CRE communities have been reported to be, amongst others, more cohesive; skilled, knowledgeable, and empl

ommunities have been reported to be, amongst others, more cohesive; skilled, knowledgeable, and employable; energy aware, literate and benign; economically and energy independent; politically efficacious; empow- ered, future-proof, and resilient than before engaging with RE (Rogers et al., 2012; Walton, 2012; Hicks and Ison, 2015; Gubbins, 2010; Slee, 2015; NEF, 2012; Seyfang et al., 2013). While some of these claims are well supported, what is exactly is meant by umbrella concepts such as resil

some of these claims are well supported, what is exactly is meant by umbrella concepts such as resilience, empowerment and future-proof, and how these are operationalised and observed often tends to remain unclear. Furthermore, such benefits are mostly derived from interviews with core members of CRE groups, such as project ini- tiators or board members, which compromises the validity and neutrality of these accounts, and results in a lack of insight on how the broader community experiences the

ty of these accounts, and results in a lack of insight on how the broader community experiences the impacts of its CRE project. Only sporadically critical notes are made or negative outcomes re- ported, but CRE can be ‘locally divisive and controversial’ as well (Walker and Devine-Wright, 2008, p. 499). as power lines or highways), and the stakeholder management during the development process (Vanclay et al., 2015; Bere et al., 2015). Local impacts of CRE An assessment of local impacts cannot start with a fixed list of in- dicators and should be adaptable to the specificities of each CRE project. However, it still needs to start from an awareness of the local impacts that can be encountered. To this end an overview is made of possible im

areness of the local impacts that can be encountered. To this end an overview is made of possible impacts of CRE projects gathered from reviews and empirical studies taking stock of CRE’s impacts (Hicks and Ison, 2015; Berka and Creamer, 2018; Gubbins, 2010; Slee, 2015; Walton, 2012; Bere et al., 2015; Brummer, 2018). ase of a wind turbine e.g. potential bird and bat victims; 11. These effects can be induced during the project development phase as well as the operational phase, and can change over time. Impacts can also accumulate and flow on from other impacts, for example local economic effects may improve the attractiveness of a rural area as a result of the jobs and services that are financed by RE revenues and help to reduce outmigration. 4. Assessing

he jobs and services that are financed by RE revenues and help to reduce outmigration. 4. Assessing local impact through mapping theories of change From an awareness of the scope of the potential impacts the assess- ment can start. The essence of impact assessment is to establish the perceived chain of causation from the intervention to the impacts and describe the changes that are induced along the chain. For operation- alising impact, this study applies a definition from the field of Social Im

he chain. For operation- alising impact, this study applies a definition from the field of Social Impact Assessment, which studies “the processes of analysing, moni- toring and managing the intended and unintended social consequences, both positive and negative, of planned interventions […] and any social change processes invoked by those interventions” (Vanclay, 2003, p.1). Here impacts are defined as all issues related to a planned intervention that affect or concern people, directly or indire

ed as all issues related to a planned intervention that affect or concern people, directly or indirectly, perceptual or corporeal (Vanclay et al., 2015). A methodology that can assess such impacts is change mapping. Change mapping can be helpful in understanding dynamics between a project, the host community and contextual factors. As it takes into ac- count both the impacts and the distinctive processes to which these can be attributed, it avoids the frequently occurring magic or black box appr

ocesses to which these can be attributed, it avoids the frequently occurring magic or black box approach to evaluation: measuring the impacts of a project but failing to develop an understanding of the way in which project endogenous and exogenous factors contribute to the identified outcomes (Brousselle and Champagne, 2011; Funnell and Rogers, 2011). Through change map- ping a description can be created of how an intervention, such as a CRE project, contributes to a sequence of impacts (Funnell

created of how an intervention, such as a CRE project, contributes to a sequence of impacts (Funnell and Rogers, 2011; Esteves and Barclay, 2011; Franks et al., 2010). These descriptions are called theories of change, and represent the mental models people have of the change that an intervention causes. They make visible the cascade of cause and effect leading from an intervention to its desired effects. The change mapping method on which the impact assessment draws, creates theories of change t

ffects. The change mapping method on which the impact assessment draws, creates theories of change through the following consecutive steps, adapted from Vogel (2012), to a change mapping approach for CRE projects (see Annex 1 for main adaptations): 1) Create a community profile Establish a comprehensive understanding of the project’s context, including social, economic and environmental conditions. � Geographic location community; � Main natural resources available to community; � Main sources o

. � Geographic location community; � Main natural resources available to community; � Main sources of livelihood; � Community structure and local institutions; � Available services and their accessibility; � Internal and external sources of vulnerability for the community. 2) Make a project profile Map the conditions the project is seeking to influence, and the way in which the desired change is planned to be realised. Be also aware of the conditions the project can unintentionally influence. �

lanned to be realised. enues and allocation; � Internal and external sources of vulnerability for the CRE project. 3) Construct theories of change Create an overview of the activities of the CRE group and the changes to the situation before the RE project became operational. Map com- munity members’ understanding of how these changes may have happened, and how contextual conditions may have affected their occurrence. � Identifying local impacts; � Ranking of outcomes by participants; � Drawing perceived causal models

Identifying local impacts; � Ranking of outcomes by participants; � Drawing perceived causal models of change for the highest ranked impacts. As the change mapping methodology focuses on the impact on the residents of a community, it may be best suited for imapcts that are experienced in the day-to-day community life (from the list in section 3 impact 1–4, 8–10 and 13). cy and mobilisation (11) may be highlighted by the CRE group members, but are likely not experienced by other community members, so extra attention to these should be paid in the synthesis of the findings. The methodology will not be very suitable to assess the impacts energy literacy, energy related behavioural change, local support for RE (5–7). Creating evidence of these impacts requires dedicated surveys instead of a qualitative, experiantial account, and preferably also a before and after m

ted surveys instead of a qualitative, experiantial account, and preferably also a before and after measure- ment to track change reliably. Finally, many impacts, but especially demographic change (12), are caused by complex multi-factorial pro- cesses, and the CRE project may only be part of the explanation for a change in the community. Overdetermism should be avoided by remaining senstive to how project external conditions influence the impact, because a CRE project is only one of many develop

project external conditions influence the impact, because a CRE project is only one of many developments in a community. 5. Case study methodology The change mapping methodology is explored through a case study (Yin, 2013). The case study on which the empirical work draws is the Scottish island Shapinsay’s 900 kW wind project. Selection of Sha- pinsay’s turbine scheme has resulted from narrowing down from CRE projects included in a database mapping Scottish and international CRE (Energy archipe

down from CRE projects included in a database mapping Scottish and international CRE (Energy archipelago, 2015). To begin with, a wind energy installation was searched for. This technology was expected to deliver tangible so- cial outcomes, because of the high visibility of the turbine within the community, the high profitability of wind energy, and the great organ- isational capacity completing a wind project is considered to require. Furthermore, a high degree of community-ownership, a middle-

wind project is considered to require. Furthermore, a high degree of community-ownership, a middle-sized generative capacity (around 1 MW), several years of operation, and an active community organisation pursuing a variety of community development goals, are used as selection criteria to find a matured project most likely to display a considerable and diverse impact on the community it is part of. f. These selection criteria and the fact that Sha- pinsay is a small and well-delineated island community increase the visibility of local impacts. Step 1 community profile To create a thorough understanding of the context in which the Shapinsay CRE project is embedded, this section will shortly introduce the context in which the project has been taken up by the SDT. Shapinsay is one of the Orkney Islands off the north coast of main- land Scotland, characterized by rich nature and raw, open landscap

ands off the north coast of main- land Scotland, characterized by rich nature and raw, open landscapes. Its economy is primarily based on agriculture with the exception of a few small businesses that are largely tourism-related. The island has a small but vibrant community of 300 people, who live spread out over the island at farms, detached houses, and in the small village, Balfour. The village consists of around 50 households, the elementary school, the community centre, the SDT office, the ch

onsists of around 50 households, the elementary school, the community centre, the SDT office, the church, a caf�e, a small supermarket and a ferry terminal. From the village ferry terminal, the MS Shapinsay sails six times a day back and forth between Shapinsay and Mainland Orkney. Many of the population in the working age have jobs on Mainland Orkney and use the ferry service for commuting. Last decades Shapinsay has seen quite a significant change in the balance of its population. On the one h

decades Shapinsay has seen quite a significant change in the balance of its population. On the one hand, the sustainability of its community is threatened because many young people leave the island to follow higher education courses and pursue job opportunities outwit Shapinsay. With the industrialisation of agriculture farms scaled up and jobs in agriculture decreased, but limited other employment opportu- nities have replaced those jobs. On the other hand, Shapinsay has also seen a considerabl

t opportu- nities have replaced those jobs. On the other hand, Shapinsay has also seen a considerable influx of people from elsewhere in the UK that are attracted to island life and the natural beauty of the place. This 5 E n e r g y P o l i c y 138(2020)111193 gathering process. immigration has reached an extent that the people who are born and brought up on Shapinsay are almost a minority. The new residents are mostly people over 40. Resultantly, the population has a lack of youngsters and a s

ew residents are mostly people over 40. Resultantly, the population has a lack of youngsters and a surplus of elderly. Last decade also the rise of renewables has brought major change to Shapinsay. Shapinsay and the other Orkney islands have become a frontrunner in the implementation of renewable energy. The region is blessed with some of the strongest wind, wave and tidal resources in Europe (OREF, 2015), and is already generating more RE than is required for own consumption. Besides housing th

F, 2015), and is already generating more RE than is required for own consumption. Besides housing the European Marine Energy Centre (EMEC), where seagoing technologies are developed, the county is at present home to over 500 domestic turbines, as well as several larger scale wind farms and 8 community-owned turbines (OREF, 2015; Energy archipelago, 2015). Furthermore, with 1 in 12 Orcadian households generating electricity from renewable sources, Orkney has the UK’s highest share of households p

lds generating electricity from renewable sources, Orkney has the UK’s highest share of households producing their own electricity (OREF, 2015). However, as a result of the high uptake of wind power in Orkney, the limits of the capacity of its grid have been reached, leading to some turbines being switched off on windy days through real-time Active Network Management. This means that Orcadian wind energy schemes cannot perform to their full potential despite the high wind potential. 6.2. Step 2

Step 2 Project profile This section discusses the conditions the project is seeking to influ- ence, and the way in which the desired change is planned to be realised. The plan to develop a community turbine scheme arose from a lack of funds to address local challenges and ambitions. By the nature of being a small island community, transport has always been a main bottleneck for development of the island.

a small island community, transport has always been a main bottleneck for development of the island. Ferry transport creates extra expenses and is bound to a timetable. As meeting demand in the early morning and in the evening is not profitable, the regular ferry mainly covers standard office hours. the high costs of the on-demand charter. Besides reducing flexibility, the required extra link makes island life more expensive. not allow for creating economies of scale. Not only households and businesses, but also local governments are confronted with the combination of a small user group and higher provision costs. and a balanced demographic, in 2002 local residents founded the Shapinsay Development Group at the insti- gation of the Orkney Islands Council. This group later evolved to the Shapinsay Development Trust, and aims to be a vehicle through which the islanders can collectively help to maintain and improve their lives on Shapinsay through development of extra facilities for social welfare, recreation, and other leisure time occupation; enhancement of educa- tion opportunities; protection and preser

, and other leisure time occupation; enhancement of educa- tion opportunities; protection and preservation of the environment; re- lief of poverty; assistance in the provision of housing; and stimulation of a positive business climate for trade and industry. e of the com- munity, the relative isolation has been identified as one of the main threats to sustaining the population of Shapinsay. Shapinsay. A private charter was available to residents. However, to make the service viable for the provider, this ferry had to be paid per crossing, not per user. . Especially, the Out-of- Hours (OOH) boat service is highly valued by the community mem- bers. The OOH was by far identified as the main impact of the project. ould be taken away. Modelling data and econometric methodology 3.1. Theory of the quantile regression model This study used a panel regression model to examine the impact of climate change mitigation on economic, environmental, and social development in Europe using data from 1981 to 2018. Panel quantile regression models are widely used economic a

in Europe using data from 1981 to 2018. Panel quantile regression models are widely used economic approaches in theoretical papers) (Canay, 2011; Khan et al., 2020; Lamarche, 2010; Wang et al., 2019). Quantile regression is an alternative to linear regression models. Standard linear regression only estimates the conditional mean of the dependent variable; on the other hand, the quantile regression model estimates the dependent variable’s quantile (conditional median distri- bution) and accounts

n model estimates the dependent variable’s quantile (conditional median distri- bution) and accounts for unobserved heterogeneity and heterogeneous covariate effects in panel data. The method was proposed by Koenker and Bassett (1978) to overcome the shortcomings of the ordinary least squares. Assuming that most studies employ ordinary least squares to investigate economic and environmental phenomena (Xu et al., 2017), 5 E n e r g y P o l i c y 178(2023)113589 the traditional approach is only he

ena (Xu et al., 2017), 5 E n e r g y P o l i c y 178(2023)113589 the traditional approach is only helpful in revealing the average impact (mean value) as a conditional expectation on economic and environ- mental parameters by influencing factors. OLS failed to provide a complete picture of the conditional distribution (Xu and Lin, 2018). Quantile regression is suitable for study when parameters impact varies across conditional quantiles and determiners the regression coefficient based on quantil

pact varies across conditional quantiles and determiners the regression coefficient based on quantiles (Arshad et al., 2018). In traditional linear regression models, the error term is considered the independent values of the variables, and the variances are homo- geneous across data, but in quantile regression models, the error term varies, and there is not set assumption about the variance structure. Due to this, panel quantile regression offers a stable estimate, and the results are more effi

ture. Due to this, panel quantile regression offers a stable estimate, and the results are more efficient in calculating the impact than traditional linear regression models and provide optimal estimates on the conditional estimates, which provide the opportunity to see the diversity of the conditional variability (Koenker, 2004; Nzama et al., 2023). Econometric methodology In order to obtain optimal unbiased results, appropriate methodol- ogies must be applied to the data. Kapetanios et al. (2011) argued that panel data often contain CSD (cross-sectional dependence) issues, which result in misleading estimates by distorting the data. Introduction and motivation The coming decades are set to see a tremendous growth in de- ployments of renewable energy (RE) technologies globally. These will be driven by at least three forces. Firstly, pursuit of United Nations Sus- tainable Development Goal 7 demands that “affordable, reliable, sus- tainable and modern energy”

us- tainable Development Goal 7 demands that “affordable, reliable, sus- tainable and modern energy” [1] be expanded to be available to the 940 million people currently living without it [2]. Secondly, addressing climate change requires the complete displacement of current fossil fuelled electricity generators with zero emissions generators, the vast majority of which will likely be renewables (with the remainder being nuclear). Thirdly, another consequence of mitigating climate change will see

th the remainder being nuclear). Thirdly, another consequence of mitigating climate change will see demand for electricity increase substantially as fossil fuel powered appliances, including vehicles, are replaced by more efficient electric appliances that can run on zero emissions electricity. * Corresponding author. ** Corresponding author. This rise in RE deployments, and the corresponding decline of fossil fuel activities, will have global environmental [3,4], economic [5,6] and geopolitical consequences [7–10]. It will also have profound local im- pacts. These include local social, economic and environmental impacts on the communities situated close to deployments and which they serve. This review synthesises the global studies from the past decade on the impacts of RE deployments aroun

is review synthesises the global studies from the past decade on the impacts of RE deployments around the world in geographical loca- tion and type of economies. Unlike other review studies [11–14], this work does not focus on a specific type of RE, nor specific regions nor disciplines, but rather focuses on multiple RE technologies, across a range of geographies and disciplines. of) the diversity of impacts and thereby provides relevant information for RE de- velopments in many distinct local contexts and reveals areas of ambi- guity and contradiction in the studies as well as research gaps. Methodology This section describes the research questions included and excluded from investigation through the review and the methodology by which the literature review was performed. 2.1. Research questions of the literature review The research que

e literature review was performed. 2.1. Research questions of the literature review The research questions addressed in this review are: • RQ1: How do RE deployments impact, and how are they impacted by, local social conditions? • RQ2: How do RE deployments impact, and how are they impacted by, local economic conditions? • RQ3: How do RE deployments impact the local environment? These questions define the limits of the study. Some of the exclusions from the study are impacts upstream or downstre

fine the limits of the study. Some of the exclusions from the study are impacts upstream or downstream from RE de- ployments; impacts on electricity systems such as the degree of decen- tralisation, network loads, and the need for energy storage; the efficacy of energy market structures or policies; the history and politics of energy transition. Another limitation of the study comes from what RE technologies are included. The review considers major RE technologies: solar photovol- taic (PV), ons

echnologies are included. The review considers major RE technologies: solar photovol- taic (PV), onshore and offshore wind farms, wave energy converters (WECs), tidal turbines, floating solar photovoltaic (FPV), hydro energy and bioenergy. Geothermal technologies were not considered because geothermal energy is only available in limited locations. Some recent studies that tackled the social, economic and environmental impacts of geothermal energy include [34–36]. Nuclear energy and hydrogen were

mic and environmental impacts of geothermal energy include [34–36]. Nuclear energy and hydrogen were not considered because nuclear is not a renewable energy source and hydrogen is not a technology for generating energy but rather may play a role as a medium for energy transport or storage. With the urgent need to decarbonise and find alternative fuel solutions, focus on hydrogen has been growing quickly and recent studies covering the social, economic and environmental impacts include [37–40].

quickly and recent studies covering the social, economic and environmental impacts include [37–40]. In addition, this review only covers RE generators. Energy storage systems such as batteries, thermal energy storage, compressed-air storage and flywheels, were all excluded. The reader is pointed to recent review studies covering im- pacts of energy storage systems, including [41–43]. 2.2. trategy and selection criteria A systematic literature review was performed to identify and analyse published studies related to the research questions. This consisted of three-steps: (1) identification, (2) screening, and (3) eligibility. In the identification step, peer-reviewed studies were retrieved from the Scopus database using specific keywords. This process was con- ducted between 04 October and 04 November 2022. er and 04 November 2022. The search was restricted to journal articles published from 2010 to 2022 so as to focus on developments from the past decade. Literature review This section covers the literature review and is organised in three subsections namely 1) social impacts of, and on, renewable energy de- ployments, 2) economic impacts of, and on, renewable energy de- ploym

, and on, renewable energy de- ployments, 2) economic impacts of, and on, renewable energy de- ployments, and 3) environmental impacts of renewable energy deployments. 3.1. Social impacts of, and on, renewable energy deployments This section examines the relationship between RE technologies and the societies within which they are deployed. This includes how RE deployments impact societies and things that societies value, as well as the characteristics of societies that may influence their positi

gs that societies value, as well as the characteristics of societies that may influence their position towards RE deployments. The analysis begins with broad issues of demographics, environ- mental concerns, impact on landscape and place attachment. The study then reviews procedural aspects of building and maintaining trust, community engagement, community ownership and benefits. Finally, the section concludes with insights into more granular effects of the type of RE technology, proximity of RE

ion concludes with insights into more granular effects of the type of RE technology, proximity of RE deployments, and knowledge of, and past exposure to, RE technologies. 3.1.1. Demographics Numerous studies have examined the influences that gender, age, education and employment status may play in the social acceptance of RE. A comparison of these studies shows significant variation across contexts, including many contradictory findings. For instance, a survey in Croatia showed that female parti

s, including many contradictory findings. For instance, a survey in Croatia showed that female participants exhibited higher willingness to pay for RE compared to their male counterparts [98], while a survey in Ghana revealed that male participants were more likely to accept RE than their female counterparts [254]. One trend that appears to hold broadly across contexts is that younger people are more willing to pay for RE (including in Japan [236] and China [283]) and are generally more likely t

ore willing to pay for RE (including in Japan [236] and China [283]) and are generally more likely to feel optimistic about the positive impacts of RE and to support RE (including in the United Kingdom [99], United States of America [44], and Greece [100]). Higher education levels are also broadly found to correspond with a higher awareness of, and positive disposition to, RE technologies [101, 102]. 2]. This review reveals an underrepresentation of First Nation’s per- spectives and engagement in the literature. Environmental concerns Sensitivity towards environmental concerns, and climate change in particular, is a major driver for support for RE. This has been found consistently in studies around the world, including in the Shandong Province of China, where local residents indicated that their acceptance of RE stemmed from their environmental concerns [284]; in Greece, where environmental protection was the most

med from their environmental concerns [284]; in Greece, where environmental protection was the most important reason for RE deployment, followed by reduction in oil dependency [103]; in 4 R e n e w a b l e a n d S u s t a i n a b l e E n e r g y R e v i e w s 2 07 (2025) 114956 Romania, where serious concerns about climate change correlated with strong support for RE technologies and a preoccupation with energy saving [104]; in the Czech Republic, where concern for the environment correlated wit

on with energy saving [104]; in the Czech Republic, where concern for the environment correlated with support for RE and worries about the use of nuclear energy [105]; in India, where a survey revealed that participants who were mostly in favour of RE were also aware of the environmental problems of fossil fuels [285]. However, in some cases, for countries with low levels of carbon emissions, RE consumption is seen more as a way to improve energy access, rather than a motivation to mitigate clim

onsumption is seen more as a way to improve energy access, rather than a motivation to mitigate climate change [274]. Furthermore, Karytsas et al. showed that people who have better environmental behaviours (recycling, domestic energy saving, habits during transportation, participation in environmental organizations) are more likely to know about RE [106]. In Japan, a survey study showed that installation of residential PV systems affects people’s concerns for the environment and leads to an inc

allation of residential PV systems affects people’s concerns for the environment and leads to an increase in environmental behaviour [237]. Incorporating biodiversity benefits into the design of RE in- frastructures could also help bolster public support, as suggested by Klain et al. where their study found that residents were more willing to pay for offshore wind farms (OWFs) that offer high quality artificial reef habitat [351]. ity artificial reef habitat [351]. Community engagement The communities surrounding RE projects have the potential to be powerful project supporters or to be persistent hinderers [127]. The consistent finding across studies is that early inclusion in decision-making processes is vital to engaging communities and informing their perspective on project risks and benefits [128,129,354]. Where this is done well and early, “public participati

on project risks and benefits [128,129,354]. Where this is not done early and well, for example in Devine-Wright’s study of tidal energy in the UK, communities can become cynical about consultation procedures leading to low levels of engagement and feelings of being ignored throughout the project [130]. Summary and critical analysis of social aspects In terms of demographics, this review found broad agreement across countries that younger people are more willing to pay for RE. Higher education levels, better knowledge about RE technologies and their benefits, awareness about climate change and the need to reduce reli- ance on fo

hnologies and their benefits, awareness about climate change and the need to reduce reli- ance on fossil fuels, were also found to correlate with higher social acceptance of RE. Trust was a recurrent theme in the review with numerous studies identifying trust – in promoters, developers and local authorities – as essential for successful deployment of RE with a com- munity. hese are arranged in terms of RE specific costs and cost mitigation options, the relative effects of fossil fuel subsidies, and the macro influences of institutional governance and economic growth. 3.2.1. Cost of renewable energy technologies The cost of deploying RE technologies has historically been signifi- cantly greater than the cost of deploying fossil fuel technologies, and far greater still than running existing fossil fuel plants. For instance, a case study in Nigeria found that due to

l than running existing fossil fuel plants. For instance, a case study in Nigeria found that due to the limited cost reduction that RE systems have experienced in Sub-Saharan Africa, conventional energy generation systems are preferred over decentralised RE systems [280]. Cost is one of, or arguably the biggest barrier to RE adoption. Still, the last four decades have seen dramatic reductions in the costs for RE de- ployments, including the technologies themselves – particularly for wind and sol

he costs for RE de- ployments, including the technologies themselves – particularly for wind and solar – and the cost of project financing. In many regions RE is now the cheapest form of new build generation, and in certain regions such as the Group of Twenty countries (G20), the new build cost of RE is lower than running costs of existing fossil and nuclear power plants [358]. However, for continued progress and decrease in cost of RE technologies, continued public policy support is needed [277

ued progress and decrease in cost of RE technologies, continued public policy support is needed [277]. Regarding technology costs, the most remarkable changes have occurred for solar PV [231] and wind [334]. In contrast, wave and tidal en

t remarkable changes have occurred for solar PV [231] and wind [334]. In contrast, wave and tidal energy technologies have remained less mature and uncompetitive on cost [359]. The cost of hydropower meanwhile has been competitive for decades, in well-endowed locations such as Canada, despite not expe- riencing substantive cost reductions [58]. The reduction in capital costs, combined with the characteristic zero fuel costs of RE generation technologies, places a great emphasis on upfront projec

racteristic zero fuel costs of RE generation technologies, places a great emphasis on upfront project development costs and the cost of capital. Economic advantages of co-location The ability to co-locate with other economic activities is a competi- tive advantage for RE technologies because co-location is generally not attractive for fossil fuel extraction or combustion facilities due to their pollution. Interestingly, existing fossil fuel infrastructures, such as decommissioned oil and gas platforms, are prime candidates for RE deployments [1

structures, such as decommissioned oil and gas platforms, are prime candidates for RE deployments [155]. For landowners, co-location can deliver substantial revenue. Loomis showed that in Illinois United States of America, landowners who leased their land to wind farm developers earned $13 million annually in extra income [59]. However, co-location can also be the source of tensions between economic growth, indigenous rights at sea, historic injustices and access to natural coastal resources [60

ic growth, indigenous rights at sea, historic injustices and access to natural coastal resources [60]. Therefore, early, compre- hensive, genuine consultation and engagement are pre-requisites for the successful implementation and co-location of marine and land RE pro- jects [60]. In this review, co-location featured particularly prominently in ma- rine settings. This appears to be due to logistical costs and geopolitical dimensions [61] of building and servicing infrastructure in these con- tex

al costs and geopolitical dimensions [61] of building and servicing infrastructure in these con- texts and due to “environmental and economic synergies generated by the joint deployment of offshore renewable facilities and activities such as fishing, aquaculture and even the conservation of natural areas” [156]. Offshore wind turbines have, for instance, long been co-located in the North Sea with other marine activities such as fisheries, which in- creases the utilization of limited maritime spa

other marine activities such as fisheries, which in- creases the utilization of limited maritime space [157]. Co-location can also “reduce conflicts” [156], including between nations, such as through “Maritime Spatial Planning [which] can balance maritime ac- tivities and foster cross border cooperation while developing a new scheme of multilevel governance” [158]. 3.2.3. Terrestrial habitat alteration RE deployments in terrestrial settings tend to garner particular attention as they are more visible than deployments offshore or in rivers. Introduction Energy transitions are tightly coupled with responses to the global climate crisis [1,2], but energy transitions in many rural and remote regions are about meeting basic human needs and supporting social and economic li

y rural and remote regions are about meeting basic human needs and supporting social and economic livelihoods [3]. Around 10 % of the worlds’ population still lacks access to electricity, and over 3-billion people still depend on unclean sources of energy for heating and cooking [4]. Many rural and remote communities, including many Indigenous communities across Canada, do not have access to clean and reliable energy and depend on aging energy infrastructure or off-grid diesel generators [5–7].

and reliable energy and depend on aging energy infrastructure or off-grid diesel generators [5–7]. These communities experience energy injustices, including high home energy bills, vulnerable energy infrastructure, and constraints to affordable energy supply for investing in healthcare, youth education, and com- munity economic development opportunities [8–10]. Community renewable energy (CRE) projects [17] are often seen as a solution to energy insecurity in rural and remote regions [18–21]. C

ojects [17] are often seen as a solution to energy insecurity in rural and remote regions [18–21]. CRE can provide accessible, affordable, and sustainable energy, thus * Corresponding author. Energy citizenship There is a plethora of definitions of energy citizenship, from the role of individuals in acting responsibly about energy consumption [8,12], to the engagement of individuals in energy arenas through democratic and civil opportunities at local to t

engagement of individuals in energy arenas through democratic and civil opportunities at local to the supra-national scales [4,5]. Debour- deau et al. [11] explore the diverse use and interpretation of energy citizenship in recent scholarship, and posit that energy citizenship is more appropriately conceptualized as a multi-dimensional construct, encompassing both agency attributes and outcome orientations. Agency attributes refer to whom energy citizenship is ascribed, whether in- dividuals or

ientations. Agency attributes refer to whom energy citizenship is ascribed, whether in- dividuals or collectives, and thus hinges in part on opportunities or pathways for engagement, the knowledge and motivation to engage, and the capacity to engage [12]. Outcome orientations refer to how energy futures are imagined, and the reformative or transformative change that is desired by energy citizens [11], such as the perceived benefits that may accrue to local communities, economies, or wellbeing. B

11], such as the perceived benefits that may accrue to local communities, economies, or wellbeing. Both agency and outcome dimensions are important to exploring energy citizenship and to the pursuit of energy transitions, policies, or technologies that are considered appropriate or legitimate [6,7]. However, despite extant literature on energy citizenship, including the growing scholarship on the engagement of historically poor and marginalized populations [21], the socio-economic positioning of

engagement of historically poor and marginalized populations [21], the socio-economic positioning of energy citizens is poorly understood [13] and there remains a need for critical discussion as to “who counts as a citizen” [22], especially among youth, and what is to be achieved via energy citizenship. 1.2. [46]. The population of Indigenous youth in Canada is growing significantly faster than non-Indigenous youth [95]. Research question Failure to address the above challenges risks youth being left behind in energy transitions in rural and remote communities, in comparison to youth in more privileged urban and non-Indigenous settings. Jaradat et al. [39] reviewed th

rison to youth in more privileged urban and non-Indigenous settings. Jaradat et al. [39] reviewed the state of international scholarship on youth in energy transitions research and conclude that whilst youth participa- tion, as energy citizens, in energy transitions has been addressed, the focus is largely on youth energy education [54–57], conservation behaviour [58–60], energy awareness [61–63], or climate advocacy [64,65]. Moreover, notwithstanding the recognized importance of youth engagemen

or climate advocacy [64,65]. Moreover, notwithstanding the recognized importance of youth engagement to sustainable community and energy futures [38,66,67], there has been relatively limited attention among energy transitions scholars to whether and how Indigenous youth help shape CRE futures and imaginaries at the local scale [39]. The purpose of this paper is to examine the nature and role of youth energy citizenship in CRE in northern Indigenous communities. Specif- ically, this research ask

outh energy citizenship in CRE in northern Indigenous communities. Specif- ically, this research asks: how are Indigenous youth shaping their community’s energy future(s), and what barriers and opportunities exist to youth engagement in CRE? This is done based on learning from the experiences of Indigenous communities in northern Saskatchewan and Alberta, Canada. The lessons emerging are of broader value for the en- ergy scholarly community and those engaged in energy planning in other regions.

r value for the en- ergy scholarly community and those engaged in energy planning in other regions. Specifically, our research makes an important contribution to cur- rent scholarship on youth energy citizenship, responding to calls for contextual and empirically based research that seeks to understand whether and how Indigenous youth shape community energy futures [39]. er of youth as catalysts for energy transition and agents of change [66,96], suggesting that Indigenous youth should be equipped to lead CRE innovation, leveraging their “energy, talent and, eventually, expertise” to address the energy and broader social challenges that confront Indigenous communities [97]. outh active engagement and their potential to shape community energy futures. Methods Interviews are commonly used by researchers when exploring social aspects of energy transitions [13,16,48,80], and in other contexts when attempting to reveal local, lived experiences, values, beliefs and moti- vations [81]. values, beliefs and moti- vations [81]. Limitations and potential biases There are limitations to the research, namely those related to the challenges of engaging youth in northern communities and social boundaries. First, the interviewer is non-Indigenous, not from the North, and not from Canada. This may have caused some participants to be reluctant to engage, but project partners and local community energy coordinators he

ticipants to be reluctant to engage, but project partners and local community energy coordinators helped facilitate introduction to the communities. Relying on community energy coordinators via local relationships influenced who participated in the research. Such community relationships are core to Indigenous epistemology serve to strengthen the credibility of the research design and its findings [98]. Whilst this may be seen through western science lenses as introducing potential bias, our appr

[98]. Whilst this may be seen through western science lenses as introducing potential bias, our approach pro- vided a community-appropriate pathway to engage youth and commu- nity elders and to interact with knowledge holders in a way that may not have been possible with more rigid sampling designs. That said, we are cautious to not claim that the participants engaged in our study are representative of their communities. Second, the intent of this research was to focus solely on youth participan

ve of their communities. Second, the intent of this research was to focus solely on youth participants. We focused on youth above 18 yrs. of age, owing to the scope of our research ethics approvals and as discussed with community leadership. It proved challenging to identify a large sample of youth participants, even with the help of community members. The subsequent engagement of non-youth provided important perspectives to some of the challenges to youth engagement, especially perspectives fro

ed important perspectives to some of the challenges to youth engagement, especially perspectives from community elders and from those involved in youth programs or high schools. Third, the communities involved in this research are still positioned at the very early, pre-planning stages of renewable energy initiatives. Thus, there are no practical experiences for youth in the field of energy transition or opportunities to reflect on past engagement. This is the case for most northern and remote I

tion or opportunities to reflect on past engagement. This is the case for most northern and remote Indigenous 5 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 130 (2025) 104417 communities across Canada. Results Results indicate many positive attributes for youth energy citizen- ship the study communities, due in part to the ambitions of these com- munities to overcome enduring socioeconomic hardships and the ambitions of youth not just to survive but to thrive. However, findings also indicate several obstacles to youth engagement, many of which are deeply embedded in the underlying community and social challenges and relationships. Four emergent the

eply embedded in the underlying community and social challenges and relationships. Four emergent themes resulted from our interviews that characterize the perspectives of PBCN and Frog Lake community members about youth engagement, namely: (i) Indigenous values and youth’s perspectives on what is considered community-appropriate en- ergy; (ii) the value-added that CRE projects are expected to offer com- munities; (iii) the extent to which youth are engaged in their community; and (iv) the underl

r com- munities; (iii) the extent to which youth are engaged in their community; and (iv) the underlying challenges to youth engagement in their community and in CRE transition planning. Given the current state of CRE in these communities, the conversations often focused on intent or ambitions, and youth engagement in community at large. Participants provided deep insights to factors and conditions regarding youth as active energy citizens that might influence the future of energy in these commu

s regarding youth as active energy citizens that might influence the future of energy in these communities and other developmental endeavours. 3.1. Indigenous values and youth’s perspectives on community- appropriate energy Citizenship shapes and is shaped by values of the community. What is valued by any particular community has a profound impact on policy choices and how these may or may not reinforce cultural norms. A dominant theme emerging from the interviews was the importance of Indigenou

nforce cultural norms. A dominant theme emerging from the interviews was the importance of Indigenous values and how this underscores community way of life – not only energy security. Participants explained that land and nature, the source of living and thriving, are embraced by Indigenous people; every aspect of life, including energy, must be extracted from the land. This perspective was also evident during interviews with some community youth, as one youth participant described: “everything w

ident during interviews with some community youth, as one youth participant described: “everything we do in our culture is all from the land; everything we use…when we use our medicines…when we go and get our food, it’s in the bush”. Participants also expressed their relationship with land and their responsibility to preserve it for future generations by balanced and clean consumption – including energy generation and use. A community leader elaborated: “we were put here to preserve the land…. w

energy generation and use. ted in self-sufficiency and ensures the preservation of land for future gen- erations, such as solar, wind, and biomass. to be self-sustaining and self- efficient with our land.” Youth are increasingly aware of the adverse impacts of fossil fuels for energy production, whether from oil sands in Alberta or coal-fired electricity generation in Saskatchewan. Value-added expected from energy projects Energy citizenship includes reflection and community discourse on how energy projects can enhance well-being of the community. Partic- ipants identified several social values and opportunities anticipated from the introduction of renewable energy projects in their commu- nities. Economic revenues emerg

d from the introduction of renewable energy projects in their commu- nities. Economic revenues emerged as one of the most tangible values and benefits, which was identified as especially important given the current burden of high energy costs in communities alongside other underlying economic struggles such as youth unemployment. Partici- pants especially emphasized the importance of not only having renew- able energy projects in their community but also owning those energy projects. For example

ng renew- able energy projects in their community but also owning those energy projects. The participant went on to explain that owning CRE projects would mean that the community “can actually generate revenue ‘cause we’d be paying towards the band [Band Council] instead of an exte

n actually generate revenue ‘cause we’d be paying towards the band [Band Council] instead of an external company.” In doing so, this was seen as an opportunity to move toward energy self-sufficiency and to generate economic returns to reinvest in the community and to further advance local energy projects. Underlying conditions as barriers to youth engagement Building on conversations about how youth as energy citizens are engaged in their community, three underlying conditions emerged from the interview results that reveal barriers to youth agency and, thus, may help explain the constraints to youth as local agents of change in com- munity energy transitions. 3.4.

p explain the constraints to youth as local agents of change in com- munity energy transitions. 3.4.1. Limited community infrastructure to mobilize youth Full energy citizenship requires the opportunity to participate, as well as efficacy of that participation. Results indicate limited social and community infrastructure to engage youth, to foster a sense of com- munity, and thus to motivate youth participation in community initia- tives. Through our conversations, it was clear that in some comm

participation in community initia- tives. Through our conversations, it was clear that in some communities the high cost of operating community infrastructure is seen as an obstacle to youth engagement; often, communities can barely afford to meet essential infrastructure needs. resources available to invest in youth, via youth centers, extra- curricular activities, and recreational facilities. es like discussions about community energy futures, youth need to be active and inspired instead of “staying home…couch surfing.” This requires that communities offer the places and means for youth to gather and engage with community; something that one com- munity member said is “almost impossible unless you have other [external] organizations pitching in to help.” There is external government support for youth programs, but the focus is on the more critical needs related to health and social s

pport for youth programs, but the focus is on the more critical needs related to health and social services instead of other forms of community infra- structure for youth. 3.4.2. the interest of the community’s future, especially in energy and engineering. of motivation among many youths, owing to a sense “of hopelessness…like there’s no future in any- thing.” It’s hard for youth to engage and be motivated to change their community energy trajectory, another participant explained, when there is limited social interaction and “they don’t have the confidence in themselves.” 4. Discussion This research set out based on the premise that youth energy citi- zenship is an important catalyst for successful sustainability transitions in general and a cruci

i- zenship is an important catalyst for successful sustainability transitions in general and a crucial factor for CRE in particular. This premise is based on assertions present in global societal discourses via many intergovernmental platforms, such as the United Nations and IRENA, and on scholarly literature focusing on the role of youth in the global climate movement [38,87,88]. Yet, at the same time, our un- derstandings of youth energy citizenship at the local level remains limited [39,66,89

time, our un- derstandings of youth energy citizenship at the local level remains limited [39,66,89]. Notwithstanding the importance of youth in energy transition and the increased engagement of youth in the national to international energy space, lessons from our research suggest a very different story in the context of northern Indigenous communities. Although there is strong youth interest in energy transitions and CRE in our study communities, there are significant barriers to youth as ener

energy transitions and CRE in our study communities, there are significant barriers to youth as energy citizens. Research focused on Indigenous CRE often emphasizes the legacy impacts of colonization and marginalization on local energy security, engagement, and future opportunities [31,36]. Our findings similarly reflect that some of the barriers faced by Indigenous youth as energy citizens are rooted in larger socio-cultural challenges and contexts, including the historical marginalization of I

oted in larger socio-cultural challenges and contexts, including the historical marginalization of Indigenous communities in general. However, in other instances our results reflect a misalignment between Indigenous youth values and their immediate expectations about CRE as a means for local social and economic opportunity versus the longer-term climate goals that are often espoused in recent youth energy scholarship [39]. Without appropriate pathways for Indigenous youth to participate and have

h energy scholarship [39]. Without appropriate pathways for Indigenous youth to participate and have efficacy in the energy futures of their 7 A. Jaradat et al. communities, it will likely be more difficult to ensure that CRE and energy transitions realize their fullest potential to align with community values and achieve full value-added girded by legitimacy of active participation of the entire body politic, including youth energy citizens. Introduction Transitioning from centralized, fossil fuel-based energy systems to- ward renewable and decentralized energy is recognized globally as essential to mitigating climate change and providing affordable, reli- able, and accessible energy. Energy systems are often framed as socio- technical systems, whereby technolog

nd accessible energy. Energy systems are often framed as socio- technical systems, whereby technology is a social phenomenon embedded in infrastructures, shared by human actors, and used among social networks [1]. Transitions in energy systems involve multi- dimensional shifts from one mode of production and consumption to another [2] and are accompanied by changes in technologies, institu- tional arrangements, and social networks as new actors and new roles are created [1,3,4]. The success of e

arrangements, and social networks as new actors and new roles are created [1,3,4]. The success of energy transitions thus hinges in large part on the engagement and support of a diversity of energy actors, from producers and consumers to innovators and policymakers, over an extended period of time [2,5]. Energy citizenship can be a critical driver and sustainer of energy transitions [6,7]. Energy citizenship emphasizes individuals as agents of change by acting responsibly toward energy consumpt

However, notwithstanding advances among the international policy community on youth engage- ment, coupled with literature arguing for the need to engage youth in energy and climate action, there remains limited understanding of how youth are framed in current energy transitions scholarship. Scholars have discussed the importance of youth in the implementation of SDGs [30,31], especially education and employment [32,33]

portance of youth in the implementation of SDGs [30,31], especially education and employment [32,33] and also climate action [34–36], but we are not aware of any research that specifically explores whether and how youth are framed as energy citizens in global energy transition. This paper responds to this gap in energy scholarship and examines how the literature conceptualizes the role of youth in energy transitions. Specifically, we are interested in whether energy scholarship ap- proaches yout

energy transitions. Specifically, we are interested in whether energy scholarship ap- proaches youth as influential energy citizens and drivers of energy innovation, or whether youth are framed only as passive actors. In the sections that follow our approach for examining the energy literature is presented first, followed by a thematic analysis of energy research that focuses on or engages youth. Several observations are then presented concerning the state of scholarship, followed by recommenda

Several observations are then presented concerning the state of scholarship, followed by recommendations for research on youth energy citizenship in energy transitions. 2. The role of youth in the energy sector Scholarship addressing youth in the energy sector is not new or unique to renewable energy. In the 1990s, for example, Seydlitz et al. [130] explored the implications of the offshore oil industry on com- munity wellness, including youth, while Seyfrit and Hamilton [131] examined young pe

ndustry on com- munity wellness, including youth, while Seyfrit and Hamilton [131] examined young people's out-migration intentions from North Sea oil- dependent Scottish island communities. More recent scholarship has explored youth identities in oil-dependent communities in Canada [132], the expectations among youth in developing countries for new- found employment associated with the prospects of oil development [133], and even the marginalization of youth from participating in the workforce

of oil development [133], and even the marginalization of youth from participating in the workforce in emerging fossil fuel economies [134]. The emphasis of such scholarship is often on the disproportionate vulnerability of, and adverse impacts on youth in fossil fuel-dependent regions [135], coupled with opportunities for youth engagement in energy sector employment [136], rather than the role and contribution of youth to energy discourse and energy futures per se [137]. Energy systems globally

d contribution of youth to energy discourse and energy futures per se [137]. Energy systems globally are transitioning, from centralized and vertically integrated systems reliant on oil, natural gas, and coal, toward more distributed and local energy systems powered by renewables [20,126]. Associated with this transition is a new role for civil society, including youth, in shaping an inclusive and just energy transition and ensuring socially acceptable and beneficial energy futures [7,138,139].

just energy transition and ensuring socially acceptable and beneficial energy futures [7,138,139]. In the context of rural and remote communities, for example, McMaster et al. [25,56] argue that youth must be approached as critical drivers of energy innovation, as their engagement is foundational to the long-term success of any socio-technical transition. Although youth have often been excluded from energy decision making [137,139], they are now claiming “their power as mobilized citizens” to i

rom energy decision making [137,139], they are now claiming “their power as mobilized citizens” to influence both energy and climate agendas and to challenge the status quo of power relations [139]. Yet, it is only in recent years that government agencies and en- ergy organizations have been seeking to actively involve youth in their activities [71]. Sovacool et al. [140] argue the need for greater attention to under- standing the acceleration of transitions and their embeddedness in so- ciety.

Methods A systematic literature analysis was conducted based on the S

izens or as passive actors. 3. Methods A systematic literature analysis was conducted based on the Scopus database. Scopus is a scholarly database organized by a distinctive indexing system containing peer-reviewed literature, trade publications, conference proceedings, patent records and Websites, from 1996 on- ward [37,38]. Scopus was selected due to its coverage, indexing system, and the ability to replicate research queries [39–43]. Determining a comprehensive search string involved an itera

y to replicate research queries [39–43]. Determining a comprehensive search string involved an iterative process, due to the variety of terms with similar meaning as youth and the diversity of ter- minology related to renewable energy. Working with a library infor- mation scientist, the following search string was adopted: [TITLE-ABS-KEY (youth OR adolescent OR teenage OR youngster OR “young people” OR “young citizens” AND energy AND “renewable en- ergy” OR solar OR wind OR biofuel OR biomass OR

e” OR “young citizens” AND energy AND “renewable en- ergy” OR solar OR wind OR biofuel OR biomass OR bioenergy OR geothermal OR nuclear OR electricity OR “clean energy” OR “alternative energy” OR “green energy” OR “sustainable energy” OR transition OR future OR scenario]. This produced 4997 results of different document types in disciplines ranging from medicine to applied engineering (Fig. 1). The search was then limited to English-language peer-reviewed journal articles in Sco- pus subject are

e search was then limited to English-language peer-reviewed journal articles in Sco- pus subject areas of social sciences, energy, environmental sciences, and engineering, as we presumed these areas would have the most plausible connections to the topics of youth and renewable energy. This narrow- ing of the search parameters resulted in 531 articles. Results were then limited to articles published between 2005 and 2022. Narrowing the temporal scope to articles published 2005 onward was to captu

d between 2005 and 2022. Narrowing the temporal scope to articles published 2005 onward was to capture research in the decade prior to adoption of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, adopted by UN member states in 2015, and research that has emerged in the near decade since. The UN World Youth Report under the 2030 Agenda [29] emphasizes interna- tional and national actions to ensure youth engagement and participa- tion in implementing global sustainable development goals (SDGs), includ

h engagement and participa- tion in implementing global sustainable development goals (SDGs), including sustainable energy. This filtering generated 457 articles. There is a limited amount of energy scholarship published prior to 2005 that addresses youth, often focused on youth training or employment [141,142], and indeed scholarship that exists prior to the temporal scope of coverage of the Scopus database (i.e., prior to 1996) – usually focused on the impact of energy development (namely oil

us database (i.e., prior to 1996) – usually focused on the impact of energy development (namely oil and gas) on youth, such as social health or out-migration [130,131]. Titles and abstracts were then scanned for all 457 articles to exclude any studies that had limited to no direct relevance to the research topic, such as articles focused on youth health and energy (i.e., metabolism, nutrition) and technical engineering or design topics, yielding 127 ar- ticles. The remaining articles were then r

technical engineering or design topics, yielding 127 ar- ticles. The remaining articles were then reviewed to eliminate studies that focused on public acceptance of renewable energy but with no concentration on youth, or those focused on household choices about energy use but without specifying youth choices. Papers containing the term youth without studying youth per se, such as the energy efficiency of a youth center or building, were also excluded leaving a total of 91 papers for detailed an

iency of a youth center or building, were also excluded leaving a total of 91 papers for detailed analysis. A summary of the screening process is shown in Fig. 1. Articles were then reviewed in their entirety and coded thematically using NVivo v.12 qualitative analysis software. An iterative coding process was adopted, following the overall approach used by Leonhardt et al. [44] to explore government instruments for energy transition, and based on guidance for thematic analysis in qualitative re

ent instruments for energy transition, and based on guidance for thematic analysis in qualitative research [45] and applications of thematic coding in other research fields [46]. In the analysis, topics are defined as the specific areas or foci of research and themes as connecting multiple topics. Papers were first reviewed and coded to identify the key topics addressed in each paper, such as youth energy literacy, behaviour, awareness, perceptions, and attitudes regarding specific energy techno

h energy literacy, behaviour, awareness, perceptions, and attitudes regarding specific energy technologies or programs. Articles were coded 2 A. Jaradat et al. Fig. 1. Stages for searching and screening for publications related to youth an based on the major topics addressed, and it was possible that an article could address multiple topics and thus contain multiple codes. For example, an article may explore both youth literacy and youth percep- tions of renewable energy technologies. The comple

may explore both youth literacy and youth percep- tions of renewable energy technologies. The complete set of codes across all papers was then reviewed and grouped and re-grouped into parent topics or themes through an iterative process that involved multiple rounds of coding until no new themes emerged and until all topics were assigned to a theme. An initial five rounds of coding were done by the lead author, which were subsequently reviewed and validated inde- pendently by the second author,

e lead author, which were subsequently reviewed and validated inde- pendently by the second author, followed by an additional two rounds of coding by the lead author to arrive at the final set of themes. There are limitations to this approach and to this type of literature analysis and coding in general [47], for example limitations owing to the scope of search terms, the diversity of scholarship that may exist outside the peer reviewed journal literature (e.g., books, book chapters, tech- nical

that may exist outside the peer reviewed journal literature (e.g., books, book chapters, tech- nical reports), and the subjectivity in any qualitative approach to coding and thematic analysis. However, as noted by Leonhardt et al. [44], the approach adopted is commonly used by energy and social science scholars to explore or assess the state of knowledge and, arguably, provides a replicable method to enable the identification of dominant lines of inquiry and potential and critical gaps in schola

ndate policies for emissions reduction by citing court cases where youth have claimed that current policies (climate, energy, emissions) are practicing a form of discrimination against future generations and violate their rights. Most of the research captured by this theme is focused on the increasing role of youth in the multi-lateral energy space. The multi- lateral energy space is where actors (e.g., NGOs, women, Indigenous communities, labour unions, academia, businesses, local governments)

(e.g., NGOs, women, Indigenous communities, labour unions, academia, businesses, local governments) come together to discuss and work toward coordinated and collabora- tive actions of global importance [116]. Authors often refer to the Youth Strike for Climate (YSC), sparked by the Swedish teenager Greta Thun- berg in 2018, which motivated youth worldwide to engage in climate action [52], giving rise to other advocacy and grassroots initiatives to protest “unjust climate policies” that threaten

ise to other advocacy and grassroots initiatives to protest “unjust climate policies” that threaten future generations [80,118,117]. These advocacy groups range from regional to national university-based groups, such as Student Energy in Canada, contributing to energy literacy and youth empowerment through capacity building, knowledge dissemination, and advocacy actions for climate and energy justice [52]; to multi-lateral initiatives, such as the Youth Sustainable Energy Hub – the first global

ice [52]; to multi-lateral initiatives, such as the Youth Sustainable Energy Hub – the first global platform that aims to unite youth engagement in sustainable energy, developed by SDG7 Youth Constit- uency, and Brazil, Russia, India, China and South Africa (BRICS) Youth Energy Agency (YAE) [48]. That said, the literature also identifies two core challenges associated with youth climate advocacy. The first concerns the actual influence of youth councils or advocacy groups in the multi-lateral en

The first concerns the actual influence of youth councils or advocacy groups in the multi-lateral energy space, specifically international forums. Stanczyk [48] notes that while such youth councils are perceived to be effective in building relationships, providing quality input, and facilitating access to knowledge and re- sources among youth, multi-lateral spaces are not always inclusive, especially for marginalized and vulnerable populations. Strzelecki [71] implies that joining multilateral e

lly for marginalized and vulnerable populations. Strzelecki [71] implies that joining multilateral events does not necessarily translate to direct influence on climate and energy policies or actions – often owing to lack of clarity on how youth advocacy is channelled to decisions makers, the limited opportunity for more nuanced national to regional- focused conversations, and the limited long-term commitment beyond the specific events themselves. The second challenge concerns the con- flicts or

commitment beyond the specific events themselves. The second challenge concerns the con- flicts or inequalities that emerge from climate movements; for example, rural and Indigenous citizens shouldering the potential negative exter- nalities (e.g., wind turbines and landscapes) connected to renewable energy [119], and gender inequalities reflected by fossil fuel industries toward female climate activists [52]. Adding to the above are the intergenerational inequalities raised by climate movement

ctivists [52]. Adding to the above are the intergenerational inequalities raised by climate movement discourses [118] and the critiques of past generations' actions and decisions and the effects on the climate crises of today. 4.5. an Energy). Yet, the final theme emerging from our results, addressed in the least number of papers, is the role of youth in, and their influence on, local energy initiatives (Table 1). Sur- prisingly, very few papers explore specific methods or approaches to youth engagement in energy decision-making at the local level; those that do tend to emphasize citizens' assemblies and forums, online opinion polls, and preference survey instruments on governmental platforms [72,73]. However, as Dunlop et al. [72] note, such methods a

y instruments on governmental platforms [72,73]. However, as Dunlop et al. [72] note, such methods are often ineffective for empowering youth. There are barriers identified in research that prevent youth from being engaged in energy governance or decision-making at the local level. One of these barriers is the lack of recognition of youth and their exclusion from voting and consultation processes. For example, Dunlop et al. [72] report on the disproportionate impacts of hydraulic fracking on you

For example, Dunlop et al. [72] report on the disproportionate impacts of hydraulic fracking on young people in England (i.e., longer-term impacts, operations adjacent to schools), but note that youth under the legal voting age are excluded from government's formal consultation and decision-making processes. A second barrier identified is observed in rural and tradi- tional tribal communities in Indonesia, where the values, norms, and beliefs of elder leaders may limit the voice and role of you

Indonesia, where the values, norms, and beliefs of elder leaders may limit the voice and role of youth, preventing their social or political engagement in local renewable energy initiatives at the community level [23]. Other researchers emphasize men's power over youth (and women) when making decisions about local energy initiatives. For example, in Germany, Radtke and Ohlhorst [74] report patterns of injustice in community renewable energy projects, describing community energy as an “exclusive

s of injustice in community renewable energy projects, describing community energy as an “exclusive club for men”. Finally, in the absence of larger regional to national organized efforts, Nathan et al. [77] report that at the local level youth often lack the capacity to engage in rural energy projects – a scenario which, based on experiences with microgrid initiatives in rural India, can lead to project dysfunction and failure. 5. Discussion Five themes emerged from the results that characteriz

project dysfunction and failure. 5. Discussion Five themes emerged from the results that characterize the focus of recent scholarship on youth in energy transitions (Table 1). The domi- nant focus is on the importance of youth energy literacy and educating youth about energy options and the relationship between energy and environment [52,55]. Much of this literature emphasizes the importance of knowledge and capacity building among youth. This was followed by scholarship that explored and often

owledge and capacity building among youth. This was followed by scholarship that explored and often tested youth knowledge about and perspectives on energy, typically energy options or different energy technologies [49,66]. Literature focused on youth energy conservation behaviour and climate advocacy captured equal attention in the sample of papers reviewed. Studies on youth energy conservation behaviours often focus on youth lifestyles and energy consumption [51,98], espe- cially in developing

ehaviours often focus on youth lifestyles and energy consumption [51,98], espe- cially in developing regions, based on the premise that youths' under- standing of energy conservation will shape whether and how we achieve meaningful emissions reduction and energy transitions [50]. The climate advocacy literature focused on climate movements and youth participation in multilateral energy and climate spaces [48,71], emphasizing the importance of youth collective actions to drive energy and climate

spaces [48,71], emphasizing the importance of youth collective actions to drive energy and climate action. Finally, results show limited scholarship has focused on youth in energy governance, specifically the role and influence of youth at the local level in on-the-ground energy initiatives. Based on our analysis and the topics and themes that emerged, we offer three obser- vations concerning the current scope of scholarship and the framing of youth in energy transitions. Each of these is discu

current scope of scholarship and the framing of youth in energy transitions. Each of these is discussed below along with recommendations for future scholarship to support the societal embed- ding of energy transitions through youth engagement. 5.1. Science-based education vs. critical energy literacy Energy education and literacy are essential to building the required agency for youth to be key actors in energy transitions. Bastien and Holmarsdottir [32] argue that quality education and trainin

ctors in energy transitions. Bastien and Holmarsdottir [32] argue that quality education and training are foun- dational to youth driven innovation for social change. However, 6 A. Jaradat et al. research related to energy education and literacy is focused primarily on science-based education (e.g., technology, environmental science, en- gineering), with the main objective often to teach youth about energy technologies, climate, and conservation [62,63,92]. Although impor- tant, research on ener

ut energy technologies, climate, and conservation [62,63,92]. Although impor- tant, research on energy literacy must also consider the social, political, and cultural contexts in which such educational programs or in- terventions are occurring. We recommend that youth-based energy lit- eracy extend beyond the traditional science disciplines to better position youth as next-generation energy leaders [25] who are “capable of leading and making a difference” and, ultimately, reshaping power structu

s [25] who are “capable of leading and making a difference” and, ultimately, reshaping power structures [143]. Thus, building energy literacy among youth must consider multi-, inter-, and trans-disciplinary curricula that address energy literacy as multi-dimensional relationships between energy technology, social systems, cultures, and political systems. This is what Lowan-Trudeau and Fowler refer to as “critical energy literacy” [52,57], which can ultimately help shape more equitable energy fut

to as “critical energy literacy” [52,57], which can ultimately help shape more equitable energy futures, support the long-term sustainability of energy transitions, and provide for more an inclusive energy planning and policy environment. Related to the development of critical energy literacy among youth is the need for decolonization of energy literacy. This is explored through two interrelated exploratory hypotheses: First, higher opportunities for the participation of communities are closely linked to the models of particip

gher opportunities for the participation of communities are closely linked to the models of participation employed. Second, projects driven by social innovation initiatives are more likely to exhibit inclusive and transparent partici- patory practices, which are essential for achieving (wind) energy justice [12,13]. tential to challenge existing norms and power struc- tures, and therefore to cater to energy justice principles [14].
