and participatory processes that contribute to just energy transitions [10]. To address its hypotheses, the analysis takes stock of an original dataset, collected by the authors [18], of 312 cases of wind energy projects, 209 of which were considered to be led by SI [19]. Data was collated through a review and analysis of approximately 500 documents throughout 2023, including examples from across the globe, although most are from Europe and North America. the globe, although most are from Europe and North America. Focussing on wind energy allows for an examination of participatory practices within a well-established yet contested renewable energy technology sector [20], particularly as it encounters the entrance of emerging technologies, such as offshore floating wind [21]. Ecologies of participation, energy justice and social innovations Conceptually, the study takes a systemic a

s of participation, energy justice and social innovations Conceptually, the study takes a systemic and relational approach, cutting across sustainability transitions research in the scope of energy participation and social innovation studies [7,17]. In this literature, Chilvers and his colleagues [7] introduce the “ecologies of participation” framework, which understands participation systems as being shaped by both political cultures and institutional settings, and participatory practices to be

eing shaped by both political cultures and institutional settings, and participatory practices to be embedded in wider socio-technical systems [22]. Using this framework enables moving beyond an analysis of community participation in energy as isolated activities to characterise the dynamics and interrelations of such participation practices. Participatory practices are conceptualised along three constituting elements [7] (p.201), namely: the procedures for engagement, or “models” (e.g., a citiz

constituting elements [7] (p.201), namely: the procedures for engagement, or “models” (e.g., a citizen jury), the “subjects” of engagement (e.g. a farmers community) and the “objects” of engagement (e.g., a wind park, a new policy). These models, subjects and objects of participation are characterised as being “actively co-produced” (p. 201) through collective practices framed within “wider spaces of participation” (p.202) wherein participatory practices inter- connect. The framework equally emp

of participation” (p.202) wherein participatory practices inter- connect. The framework equally emphasises the relevance of in- stitutions, governance, political and regulatory frameworks, and established relations between diverse actor groups collectively advancing with energy projects, proposing that these form “systemic constitutional stabilities” (p. 202). These energy constitutions can be interpreted as a dominant socio-technical regime [23], as they are established and continuously reinfo

terpreted as a dominant socio-technical regime [23], as they are established and continuously reinforced by legal frameworks, policy and governance, while also being subject to transformative change. lic participants, activists and experts, and the “object” was the issue of nuclear energy and its governance. Media de- bates about climate change were considered wider spaces of participa- tion, whereas nuclear power is an important element of the country’s energy infrastructure and policy, and, therefore, part of its “energy constitution”. to the United Kingdom, with no link to procedural, distributive, and recognition justice aspects [2,9]. extent to which these elements enable an inclusive, transparent and influential engagement of citizens and communities, with examples from across the globe. , how the costs and benefits of wind projects are distributed) and recog- nition justice (i.e., who is (not) listened to nor included in the discus- sion) comes to the foreground [24]. Thus, this study offers an enquiry into procedural justice in the context of wind energy, while also reflecting on interrelated distributive and recognition justice aspects. on [7], the research provided critical insights for considering energy justice principles. Proposed ty- pologies include the well-known Arnstein’s “Ladder of Participation” [25] and Pretty’s “Typology of Participation” [26]. Arnstein’s Ladder identifies eight levels of participation based on how citizens can influ- ence decision-making, while Pretty’s typology is concerned with how different models of participation impact project outcomes. Aiken’s research [27] explores the role of trust and par

s of participation impact project outcomes. Aiken’s research [27] explores the role of trust and participation in wind energy projects, emphasising that mere information provision is not sufficient to ensure public support and that consultation and cooperation models are critical to address stakeholder concerns. Jobert and colleagues [28] compare wind energy development in different European countries to conclude that models such as consultation and cooperation lead to less opposition. Wüstenhag

ries to conclude that models such as consultation and cooperation lead to less opposition. Wüstenhagen and colleagues [29] evidence that financial participation mechanisms, such as community ownership, enhance local acceptance of wind energy projects. Research has also demonstrated that cooperation models can be more inclusive than mere financial and/or economic participation, as the latter may imply limited opportunities for engagement and involve a small group of stakeholders (e.g., land lease

ply limited opportunities for engagement and involve a small group of stakeholders (e.g., land lease payments, and in-kind payments). Some studies suggest that models, such as compensation payments, may be perceived as “bribes” and do not always entail active community participation [7,30]. In contrast, cooperation requires the active participation of citizens and communities, often throughout a project’s life cycle, from the early planning to construction and even decommissioning stages. roject’s life cycle, from the early planning to construction and even decommissioning stages. Studies have likewise recognised the role of resistance initiatives and networks [32,33] as one participatory practice. Materials and methods This review study is an analysis of a compilation of case studies of participation retrieved through a review of documents on wind energy developments. The analysis employs a qualitative and quantitative approach to investigate its two interrelated hypotheses, namely that higher opportunities for the participation of communities are closely linked to the models of pa

hat higher opportunities for the participation of communities are closely linked to the models of participation employed, and that projects driven by social innovation initiatives are more likely to exhibit inclusive and transparent participatory practices. A dataset [18] of participatory practices in wind energy was devel- oped, and subsequently built upon through qualitative and quantitative methods [48]. Data collection, employing web scraping techniques (i.e., surveying websites to collect a

hods [48]. Data collection, employing web scraping techniques (i.e., surveying websites to collect and store freely available information) [49] and data analysis took stock of open-source code, namely Python libraries [50], openly accessible for reuse and reproducibility. When applied, web scrapping techniques did not involve the collection of private data. Data was only collected when available on websites which, for instance, gathered information about community energy projects and did not inc

n websites which, for instance, gathered information about community energy projects and did not include the means for direct downloading of such infor- mation (e.g., RESCOOP. EU and Community Energy England websites). Cases depicting participation were determined based on the presence of keywords referencing participation in the collected corpus of docu- ments such as “engagement”, “involvement”, “social innovation”, “en- ergy community”, among others. These words were based on the synonyms dev

“social innovation”, “en- ergy community”, among others. These words were based on the synonyms developed by Chilvers et al. [7], while additional words/sy- nonyms were added in the six languages used for the search and analysis (i.e., English, Dutch, German, French, Portuguese and Spanish). n took place only once (e.g., a single moment for public consultation). However, emotional reactions to the siting of solar energy projects are still underexplored, although they appear to be a key element in the creation of the attitudes of the pop- ulation towards energy projects. Based on these considerations, it appears important to investigate whether the high acceptance level enjoyed by solar energy is likely to persist if the technology is deployed on a large scale,2 or whether it may face resistance similar to that associated with wind energy projects. For this purpose, the current study investigates how specific attitudes3 to- wards solar energy vary based on the size of the installation (solar rooftop versus solar park) and compare these to specific attitudes to- wards wind energy installations. The specific research questions are presented below: (1) How do differences in attitudes towards solar energy vary based on the size of installations, and how do the latter compare to attitudes towards wind energy installations? (2) What is the role of affect in the development of attitudes towards the different energy installations? In brief, the study finds that (1) attitudes towa

itudes towards the different energy installations? In brief, the study finds that (1) attitudes towards solar and wind park installations of a comparable size do not differ; (2) affect has a mediating role in the formation of attitudes; and, (3) the affect elicited by energy installations have the largest effect on attitudes in the case of large-scale energy installations. Literature review 2.1. Social acceptance of renewables An increasing amount of research ha

earch. 2. Literature review 2.1. Social acceptance of renewables An increasing amount of research has investigated the social accep- tance of renewable energy in recent years [e.g. 27,28]. Wüstenhagen et al. [27] list three different levels of social acceptance: socio-political, community and market acceptance. These three levels are not only interrelated but also involve different actors (project developers, poli- cymakers, consumers) who are involved in different levels [29]. For example, an i

developers, poli- cymakers, consumers) who are involved in different levels [29]. For example, an increase in challenges in the area of community acceptance (i.e., local acceptance) can impact pre-existing high levels of socio-political acceptance (i.e., general acceptance). This implies that social acceptance is a multidimensional and dynamic phenomenon [30, 31]. 2 Note: Scale, in the current study refers to the size of the energy infrastruc- ture only [e.g., 32, 33] as opposed to local and na

udy refers to the size of the energy infrastruc- ture only [e.g., 32, 33] as opposed to local and national levels, for example [e. g., 34]. 3 “Specific attitude” refers to attitudes towards local energy projects, “e.g., what people think about wind energy projects in their vicinity, regardless of the specific project characteristics [35, p.79]”. 2 R e n e w a b l e a n d S u s t a i n a b l e E n e r g y R e v i e w s145(2021)111107 Several studies have found high socio-political acceptance of t

e r g y R e v i e w s145(2021)111107 Several studies have found high socio-political acceptance of the different types thereof [36,37]. In terms of the acceptance of renewable energy technologies, solar energy stands out due to its positive socio-political perception and acceptance [16,36,38]. However, high levels of socio-political acceptance are a necessary but not sufficient condition for the successful implementation of energy projects. Regarding wind energy, different studies have identifie

uccessful implementation of energy projects. Regarding wind energy, different studies have identified high levels of socio-political acceptance [31,39], but wind energy projects frequently face acceptance-related issues on a local level that impede their deployment [40]. Moreover, solar and wind energy have experienced impressive learning curves resulting in significant cost reductions [41, 42] making renewables the most cost-effective source of new electricity generation [42]. This has led rese

king renewables the most cost-effective source of new electricity generation [42]. This has led researchers to argue that community acceptance from the population is a main limiting factor in the suc- cessful implementation of renewable energy projects [10,11]. As such, an important question is to what extent high levels of socio-political acceptance translate into high levels of local acceptance of energy pro- jects [16,27]. For many individuals, the transition to renewables is experienced as t

of energy pro- jects [16,27]. For many individuals, the transition to renewables is experienced as the extension of the energy system into places that were formerly un- touched [43, p.335]. Such interference with previously “unaffected” land appears to play an important role in the acceptance of energy sources at the local level. Wolsink [44] has, for example, highlighted that the visual impact of wind turbines on the landscape plays a sig- nificant role in the overall acceptance of wind power.

wind turbines on the landscape plays a sig- nificant role in the overall acceptance of wind power. Research has also noted that decision-making about the spatial implications of the implementations of renewable energy projects mainly involves land- scape issues, not only in terms of physical options for siting, but also the assessment of landscape values that may be affected by the infrastruc- ture [45]. Regarding the latter, the concepts of ‘visibility’ and ‘visual impact’ must be distinguishe

re [45]. Regarding the latter, the concepts of ‘visibility’ and ‘visual impact’ must be distinguished [45] as the implementation of energy projects must be planned not only in terms of their visibility, but also in terms of their visual impact: namely, the change in the quality of the landscape induced by the siting of the energy infrastructure [45]. Changes in the quality of the landscape are subjective as they are mainly due to individuals’ different assessments thereof. Accordingly, the relat

jective as they are mainly due to individuals’ different assessments thereof. Accordingly, the relationship between visual impact, landscape and perception by the population is complex [45] and the implementation of projects that do not take into account how the visual impact of energy projects may disrupt people’s values and emotions may hit roadblocks [20,45]. However, little research exists about the perception of the population concerning the visual impact of solar energy installations [e.g.

ut the perception of the population concerning the visual impact of solar energy installations [e.g., 46] and, more importantly, how such perceptions may vary based on the size of the installation. Controlling for the size of the installation in the case of solar energy appears to be particularly relevant, as previous research that used a Swiss sample, revealed that people associate solar power specifically with the placement of solar panels on rooftops, and not large-scale installations [16]. ecifically with the placement of solar panels on rooftops, and not large-scale installations [16]. Factors related to the proposed size of a project have been shown to be significantly related to their support, with larger energy projects being less likely to

shown to be significantly related to their support, with larger energy projects being less likely to be favorably received [44,48,49]. Further, Devine-Wright and Batel [50] highlighted the need to recon- sider the “localist” perspective adopted by earlier research. Specifically, the authors noted that the literature on the social acceptance of re- newables can be criticized for taking on a narrow spatial focus. They argued that low carbon energy infrastructure does not only involve local project

spatial focus. They argued that low carbon energy infrastructure does not only involve local projects. Instead, while having an evident local impact, it also involves concerns on multiple levels [43,50,51]. For example, people may feel loosely attached or estranged from the place in which they presently live [50,52,53]. In contrast, people may feel a strong attachment to localities other than those in which they now live, such as places where they have previously lived [49,54,55]. To contribute

e in which they now live, such as places where they have previously lived [49,54,55]. To contribute to this relatively new way of assessing attitudes towards local energy projects, the current study takes into account these aspects in how it measures the related attitudes by not only assessing attitudes about “local projects” involving energy projects close to respondents’ homes, but also in other places that individuals may feel close to. 2.2. Affect and the social acceptance of energy sources

places that individuals may feel close to. 2.2. Affect and the social acceptance of energy sources People’s judgments and decisions are oftentimes based on their feelings about objects (e.g., wind turbines) [56]. Feelings, which are part of the affective system of decision-making, are based on associative and intuitive processes that are generally faster than cognitive processing [57]. The affect elicited by an object can serve as a quick and efficient signal that contributes to decision-making

elicited by an object can serve as a quick and efficient signal that contributes to decision-making [58] given that positive or negative feelings associated with objects consequently guide their evaluation [59]. However, energy-related preferences have mainly been investigated from a cognitive perspective [e.g.60–62], and only a few studies have examined affective factors [16,26,63,64]. Researchers in the field of psychology have used word-association techniques for a long time [65–68]. Affecti

rs in the field of psychology have used word-association techniques for a long time [65–68]. Affective imagery, which is employed in the current research to measure affective reactions to solar and wind energy, uses word association to reveal the positive and negative associations elicited by a stimulus [21]. Researchers have used affective imagery to investigate different domains, including GM food [68], nuclear power [22], and smoking addiction [69]. Specific to energy sources, Jobin and Siegr

od [68], nuclear power [22], and smoking addiction [69]. Specific to energy sources, Jobin and Siegrist [38] highlighted that affect guided preferences for energy portfolios, and that solar energy generally elicited more positive affect than wind energy. Further, Truelove [26] found that affect was significant in predicting attitudes towards energy project implementation. Based on the former, the hy- pothesis is that feelings may have a mediating effect on the relationship between an energy inst

hy- pothesis is that feelings may have a mediating effect on the relationship between an energy installation and attitudes towards it. To the best of the author’s knowledge, although previous findings have highlighted that affect is important in the formation of attitudes towards energy source infrastructures, no studies have yet investigated how affective reactions to solar energy can vary based on the size of installations, or how the affect elicited by solar energy infrastructures of differen

on the size of installations, or how the affect elicited by solar energy infrastructures of different sizes compares to the affect elicited by wind energy infrastructure. 3. Data and methods 3.1. This is important, as past studies have highlighted that perceptions of renew- able energy infrastructures such as wind turbines or photovoltaic sys- tems not only depend on the infrastructure itself, but also on the visual impact of the energy infrastructures. The latter involves not only an asses

, but also on the visual impact of the energy infrastructures. The latter involves not only an assessment of the infrastructure as such, but of landscape quality change induced by the siting of the latter [12,45,76]. The pictures were selected to represent environments, which were not sterile but instead could elicit affective reactions from respondents (i.e., real Swiss landscapes). Further, the two pictures were presented in a random order to control for contamination effects. Note that the de

two pictures were presented in a random order to control for contamination effects. Note that the design of the energy infrastructure presented in the pictures does not account for regulations relating to whether an energy project could legally be implemented in the given setting, although regulatory issues play an important role in the selec- tion of site development. At the end of the survey, each group was surveyed about their attitude towards the implementation of a hypo- thetical solar park

ch group was surveyed about their attitude towards the implementation of a hypo- thetical solar park, solar rooftop, or wind park installation. Socio- demographic data about each subgroup can be found in Table C, Appendix C. 3.3. Measures 3.3.1. Affective reactions Affective reactions were measured following the method of continued word association, as described by Peters and Slovic [22]. To measure the general spontaneous associations and related affective evaluations elicited by wind and solar

re the general spontaneous associations and related affective evaluations elicited by wind and solar energy, respondents were asked “What are the first thoughts or images that come to your mind when thinking of solar/wind energy?”.6 In a second step, each subgroup was asked: “What are the first thoughts or images that come to your mind when looking at this picture?” (installation-specific level). Respondents were asked to give a minimum of one association, and a maximum of five. Each association

Respondents were asked to give a minimum of one association, and a maximum of five. Each association provided by the respondents was subsequently evaluated by them on a seven-point affect scale from 1 (“very negative”) to 7 (“very positive”). If a respondent offered five responses, the affec- tive evaluations of each of these was averaged to develop the mean af- fective evaluation. In the analysis, the mean affective evaluation of the associations was used, which corresponded to the average of e

, the mean affective evaluation of the associations was used, which corresponded to the average of every af- fective evaluation of each respondent. The descriptive statistics of the variables can be found in Table D, Appendix D. 3.3.2. Local concern To measure attitudes towards the implementation of the energy infrastructure, a proxy, local concern, which is an attitudinal measure, was used. Further, attitudes to the implementation of the energy infra- structure close to respondents’ homes or ot

er, attitudes to the implementation of the energy infra- structure close to respondents’ homes or other places they may feel close to, rather than attitudes towards the specific projects depicted in the pictures were measured. Specifically, local concern was measured by asking respondents to specify their level of agreement with the following statement: “I would be concerned if a wind park/solar park/solar rooftop installation was built close to my neighborhood or in other places I feel close to

ark/solar rooftop installation was built close to my neighborhood or in other places I feel close to,” on a scale from 1 (“totally disagree”) to 5 (“totally agree”). The design of this question – and in particular, the addition of the phrase “(…) in other places I feel close to” – was motivated by recent research by Devine-Wright and Batel [50], which highlighted the need to reconsider the “localist” perspective adopted by previous research. Discussion As different countries are revising their energy strategies with the aim of decarbonizing the energy system, the acceptance of renewables at the community (local) level will be a key element in the success of this energy transition

wables at the community (local) level will be a key element in the success of this energy transition and proposed energy policies. Earlier literature that compared preferences for different renewable energy sources did not control for the size of installations when determining respondent pref- erences. However, lacking this approach, it is difficult to gauge which types of installations respondents were expressing their preferences for. Installation size appears to be critical, as it may amplify

s were expressing their preferences for. Installation size appears to be critical, as it may amplify the visual impact of an energy installation on the landscape (namely, the change in landscape quality caused by the siting of the infrastructure) - which has been shown to be a dominant factor in the case of wind energy [45,76]. To address this gap and contribute to the research stream about the social acceptance of renewable energy and more specifically how the generally high acceptance level (i

cial acceptance of renewable energy and more specifically how the generally high acceptance level (i.e., socio-political acceptance) of re- newables may (may not) translate into a high acceptance level at the community level, the present study investigated how attitudes towards solar energy compare to attitudes towards wind energy, while control- ling for the size of the installation. This result indicates that comparing attitudes towards different technologies but not taking into account the size of infrastructure may reveal preferences that do not endure as the related technologies are deployed on a larger scale. This finding is important, as European energy policies have established clear targets for renewables, but the configuration of their deployment is

policies have established clear targets for renewables, but the configuration of their deployment is not yet clear. The latter factor is particularly important as the transition from fossil fuels to renewables has resulted in the proliferation of new types of landscapes that are sometimes contested, spurring interest in the relation between land use and social acceptance [80]. Wolsink [45] argues that local people and politics, not project de- velopers, should take center stage in decisions about site selection and the use of land. The latter may be of particular relevance because

cisions about site selection and the use of land. The latter may be of particular relevance because renewable infrastructure is increasingly being deployed, and at a larger size, meaning that its visual impact is becoming more significant. Correspondingly, while in the case of Switzerland the population voted in favor of significantly increasing the share of national renewables through the Energy Strategy 2050, there is still hardly any awareness of what the implementation of this strategy means

Strategy 2050, there is still hardly any awareness of what the implementation of this strategy means in spatial terms [15], while the active involvement of the latter in site selection may be critical in terms of the acceptance of the former [45]. In sum, policymakers should be aware that despite the general high acceptance of renewables, there exists the risk that any technology, if deployed on a large scale, might face resistance. Specific to solar energy, solar parks may face resistance conne

large scale, might face resistance. Specific to solar energy, solar parks may face resistance connected to land availability and use of land and to their visual impact - factors that have already been identified in the case of wind parks [45,81]. On the other hand, solar rooftop systems do not occupy productive land [81]. identified “unanimously positive imagery of solar power” [16, p.362]. 5.3. The important role of affect in attitude formation regarding energy infrastructure Another important element that arises from the analysis is that affect matters in developing attitudes towards an energy project. Affect was found to have a mediating role in forming attitudes towards energy in- stallations. Further, affect was the most relevant factor underlying at- titudes towards a wind park, solar park, and solar roofto

as the most relevant factor underlying at- titudes towards a wind park, solar park, and solar rooftop installation. Specifically, the more positive the affect, the lower the level of concern about the implementation of the energy project. Also interesting was the fact that when measured at a general level, affect had a larger effect on attitudes towards the implementation of a wind park than it did in relation to a solar park or solar rooftop instal- lation. Also, affect elicited by solar energy

d in relation to a solar park or solar rooftop instal- lation. Also, affect elicited by solar energy in general appeared to be a better predictor of the acceptance of solar rooftop installations than solar parks. The conclusion is that individuals typically thought of solar rooftop installations when thinking about solar energy at a general level. Further, the results highlighted that the regression models that included affect elicited by the solar and wind energy installations compared to solar

on models that included affect elicited by the solar and wind energy installations compared to solar and wind energy in general had greater explanatory power in terms of attitudes. This suggests that the more concrete an energy project appears to an individual, the more affect seems to matter in attitude formation. Additionally, the different sizes of the effect of affect on attitudes highlighted that the latter may matter more in the case of large-scale than small-scale installations. In the in

that the latter may matter more in the case of large-scale than small-scale installations. In the introduction, it was noted that Devine-Wright [20] highlighted that the implementation of energy technologies may disturb emotional bonds to a place, and that we can expect that these bonds will be more strongly disrupted as the size of the energy infrastructure increases. The results seem to support this assumption. Debates concerning the implementation of energy projects may thus become increasing

this assumption. Debates concerning the implementation of energy projects may thus become increasingly emotionally loaded as the size of 8 J. Cousse projects and the general deployment of renewable energy projects in- creases. In general, in line with earlier work [32–34], the current study highlights a negative relationship between project size and public sup- port. Future research may investigate whether many small projects rather than a few large ones may be preferred by the population. Final

e whether many small projects rather than a few large ones may be preferred by the population. Finally, results show that French-speaking respondents are more concerned about the implementation of a wind park than German- speaking respondents, and the opposite holds true in the case of a solar rooftop installation. With more wind parks being planned in the French-than in the German-speaking part of Switzerland, a larger part of the French-speaking population may be in the second phase of the U-

f Switzerland, a larger part of the French-speaking population may be in the second phase of the U- shaped curve, the phase during which the population is most critical. Interestingly, however, no differences between the two language regions in the case of attitudes towards solar parks were identified. This may be caused by the low level of familiarity or experience of the Swiss popu- lation with solar parks. 5.4. Limitations and future research As with any research paper, there are some limitat

solar parks. 5.4. Limitations and future research As with any research paper, there are some limitations that can the source for future research. First, the study focused on a specific geographical context. Switzerland is similar in many regards to other countries that have become engaged in the low-carbon energy transi- tion, yet it also has specific characteristics that may affect the research findings, such as a relatively high per-capita income, no carbon- intensive domestic electricity gene

indings, such as a relatively high per-capita income, no carbon- intensive domestic electricity generation, and a low density of solar and wind parks. It would be worthwhile investigating how attitudes to solar parks compared to those of wind parks vary in countries with different characteristics, especially in countries in which citizens are more familiar with solar park installations. Second, the research measured affective reactions to solar and wind energy on an abstract level, as well as re

e research measured affective reactions to solar and wind energy on an abstract level, as well as reactions to pictures of energy installations. However, debates concerning the implementation of en- ergy projects are often emotionally loaded, not only because of the na- ture of the energy infrastructure or its visual impact on the landscape, but also because of how project developers tackle the projects [84]. The current study investigated the acceptance of the public only and public opinion as

ojects [84]. The current study investigated the acceptance of the public only and public opinion as part of the socio-political and community acceptance di- mensions of the social acceptance triangle [27]. However, the concept of social acceptance of renewables is not only made up of mutually influ- encing processes at the three levels (community, socio-political, market) but also involves different actors (project developers, policymakers, consumers) who are active at different levels [29]. Fur

nt actors (project developers, policymakers, consumers) who are active at different levels [29]. Further studies could investigate how emotions are elicited and influenced in line with the different levels of the social acceptance triangle [27]. This calls for longitudinal research that could potentially contribute to the call of Wolsink [29] to recognize social acceptance as a “bundle of dynamic processes instead of a set of actors’ positions” (p.286). Third, the aim of the study was not to pre

processes instead of a set of actors’ positions” (p.286). Third, the aim of the study was not to predict individuals’ actual intentions to act in favour or against a specific renewable energy project, but rather to compare attitudes towards solar and energy projects in a hypothetical setting. Investigating the attitude formation process early on, however, may be very important for forecasting local acceptance or voting intentions [85]. 5.5. Introduction Many countries around the world are transitioning to lower emission energy technologies. One key component of this energy transition is the use solar of photovoltaics to create electricity [1]. Solar energy (e.g., using solar photovoltaics) is one of the fastest growing energy produc- tion methods and will have an important role to play in reducing the world’s reliance on fossil fu

- tion methods and will have an important role to play in reducing the world’s reliance on fossil fuels [2]. However, producing energy at the scale required to replace fossil fuel-based production methods will require large scale solar farms, in addition to roof-top solar. It is, therefore, important to understand the factors that may facilitate or impede the successful development and deployment of large-scale solar farms. One important stakeholder to consider when building large-scale solar fa

of large-scale solar farms. One important stakeholder to consider when building large-scale solar farms is the community that will live near them. Research has shown that community acceptance of public siting energy technologies, or a lack thereof, can greatly influence the successful deployment of renewable energy projects [3]. Future social licence research should consider the role of en investigating the factors that facilitate or impede local acceptance of renewable implementation of large-scale solar farms. Unlike other energy pro- duction technologies like wind turbines, few studies have comprehen- sively explored the acceptance of large-scale solar farms [4]. As such, this research explores the community acceptance of large-scale solar farms associated with seventeen explanatory factors (common social licence fac

of large-scale solar farms associated with seventeen explanatory factors (common social licence factors, worldviews, and agreement with the energy transition narrative) using a large, representative sample of the Australian popu- lation. Moreover, this work uses a novel exploratory data analysis approach to identify potential causal mechanisms that influence local community support for solar farms. Understanding these factors can inform planning and decision-making including approaches to commu

s. Understanding these factors can inform planning and decision-making including approaches to communication and engagement strategies and other interventions that support responsible development and deployment of large-scale renewable energy infrastructure into local communities. 1.1. Wider public attitudes versus local community acceptance Research to date suggests that people are generally supportive of using renewables and solar power in particular [5–7]. Visschers and ted 26 October 2023 ss

ortive of using renewables and solar power in particular [5–7]. Visschers and ted 26 October 2023 ssarticleundertheCCBYlicense(http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/). M. Scovell et al. Siegrist, for example, found that people tend to be more accepting of solar power than other energy technologies such as nuclear, wind and natural gas [6]. Similar results have also been found in Australia, with people tending to support the use of wind and solar over other energy technologies like hydro-pow

th people tending to support the use of wind and solar over other energy technologies like hydro-power, nuclear, coal and gas [5]. Another study comparing levels of renewable energy acceptance across countries in Europe also found that solar power had the highest level of support of all renewable energy technologies [8]. Overall, these findings suggest that people, for the most part, like the idea of using more solar power in the future; though it is less clear on people’s perceptions of large s

of using more solar power in the future; though it is less clear on people’s perceptions of large scale solar farms. Furthermore, high levels of broader public acceptance does not necessarily translate into high levels of acceptance at the smaller com- munity level [9–13]. Some community members experience, or expect to experience, more of the costs (e.g., local environmental impacts) and less of the benefits (e.g., wealth creation) associated with an energy production technology than the broad

the benefits (e.g., wealth creation) associated with an energy production technology than the broader population [12]. It is not un- common for publicly supported renewable energy projects to be met with local opposition [14]. Wind farms, for example, are strongly sup- ported at the population level but often face local opposition for a va- riety of reasons [15,16]. Few studies have, though, comprehensively investigated how communities think about, and are likely to respond to, large-scale sola

rehensively investigated how communities think about, and are likely to respond to, large-scale solar farms [2,4,17], especially the role of the wider energy transition narrative on local community acceptance. 1.2. Local community acceptance of renewable energy developments Recent empirical research has investigated levels of local support for utility-scale solar energy installations and the factors that influence it. Carlisle and colleagues in a series of studies found that local support for ut

rs that influence it. Carlisle and colleagues in a series of studies found that local support for utility-scale solar in the US is high, but varies depending on a number of factors [2,17,18]. In two studies, they found that local support for utility-scale solar in California was significantly influenced by perceived positive impacts like jobs and increased property value [2,18]. They also found that, contrary to past research [11], place-based beliefs (e.g., level of agreement that where they li

at, contrary to past research [11], place-based beliefs (e.g., level of agreement that where they lived was beautiful, had a great commu- nity of people etc.) and perceived negative impacts had no significant influence on support for utility-scale solar when controlling for other factors like perceived positive impacts [2,18]. In another study, using a sample from the whole US population, it was found that political ide- ology, believing that a solar farm would spoil scenery, and trust in the de

ound that political ide- ology, believing that a solar farm would spoil scenery, and trust in the developers were significant predictors of acceptance of a solar farm in one’s local county [17]. Other factors like the perceived seriousness of climate change and believing that a solar development would decrease property value were not significant predictors of local acceptance [17]. These findings suggest the perceived personal and community benefits of a local solar project are particularly stro

suggest the perceived personal and community benefits of a local solar project are particularly strong drivers of local acceptance. However, the statistical models in all these studies only explained relatively small amounts of variability (between 18 and 40 % in sample) in local support, which suggests other important factors need to be included to more comprehensively explain solar farm acceptance. More recent research investigating local acceptance suggests that while communities are not agai

ce. More recent research investigating local acceptance suggests that while communities are not against large-scale solar, they are more supportive of smaller scale solar applications [4,19]. Two recent studies, for instance, found that support for large ‘utility-scale’ solar was significantly lower than support for rooftop or community-scale solar [4, 19]. The findings suggest that as the size of the projects increase, the level of support tends to decrease. Cousse also found that, contrary to

the projects increase, the level of support tends to decrease. Cousse also found that, contrary to past findings, people express similar levels of support for solar and wind when controlling for the size of the project suggesting that the type of technology is less important than the overall footprint [4]. One expla- nation for this finding is that larger-scale solar projects seem to be viewed as an ‘industrial activity’ and thus people tend to think more about the local impacts [19]. Theories

an ‘industrial activity’ and thus people tend to think more about the local impacts [19]. Theories of social licence and reviews of empirical research highlight a few key factors that help to explain local acceptance of energy-related 2 R e n e w a b l e a n d S u s t a i n a b l e E n e r g y R e v i e w s 189(2024)114029 projects [10,20–22]. These beliefs and attitudes can be broadly cat- egorised into three main explanatory factors: (1) beliefs about a project’s consequences (2) social trust

sed into three main explanatory factors: (1) beliefs about a project’s consequences (2) social trust and relationships with industry operators, and (3) implicit attitudes and/or feelings about the energy technology. This section will briefly summarize these three main belief/attitude categories and highlight how they are usually operationalised in empirical research. One important set of beliefs that is a reliable predictor of local acceptance is how people think about the consequences (i.e., ef

hat is a reliable predictor of local acceptance is how people think about the consequences (i.e., effects) of an energy project. Perceived consequences are often categorised as the perceived negative impacts (e.g., perceived risks and/or costs) and the perceived benefits (both local and societal). Studies that measure perceived impacts (or perceived risk/cost) find that the more people agree that a project will cause negative outcomes, the less they support it [23–28]. Conversely, believing that

a project will cause negative outcomes, the less they support it [23–28]. Conversely, believing that an energy project will bring about benefits is positively related to acceptance [29–33]. There is some evi- dence to suggest that perceived individual and community effects of a solar farm (both benefits and impacts) are also important for explaining local solar farm acceptance [2,18]. However, the past research investi- gating large scale solar farms uses relatively few survey items to assess pe

past research investi- gating large scale solar farms uses relatively few survey items to assess perceived impacts and benefits. As such, it is relatively unclear from previous research what types of perceived effects are important for explaining solar farm acceptance and why. Of particular importance is the extent to which people trust the company involved with an energy project or the development of a technology [20,34]. Several studies have shown that social trust is strongly associated with

nt of a technology [20,34]. Several studies have shown that social trust is strongly associated with acceptance in many different contexts [26, 35–37], including the acceptance of solar farms [17]. However, the findings are mixed regarding the extent to which social trust explains acceptance in less contentious energy industries like renewable energy projects [9,23]. Associated with social trust, other psychosocial factors also seem to explain local acceptance of energy projects. For example, pr

other psychosocial factors also seem to explain local acceptance of energy projects. For example, procedural fairness, believing that the community has been fairly involved in a company’s decision making, and relationship quality, believing that there is a good community relationship with a company, have both been shown to be strongly association with trust and, in turn, local acceptance [26,35]. In addition to these factors, other research finds that distributive fairness (i.e., believing that

In addition to these factors, other research finds that distributive fairness (i.e., believing that local benefits will be shared fairly) and governance (i.e., believing that governance would be effective and trustworthy) also help to explain variability of a measure of local acceptance [26]. While these factors do seem to explain local acceptance in contentious energy industries (e.g., mining and coal seam gas extraction) it is unclear from past research how much these addi- tional contextual f

coal seam gas extraction) it is unclear from past research how much these addi- tional contextual factors help to explain local solar farm acceptance. Beyond more explicit beliefs about a project’s impacts and the context, affect and/or feelings tend to be another reliable predictor of energy technology acceptance. Affect is a broad term that can encom- pass general moods, specific emotions and feelings.1 Psychological research has consistently shown that people’s affect and/or feelings are str

eelings.1 Psychological research has consistently shown that people’s affect and/or feelings are strongly associated with explicit attitudes, preferences and behaviour [38,39]. The affect heuristic provides one explanation for this finding and posits that people tag attitude objects (people, places, technology) with either positive or negatively valanced imagery [40,41]. Put simply, people tend to form quick evaluations of things based on feelings of relative ‘goodness’ or ‘badness’ they associa

form quick evaluations of things based on feelings of relative ‘goodness’ or ‘badness’ they associate with it. There is some evidence to suggest the feelings people associate with solar farms help to explain their reported level of acceptance. For example, Cousse found that one of the strongest predictors of local concern about a large-scale solar farm was the affective responses people report (i.e., ‘liking’ or ‘disliking’) when shown pictures of a solar farm [4]. The same study found that whi

.e., ‘liking’ or ‘disliking’) when shown pictures of a solar farm [4]. vely valanced words when asked to think about solar panels in general, when respondents were presented with pictures of large-scale solar farms they tended to think of more negatively valanced words. The results suggest people tend to associate images of larger scale pro- jects with more negatively imbued thoughts, which in turn, lowers community acceptance [4]. These findings are supported by other research showing that people tend to think about solar power, and other renewables, in an abstrac

er research showing that people tend to think about solar power, and other renewables, in an abstract way, which tends to lead to more favourable evaluations [9]. However, when people are asked to think more about the potential negative impacts of a solar farm project (e.g., toxic waste generated during production), then acceptance is lower [9]. The find- ings suggests that the feelings people associate with solar farms should help to explain acceptance. 1.3. Wider public attitudes toward the en

ociate with solar farms should help to explain acceptance. 1.3. Wider public attitudes toward the environment and energy While most studies investigating social acceptance of energy tech- nologies tend to focus on contextual factors, other research suggests that broader beliefs people hold about related topics (e.g., climate change, the environment, politics) help to shape how people think about energy projects [42]. Several studies have, for example, shown that pro- environmental beliefs (and/o

nergy projects [42]. Several studies have, for example, shown that pro- environmental beliefs (and/or environmental concern) and specific values (e.g., altruism) help to shape attitudes towards a range of energy technologies [16,43–46]. Moreover, these values seem to influence acceptance through their influence on more context specific beliefs like the perceived risk and benefit of a technology [16,29,42,47]. There is evidence to suggest that broader beliefs and values also influence local solar

6,29,42,47]. There is evidence to suggest that broader beliefs and values also influence local solar farm acceptance. Carlisle et al. highlights that broader beliefs about the environment and one’s political affiliation are related to local support for utility-scale solar [17]. Cultural theory posits that environmental values, beliefs, and attitudes interplay with political perspectives and about how best to address environmental issues such as climate change [48]. This interplay is reflected in

how best to address environmental issues such as climate change [48]. This interplay is reflected in broad worldviews about relationships between humans and nature, the robustness of the environment to change, the need for human intervention, and the role of government and technology [49]. Another important aspect that has received little consideration in past research is the extent to which people support more specific nar- ratives about the renewable energy transition [50,51]. A narrative typi

e support more specific nar- ratives about the renewable energy transition [50,51]. A narrative typically addresses why a technology is needed, why this technology over other options, and how this technology solution fits into a broader solution, in this case as part of a contested energy mix [50]. The narrative reflects the underlying rationale as to how effectively the technology addresses a broader problem or issue. Narratives have been identified as important in shaping public debate and cri

ader problem or issue. Narratives have been identified as important in shaping public debate and critical in speeding up or slowing down a transition to novel technologies [52]. The narrative around the energy transition developing in Australia has different dimensions based on the role that individual energy sources are seen to play in the economy and how they play into the trilemma of energy needs [53,54]. Australia, for example, is a country that has created much of its wealth due to mineral

ds [53,54]. Australia, for example, is a country that has created much of its wealth due to mineral and energy extraction and export. As such, energy transition narratives include implications for these industry sectors. The energy narrative also stipulates a need for energy to be affordable, sustainable and reliable for the everyday household and for the functioning of the economy. This implies a po- tential trade-off: the willingness of households to trade-off energy affordability and reliabil

po- tential trade-off: the willingness of households to trade-off energy affordability and reliability for a speedier energy transition. 3 R e n e w a b l e a n d S u s t a i n a b l e E n e r g y R e v i e w s 189(2024)114029 1.4. A country experiencing an energy transition The public discussion of the energy mix of the future also needs to be set in context of the role that energy plays in the revenue of the country and national economic benefits. Australia is an energy exporter with coal min

revenue of the country and national economic benefits. Australia is an energy exporter with coal mining accounting for 1.7 % and activities from oil and gas extraction accounting for around 2 % of the gross domestic product [55] 2. Additionally, royalties contribute around $10 billion or 4.4 % of the states’ general finance statistics revenue [55].3 Almost 19 % of Aus- tralia’s product exports in 2019 were fuels [56] and the country is one of the top four global exporters of LNG and a top two co

2019 were fuels [56] and the country is one of the top four global exporters of LNG and a top two coal exporter [57]. Like many countries dependent on fossil fuel related revenues, a tran- sition to the use of renewables and phasing out of fossil fuels extraction can be perceived as a risk for this source of revenue and economic ac- tivity in the country. It is, therefore, likely that public beliefs about the broader energy system in Australia and, particularly its historical reli- ance on the f

fs about the broader energy system in Australia and, particularly its historical reli- ance on the fossil fuel industry for economic growth, will influence how people think about renewables and, in turn, solar farms. Different energy resources will have different timelines for being phased in or phased out. In global demand scenarios based on stated policies and announced pledges, natural gas still appears to have a long- term role to play in the energy mix of countries and plausibly in Aus- tra

l appears to have a long- term role to play in the energy mix of countries and plausibly in Aus- tralia’s energy exports [58]. Perceptions about natural gas and its future role in Australia [53], as well as the country’s capacity to become an exporter of renewable energy should also influence views about the role for renewables and local acceptance of solar farms. Determining the extent to which such view influence local acceptance also has implica- tions for other countries outside of Australia

uch view influence local acceptance also has implica- tions for other countries outside of Australia experiencing a similar en- ergy transition who also rely on natural gas as part of their energy system. 1.5. Aims This study has two main aims. The first is to provide a descriptive representation of the social licence factors that are associated with local large-scale solar farm acceptance. Past research highlights the impor- tance of explanatory factors such as perceived impacts, benefits and s

esearch highlights the impor- tance of explanatory factors such as perceived impacts, benefits and social trust for explaining local acceptance of energy technologies. However, the relative importance of such factors, using a comprehensive set of measures, for explaining the local acceptance of solar farms has yet to be tested empirically. The second aim of this study is to explore the extent to which broader beliefs about the energy transition narrative may further influence local solar farm ac

hich broader beliefs about the energy transition narrative may further influence local solar farm acceptance (directly and via other explanatory factors). As this study uses an exploratory data analysis approach, no specific predictions are made about the relative impor- tance of specific variables, causal mechanisms, or the validity of a spe- cific theoretical model. Instead, the goal of this study is to identify robust effects in a large, representative sample to aid future research and theory

dy is to identify robust effects in a large, representative sample to aid future research and theory development. The findings are also relevant to practitioners seeking to understand the key factors underpinning social acceptance of large-scale solar farms and how these are influenced by energy transition narra- tives. The research is of particular relevance to those parts of the world where fossil fuel related industries still hold a key role in the economy. 2. Methodology This research used a

alian population. To recruit participants, a market research company sent information about the survey to Australians enrolled on their research panel. Participation in the study was voluntary for members of the research panel and par- ticiaptns were awarded points (redeemable for monetary vouchers) from the market research company for participation. In total, 3103 re- spondents completed the survey, 45.9 % male 53.8 % female and 0.2 % who preferred not to disclose the gender or identified as no

ey, 45.9 % male 53.8 % female and 0.2 % who preferred not to disclose the gender or identified as non-binary. This sample size was chosen to achieve sufficient statistical power for the planned analyses, which extended beyond the analysis presented in this research. The sample was representative of the Australia population with respect to gender, age and location of residence (see Walton et al. Most of the respondents completed high school (84 %) and over a third had a uni- versity degree (i.e., a bachelors and/or postgraduate degree; 37.6 %). 2.2. eptance or any of the other psycho- logical constructs assessed in the survey [59]. As such, the experiment conditions were not included as confounding variables in the subsequent analyses. Data analysis method The data were analysed using u

nuous variables in the subsequent analysis. 2.3. Data analysis method The data were analysed using undirected network models to explore the conditional associations between the variables of interest. While still relatively novel when applied to human data, the network approach has been used in other contexts to explain attitudes [62], psychopathology [63], personality [64] and intelligence [65]. Applied research has also used this approach to explain attitudes towards plastic [66], vaccine decis

pplied research has also used this approach to explain attitudes towards plastic [66], vaccine decisions [67], exercise behaviour [68], environmental related beliefs [69] and responses to the COVID-19 pandemic [70]. However, to the authors’ knowledge, this is the first time that a network modelling approach has been used to explore how psychological factors influence local acceptance of an energy technology. This innovative approach can shed light on the complex factors that drive acceptance of

technology. This innovative approach can shed light on the complex factors that drive acceptance of energy technologies, which is crucial for effectively implementing sustainable energy solutions in communities around the world. Estimating undirected network models was selected as an analysis approach for two main reasons. First, network modelling is well suited for exploratory analysis. Whether specific variables (i.e., nodes) will be connected and the direction of the relationships do not need

pecific variables (i.e., nodes) will be connected and the direction of the relationships do not need to be specified a priori when estimating a network model. This is useful when a strong theory does not exist, and the goal is to, instead, identify robust phenomena (e.g., conditional associations between explanatory factors). As argued in the introduction, there are many different factors that may influence the acceptance of large-scale solar, and the mechanism by which these factors influence a

influence the acceptance of large-scale solar, and the mechanism by which these factors influence acceptance are often unclear. A good example of an unclear causal mechanism is the association between trust, the perceived effects of an energy technology and acceptance. Some research argue that trust causes differences in risk/ benefit perception and, in turn, causes acceptance [34]. Other work suggests that the direction runs the other way: beliefs about impacts and benefits causes changes in t

ggests that the direction runs the other way: beliefs about impacts and benefits causes changes in trust and, in turn, acceptance [26,35]. Other explanatory factors like fairness and relationship quality are also hypothesised to influence acceptance through different mechanisms depending on the theory [20,26,35]. As such, a modelling approach that relaxes the assumption of causality and specific pathways is useful in this context and can assist future theory development and hypothesis generation

athways is useful in this context and can assist future theory development and hypothesis generation. The second reason for using an undirected network analysis approach is that these models do not assume acyclicity. That is, undi- rected network models do not assume that causality only flows in one direction (i.e., there are no feedback loops in the model). Not assuming acyclicity is useful in this context as many of the associations between psychological factors may be caused by bidirectional

is context as many of the associations between psychological factors may be caused by bidirectional effects, or by un- observed (latent) variables. For example, perceived impacts may covary with perceived benefits due to independent causal effects that each 5 R e n e w a b l e a n d S u s t a i n a b l e E n e r g y R e v i e w s 189(2024)114029 variable has on the other, or due to an unobserved latent variable (e.g., an implicit attitude towards solar technology; [40,71]). Using a modelling app

atent variable (e.g., an implicit attitude towards solar technology; [40,71]). Using a modelling approach that does not assume acyclicity, and therefore not ruling out feedback loops and the presence of latent variables, makes it easier to identify such effects to refine existing theories and inform the design of future confirmatory research. Undirected network models also offer a unique perspective of prac- tical importance by allowing variables to exert simultaneous influence on multiple other

ve of prac- tical importance by allowing variables to exert simultaneous influence on multiple others. Most statistical models applied in the context of understanding social acceptance focus on a singular dependent variable and thus obscure the intricate interconnections among independent variables. This approach proves especially advantageous when exploring complex acceptance phenomena, as variables can propagate effects through a web of intricate associations that evade clear visuali- zation v

les can propagate effects through a web of intricate associations that evade clear visuali- zation via conventional methods like linear regression. By modelling variable associations in this manner, it is possible to ascertain which variables have the greatest influence across the entire network. These insights carry significant practical implications for policy and decision- making, as it is possible to determine the most influential variables within a system, rather than fixating on single biv

ible to determine the most influential variables within a system, rather than fixating on single bivariate associations. Gaussian Graphical Models (or partial correlation networks) were estimated using the ‘bootnet’ [72] package in RStudio. A Gaussian Graphical Model is a type of undirected network model that assumes a multivariate normal distribution. In its simplest form (without regular- isation or pruning of edges), a Gaussian Graphical Model represents bivariate partial correlation coeffici

n or pruning of edges), a Gaussian Graphical Model represents bivariate partial correlation coefficients between all pairs of variables included in the model, conditioning on all other variables. In this study, network models were estimated using the mixed graphical model [73] function inside bootnet, which uses nodewise regressions and the lasso regularisation penalty to reduce spurious edges (i.e., weak coefficients). The nodewise estimation approach was chosen so that the explained variabilit

e., weak coefficients). The nodewise estimation approach was chosen so that the explained variability in sample (i.e., R2) could be calculated for each node in the network. Graphical representation of the results of the undirected network models were created using the qgraph package [74]. When plotted, the nodes in graphs represent each variable included in the analysis, and the links or ‘edges’ (associations been variables) can be interpreted as par- tial correlations or scaled regression coeff

associations been variables) can be interpreted as par- tial correlations or scaled regression coefficients (see Ref. [75] for a summary of how partial correlations correspond to regression co- efficients). The thickness of the edges represents the strength of the regularised regression coefficient between two variables (nodes) after controlling for all other variables in the network. In other words, a thicker edges represent higher regression coefficients. Another way to interpret the network g

ds, a thicker edges represent higher regression coefficients. Another way to interpret the network graphs is to think of each node as a distinct dependent (outcome) variable in a multiple linear regression model with all other nodes being independent (predictor) variables. The spring layout was specified to create the plot, which uses the Fruchterman-reingold algorithm [76] to determine spacing between nodes in a way that reflects the relative strength of associations [74]. Put simply, nodes tha

tween nodes in a way that reflects the relative strength of associations [74]. Put simply, nodes that have stronger associations are placed closer together in the graph. The colour saturation and width of edges are scaled with reference to the strength of the coefficients. 3. Results 3.1. Discussion This research explored the socio-psychological factors that in

beliefs in the network. 4. Discussion This research explored the socio-psychological factors that influence local solar farm acceptance using a network modelling approach. In accordance with past social acceptance research, perceived benefits, feelings and distributional fairness were all directly associated with local solar farm acceptance [20,24,26]. However, unlike similar research in other energy domains, other social factors (e.g., trust and relationship quality) did not have direct associa

rgy domains, other social factors (e.g., trust and relationship quality) did not have direct associations with acceptance and had rela- tively weak indirect effects via typical mediating variables (e.g., perceived benefits and impacts). The findings also suggest that support for renewable energy in general (i.e. the energy transition narrative) is an important factor helping to explain local solar farm acceptance. This section will expand on these main empirical findings and outline some directi

farm acceptance. This section will expand on these main empirical findings and outline some directions for future research with reference to relevant theory. The discussion will end by highlighting some of the study’s limitations and summarising the main implications. 4.1. Broader beliefs about the energy transition One novel finding in this research was the relatively strong associ- ation between local solar farm acceptance and general support for renewable energy. Support for renewables had o

n local solar farm acceptance and general support for renewable energy. Support for renewables had one of the strongest bivariate correlations with acceptance and was directly related to both acceptance and positive feelings when controlling for all other variables in the first network model. One explanation for this finding is that people tend to think about solar farms, and their benefits, in a broader, more abstract way. Put another way, it seems that local solar farm acceptance is strongly i

broader, more abstract way. Put another way, it seems that local solar farm acceptance is strongly influenced by a positive attitude towards re- newables in general compared to a more considered assessment of the local impacts (both positive and negative) of a solar farm. This expla- nation is supported by past research showing that people tend to think about renewable energy technologies at an abstract level, which tends to result in a positive attitude [4,9]. stract level, which tends to result in a positive attitude [4,9]. hough conditional associations in the network do not infer causal effects, theory would suggest that the ‘fragile’ worldview has a causal effect on other factors as it is a more fundamental attitude. Theories that explicate the role of ‘worldviews’ and/or ‘values’ posit that they influence energy technology acceptance via their effect on more contextual beliefs the (e.g., perceived risk and benefits of a technology; [29,46]). ts of a technology; [29,46]). The findings, overall, suggest that those who endorse this ‘fragile’ worldview tend to be more accepting of local solar farms. ut the energy transition. Fig. 3 shows that thinking the environment is fragile is most strongly associ- ated with two specific beliefs about the role of renewables. etwork. The finding suggests that these two beliefs, which seem to be shaped by a specific worldview, have a strong influence on people’s overall attitude toward renewables and the energy transition more broadly. Other beliefs about the potential direct effects of using more renewables (e.g., energy reliability, energy cost and employment op- portunities) have a relatively weak influence on the network. twork. These findings, again, support the idea that how people think about renew- ables in the abstract primarily shapes their overall attitude towards them. Although some broader beliefs about the renewable energy transi- tion seem to help explain local acceptance, others seem less important. Another interesting finding was that willingness to make trade-offs was quite isolated in the network models. els. This is a surprising result as strong support for renewables should be reflected in an increased willingness to make personal sacrifices if required (i.e., pay more for electricity or experience more blackouts). This finding suggests that while people tend to have a positive attitude towards renewables, such an attitude may not necessarily translate into behaviour that will facilitate a faster renew- able energy transition (e.g., willingness to pay more for energy if 8 M. nct in the network models. This finding suggests that beliefs about the role for renewables are uninfluenced by beliefs about other com- ponents of Australia’s current energy system. be more supportive of renewables, but less supportive of fossil fuels [5]. The findings also suggest that if the continued use of fossil fuels does not impede the development of renewable energy technologies, views about the role of fossil fuels will have a limited effect on attitudes towards renewables and, in turn, local solar farms. 4.2. Regarding decarbonisation, the total estimated territorial greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions were 454.8 million tonnes of carbon dioxide equivalent (MtCO2e) in 2019, 43.8% lower than the 1990 level. bon dioxide equivalent (MtCO2e) in 2019, 43.8% lower than the 1990 level. In particular, the estimated GHG emissions from power stations were 58 MtCO2e in 2019, 65.5% lower than the 1990 level [1]. This paper provides a new angle to examine the different impacts of renewable policies on consumers and the electricity sector. Our first contribution is to construct a framework for the electricity sector with three players (consumers, traditional generators, and renewable gener- ators) and three markets (a domestic electricity market, an international fossil fuel market, and an international carbon ma

(a domestic electricity market, an international fossil fuel market, and an international carbon market). The scheme may bring negative gain to consumers, as [17] indicate, but we suggest that this negative gain was transferred to (or offset by) the positive gain received by renewable generators (and suppliers), leaving no impact on the sector. Our analysis shows that the costs of support schemes were considered when discussing the gain to consumers but became irrelevant when addressing the net

were considered when discussing the gain to consumers but became irrelevant when addressing the net gain to the sector.6 Further, we suggest that the transition to renewable electricity brought positive net gain to the electricity sector from two aspects. First, as the UK was dependent on the imports of fossil fuels, a decrease in the electricity generated by tradi- tional generators should reduce the costs of importing from the inter- national market, reducing energy dependence and improving e

e the costs of importing from the inter- national market, reducing energy dependence and improving energy security in the UK. Second, as the UK was included in the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS), lower GHG emissions meant that UK tradi- tional generators were not required to buy additional allowances or could sell their abundant allowances to installations in other countries under the system. e 200 Mt.11 Since the 1960s, the coal mining industry has been on a long declining trend mainly due to cheaper substitutes such as oil, gas, nu- clear, imported coal, and recently, renewables.12 In the last two decades, from 2000 to 2020, as Fig. 1 shows, coal production declined from 31.2 Mt to 1.7 Mt. industrial plants) across EU Member states and airlines operating internally.16 The EU ETS covers around 40% of the EU’s greenhouse gas emissions, and it was the crucial legislation that contributed to the reduction of GHG emissions by 31% below 1990 levels in 2020, exceeding the target of 20% [58]. Further, in 2020, the target was extended to 55% by 2030, compared to 1990 levels [59]. The EU ETS adopted the cap-and-trade system, in which a cap is set on the total amount of certain greenhouse g

adopted the cap-and-trade system, in which a cap is set on the total amount of certain greenhouse gases that can be emitted. By reducing the cap gradually, the system ensures that total emissions fall over time. Each allowance gives the holder the right to emit: one tonne of carbon dioxide (CO2) or the equivalent amount of other greenhouse gases, measured as tonne of carbon dioxide equivalent (tCO2e). O2e). Each year, an installation should surrender adequate allowances according to its emissions, and penalties are imposed on any quantity of allowances missed. Players and markets This section illustrates a framework of the electricity sector, as shown in Fig. 5. The framework includes three players (consumers, traditional generators, and renewable generators) and three markets (a competitive domestic electricity market, an international market for fossil fuels, and an international carbon market). fuels, and an international carbon market). The electricity sector may include both wholesale and retail markets, which are jointly rep- resented by a domestic market, so suppliers (retailers) are not included for simplicity. However, renewable generators may sign Power Purchase Agreements (prices tend to be fixed but below the average market price) with suppliers, so some of the benefits from support schemes were realised by suppliers rather than generators. Before referring to the framework, we first consider the situation of a closed domestic electricity sector, i.e., coal is produced domestically and the domestic GHG emissions trading system is isolated from other countries. Large-scale renewable energy projects offer a range of potential benefits for regional1communities – including employment, landholder payments and community benefit-sharing schemes – but they can also yield a variety of adverse impacts, including disruption of visual amenity and land use, and economic volatility [4]. The design of REZs, whi

ding disruption of visual amenity and land use, and economic volatility [4]. The design of REZs, which makes it more likely that a given area will host multiple projects, “concentrates both the benefits and the impacts for local communities” (INT12N; see Appendix A1 for details of interview focus/group partici- pants). d projects (e.g. pooled benefit-sharing schemes, regional training initiatives and coordinated impact management), it also increases the challenge of dealing with adverse impacts and the risk of community opposition.
