Embodied political ecology Facilitated by decades of globalization, an estimated 70 % of inter- national trade takes place through global supply chains [11]. These are functionally and geographically fragmented networks led by lead firms that seek to find the greatest economic advantage in having parts of commodities produced in stages using different suppliers, producers, and factories [c.f. [12]

rts of commodities produced in stages using different suppliers, producers, and factories [c.f. [12]. Global supply chains have significant socio- ecological impacts [7,13,14], with analysis frequently focusing on spe- cific issues, corporations, and sectors to ascertain whether companies are meeting environmental, social, and governance standards. Many environmental and social standards are established by multinational corporations pre-empting regulation or to demonstrate their corporate social

lished by multinational corporations pre-empting regulation or to demonstrate their corporate social responsibility considering reputational risks from controversies informing shareholder and consumer behaviour [15]. A range of voluntary transnational governance initiatives for RE now exist with many driven by companies, states, and international organi- zations including the EU and the OECD, as well as non-government or- ganizations in a variety of multistakeholder initiatives [10,16]. Private

ell as non-government or- ganizations in a variety of multistakeholder initiatives [10,16]. Private or non-state market governance [17,18], includes an abundance of voluntary codes, self-defined standards, certification systems, and public reporting [19,20]. LeBaron and Lister [8] reviewed 290 studies of the effectiveness of initiatives to govern global supply chains and observed that they often do not meet the criteria for minimising impacts even against their own metrics. This reinforces conce

o not meet the criteria for minimising impacts even against their own metrics. This reinforces concerns that global supply chain governance lacks accountability given they are designed by corporate actors whose primary interest is not mitigating socio- ecological impacts [7,9]. Rather than focusing on specific impacts, this paper argues that commodities like solar PV have an ‘embodied political ecology’ or a range of socio-ecological impacts that occur throughout the product life cycle and there

ology’ or a range of socio-ecological impacts that occur throughout the product life cycle and therefore along the global supply chain. This is a conceptually driven argument that builds on concepts from the multidisciplinary literature on energy and supply chains such as ‘embodied energy injustice’ [21–23] and ‘shadow ecology’ [24] to recognise a plethora of socio-ecological impacts that arise across temporal and spatial scales from producing these commodities for global markets. The term ‘embo

ross temporal and spatial scales from producing these commodities for global markets. The term ‘embodied’ captures the idea from the building industry that energy and emissions created for construction should be counted in its impact, which scholars have argued should also include environmental in- justices as detailed below [21]. A solar panel, like a building, has embodied emissions as well as contributing to socio-ecological impacts such as extractivist violence, eco-toxicity, pollution, wate

ontributing to socio-ecological impacts such as extractivist violence, eco-toxicity, pollution, water depletion, community impacts, and forced labour, as documented below. We invoke the concept of ‘political ecology’ to capture these harms, where political ecology is an interdisciplinary field that recognises that environmental change is embedded in social, economic, and political interactions [25]. It investigates how economic structures and power relations impact socio-ecological relations acr

]. It investigates how economic structures and power relations impact socio-ecological relations across temporal and spatial scales [26]. of political and economic decision making throughout the life cycle of producing commodities like solar PV for a global market. For example, scholars invoke ‘sacrifice zones’ to explain economic and political decisions to contain environmental impacts and send waste elsewhere [27]. in primarily poor and people of colour communities, such that they had to bear the unjust burden of toxic pollution, waste, and chemical exposure [28]. source dependence, or sys- tematic patterns of unequal exchange that lock states into “destructive development dependencies of primary extraction, land expropriation, elite capture, and unsafe disposal of toxic and hazardous waste” [1]. tices that occur to people along the supply chain. onmental impact assessments undertaken at the extraction and production points, the argument is that the entirety of the global supply chain is not captured in the assessment of the commod- ities' impacts. aking around the world. In the following section we outline the methodology for identifying and tracking the socio-ecological impacts of solar PV through LCA and non-LCA studies before tracing them along the global supply chain. 3. Methodology We articulate the embodied political ecology of solar PV via two ways: through the LCA approach which is often used in geo-engineering to capture primarily ecological, energy, and health harms, and through non-LCA studies that capture social impacts derived from a compre- hensi

d health harms, and through non-LCA studies that capture social impacts derived from a compre- hensive literature review, including the RE transition literature dis- cussed above. LCA studies use a standardised assessment framework to document impacts from energy systems (ISO 1440-2006). LCAs commonly identify four lifecycle stages: 1) raw material extraction, processing, and manufacturing; 2) installation; 3) operation and main- tenance (O&M); and 4) end-of-life (EoL) including decommissioning

tallation; 3) operation and main- tenance (O&M); and 4) end-of-life (EoL) including decommissioning and disposal. As illustrated in Table 1which summarises our literature re- view findings, LCA studies have often focused on energy-related envi- ronmental indicators (e.g. Energy Pay Back Time [EPBT], and GHG emissions) [36,37]. This is important because it brings together a comprehensive list of all of the known impacts from producing solar PV using this approach, and provides a baseline for iden

l of the known impacts from producing solar PV using this approach, and provides a baseline for identifying ecological and human health impacts of solar PV. This is detailed below. Although LCAs are useful tools to identify impacts throughout the lifecycle of a given technology, certain socio-ecological harms are difficult to quantify and are more appropriately documented using qualitative methods. For example, LCA's focus on energy-related metrics obscure impacts such as social dispossession an

. For example, LCA's focus on energy-related metrics obscure impacts such as social dispossession and poor labor practices, including forced labor. in relation to solar PV. lobal supply chain. Drawing together the full impacts of solar PV into the concept of embodied political ecology builds on current concepts that are limited in how they apply to global supply chains, and the site specific and inductive approach they take to investigating their impacts. Embodied political ecology provides a way to capture all known socio-ecological impacts from all political and economic decisions along the supply chain, thus incorporating findings of site and stage specific stud

onomic decisions along the supply chain, thus incorporating findings of site and stage specific studies into a broader conceptualization of the commodity. The geographic concentration in Xinjiang for polysilicon production also has implica- tions for GHG

The geographic concentration in Xinjiang for polysilicon production also has implica- tions for GHG emissions, as its electricity is primarily based on coal, making this province particularly emissions intensive. The manufacturing of solar PV panels also requires the use of toxic materials and chemical processes. This increases human toxicity, eco- toxicity, and carcinogenic, and resource depletion impact categories [77]. Several hazardous materials are used in the dominant PV modules, includin

on impact categories [77]. Several hazardous materials are used in the dominant PV modules, including lead in c-Si PV panels and cadmium in thin-film CdTe PV panels (see Tables 4 and 5).4[151] recommend substituting toxic ma- terials to minimise socioecological impacts where possible. Solar PV records higher GHG emissions than wind and hydropower but is still significantly lower than that of fossil fuels [36,38,39,69]. Other impacts include water consumption, acidification and eutro- phication p

l fuels [36,38,39,69]. Other impacts include water consumption, acidification and eutro- phication potential, as well as having occupational health and safety impacts, economic dependence, exploitation, job insecurity, and com- munity marginalisation [29,104] (see also Tables 2 and 3). For example, despite having less socio-ecological impacts and being touted as a means to achieve (an anti-racist) ‘energy democracy’ and ‘energy justice’ [81], the decline of solar PV manufacturing in Germany from

‘energy democracy’ and ‘energy justice’ [81], the decline of solar PV manufacturing in Germany from competition with China is an example of how some communities became marginal- ised from the global competition for solar PV production [29]. China's role as a lynchpin of the global supply chain has also revealed forced labour as a human rights issue. According to official Chinese government reports published in late-2020, 2.6 million, or around one- fifth of Uyghur, Kazakh, and other ethnic mino

s published in late-2020, 2.6 million, or around one- fifth of Uyghur, Kazakh, and other ethnic minorities in the in Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region (XUAR) have been placed in state- sponsored ‘labour transfer’ programs to produce commodities, including solar PV, under the guise of poverty alleviation [104,105]. The solar PV supply chain is particularly vulnerable to the use of forced la- bour, given the supply chain's concentration in Xinjiang, as well as the dominance of c-Si technologies in

given the supply chain's concentration in Xinjiang, as well as the dominance of c-Si technologies in the PV market. Companies have been given significant subsidies to move to the XUAR and participate in forced labour programs. Examining the solar PV industry, [105] iden- tified “11 companies engaged in forced labour transfers; 4 additional companies located within industrial parks that have accepted labour transfers; 90 Chinese and international companies whose supply chains are affected” (p. 7;

ed labour transfers; 90 Chinese and international companies whose supply chains are affected” (p. 7; see also Table 5). This illustrates how the PV chain is affected by forced labour, with companies early in the manufacturing process directly affected by labour transfers. This, in turn, affects later stages of the PV chain, even if these companies are not directly involved. This is a non-exhaustive list, and for many companies their involvement in labour transfer programs lacks transparency [105

e list, and for many companies their involvement in labour transfer programs lacks transparency [105]. Responses to forced labour are discussed in section six. Stages 2–3: Installation, Operation and Maintenance: Decisions that shape the embodied political ecology of solar PV also occurs at the installation and O&M stages although there are compa- rably fewer impacts than upstream stages. it does include land 4 Two main PV sub-technologies control the bulk of the global solar PV market: crystalline silicon technology (c-Si, 97.5 % in 2022) and thin-film PV (the remainder of global PV production, with CdTe PV representing the bulk of this share, [119]). C–Si technology is “expected to dominate the solar PV market for many years to come” ( [119]; see also [85]). exclude third generation technologies even though c-Si PV technologies are particularly energy intensive compared with other solar PV, resulting in higher GHG emissions and EPBT [41,61,69,70,152]. The embodied political ecology of solar PV this stage extends to wherever solar panels have been exported, installed, and maintained. Size and siting matter in terms of the socioecological impacts of solar PV at this stage. Land use requirements for solar PV farm construction contributes to deforestation and habitat loss, leaching of toxic materials, as well as visual and noise impac

es to deforestation and habitat loss, leaching of toxic materials, as well as visual and noise impacts [96,153]. Several RE studies document that solar PV records relatively low land use impacts, especially compared with biomass (e.g. [46,50]). In comparison, fossil fuels and nuclear power have ‘secondary effects’ on land use via site contamination and disruptions to local ecosystems [50].5The area required for solar farms can be 2–3 times larger than the area occupied by the actual solar panels

a required for solar farms can be 2–3 times larger than the area occupied by the actual solar panels [154]. Grid expansions to accommodate the uptake of solar PV will also entail additional land use, although Luderer et al. [64] argue that this “is small compared to the land footprint from the general electricity grid” (p. 5). Options for reducing land use for solar PV include rooftop and ‘building integrated’ PV (BIPV) systems [93]; floating PV [153]; and agrivoltaic systems [88]; as well as up

g integrated’ PV (BIPV) systems [93]; floating PV [153]; and agrivoltaic systems [88]; as well as upgrading or replacing solar PV systems at existing sites of electricity generation, which would limit land use and soil degradation. Although solar PV panels do not make noise during operation, installation creates some noise pollution, impacting local communities and animal populations [96]. These noise impacts are relatively minor compared with other RE during their construction phases. Construct

oise impacts are relatively minor compared with other RE during their construction phases. Constructing and operating large-scale solar PV farms also creates visual pollution, which can lead to community opposition and project delays [96]. Ways to reduce visual impacts are the same as for reducing land requirements or using previously contaminated ‘brownfield’ sites. Smaller-scale community RE also reduce visual impacts associated with utility-scale projects, and studies have shown that communit

o reduce visual impacts associated with utility-scale projects, and studies have shown that community engagement reduces opposition to RE [155]. Water use requirements for solar PV are also found to be minimal [51,66]. Although the operation phase is considered to have only minimal impacts relative to the overall life cycle, the use of toxic materials does raise the possibility of them leaching into soil and groundwater if the panel is damaged (e.g. due to reoccurring stress, extreme weather, or

to soil and groundwater if the panel is damaged (e.g. due to reoccurring stress, extreme weather, or poor quality). time” (pp. 1674, 1679). Proper maintenance and monitoring can minimise this risk [48]. In the social science literature scholars have highlighted that people may be dispossessed of their land or access to communal resources to make way for solar parks through “legal eviction, illegal expropriation (land grabbing), land speculation,” and losing their land rights leading them to become economically displaced [1,156]. In India, large-scale solar parks have created land use conflicts in often poorer

displaced [1,156]. In India, large-scale solar parks have created land use conflicts in often poorer, agricultural regions and have seen small landholders dispossessed of farmland, while poor households have also been restricted from accessing biomass and pastoral lands which disproportionately affects lower caste women [110]. Through the concept of ‘Cuerpo-territorio’ feminist political ecologists underscore the deep connection between land and people, where “[W]hat is experienced by the body i

s underscore the deep connection between land and people, where “[W]hat is experienced by the body is simultaneously experi- enced by territory in a codependent relationship” [84]. While using different ontological and epistemological frameworks, feminist political ecology reinforces the utility of the concept of solar PV's embodied po- litical ecology because it shows how economic and political decisions about energy shape socio-ecological impacts on marginalised communities. Stage 4: End-of-li

ecisions about energy shape socio-ecological impacts on marginalised communities. Stage 4: End-of-life: Like stages 2–3, the end of life (EoL) stage in the solar PV life cycle is not limited to China, although it outstrips the rest of the world in installed capacity and therefore must grapple with disposing of solar PV on a larger scale. Indeed, the top five markets for cumulative waste volumes are China, the US, Japan, India, and Germany [157]. Mean- while some developing countries are already

e China, the US, Japan, India, and Germany [157]. Mean- while some developing countries are already reporting environmental and health issues due to poor disposal [119,158]. The embodied polit- ical economy of solar PV captures how decisions about disposal con- tributes to socioecological impacts. Currently, solar PV panels are assumed to last between 25 and 30 years; they can be disposed in landfill, incinerated, re-used, or recycled, and in some cases used for land reclamation [69,159]. LCA st

ill, incinerated, re-used, or recycled, and in some cases used for land reclamation [69,159]. LCA studies often exclude the EoL phase [61], although this is changing (e.g. [40,60,63,73,74]; see Table 1). Significant increases in PV waste anticipated after 2030 [157]. To date, most solar PV are sent to landfill or are incinerated [77,95,151,159]. This poses a problem given their toxic materials (e.g. lead, chromium, bismuth, tellurium, cadmium, selenium, nickel, zinc, silver), especially if they

g. lead, chromium, bismuth, tellurium, cadmium, selenium, nickel, zinc, silver), especially if they are improperly disposed of which could result in toxins leaching into soil and water systems [40,48,63,94,95]. Incin- eration releases toxic and carcinogenic emissions into the atmosphere [73], impacting human health and ecosystems [94,95]. Recycling is the preferable option [40,44,48]. The dominant c-Si technologies use hazardous materials such as lead and silver, while thin- film PVs such as CdT

c-Si technologies use hazardous materials such as lead and silver, while thin- film PVs such as CdTe uses cadmium/tellurium, which have different recycling possibilities (see Tables 4 and 5). Introduction Electricity and modern energy access are crucial for economic and social development, as reflected by Sustainable Development Go

y access are crucial for economic and social development, as reflected by Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 7 and its synergies with the other Goals of Agenda 2030 [1,2]. A lack of reliable energy is still an issue for seventeen million people in Latin America. A major reason for this is that most of Latin America’s energy poor live in rural hard-to-reach areas in Non-Interconnected Zones (NIZ), making it harder to connect them to a centralised grid system [3]. In Colombia, even though the coun

making it harder to connect them to a centralised grid system [3]. In Colombia, even though the country reached a 95 % electrification rate over a decade ago, approximately 1.9 million people lacked clean and reliable electricity access in 2020 due to living in NIZ, covering 52 % of Colombia’s territory [1,72]. Connecting NIZ to cen- tralised grid systems is often not a commercially viable option and not highly prioritized politically, which makes providing reliable energy to NIZ inherently diff

and not highly prioritized politically, which makes providing reliable energy to NIZ inherently difficult [8]. However, Colombia has extremely favour- able conditions for wind and solar energy production [4], leading several researchers to suggest decentralised renewable energy micro- grids as a potential solution to reach the energy poor in Colombia’s NIZ [1,5,6]. This paper aims to address these research gaps by placing the views of current end users of renewable energy microgrids that live in NIZ in low-income commu- nities in a developing country first and comparing it to people in their neighbouring communities without such system

oping country first and comparing it to people in their neighbouring communities without such systems. This is especially important since the social dimensions of diffusing microgrids often determine whether an innovation is successfully adopted by the poten- tial beneficiaries [9]. The Wayuu people in La Guajira, Colombia, were chosen as a case for this study since they reside in a NIZ zone where decentralised solar energy microgrids have started to be deployed on a small scale [25,26]. At the

decentralised solar energy microgrids have started to be deployed on a small scale [25,26]. At the same time, the vast majority of the region’s rural population still suffers from energy poverty [25]. The study aims to identify the specific enablers and barriers to microgrid diffusion and how the presence of microgrids amongst neighbours impacts the willingness to adopt them by non-users. The Colombian context In Colombia, one of the main electrification challenges is the rural energy poor’s geographical isolation, making connecting them to the national electricity grid inherently hard [1]. The difficulty hindering providing the rural energy poor with clean and reliable energy is that 52 % of Colombian territory

g providing the rural energy poor with clean and reliable energy is that 52 % of Colombian territory lies in NIZ, making it difficult and expensive to connect them to the national grid [8]. The NIZ are areas which do not have access to the National Interconnected System of energy (SIN), as seen in Fig. 1 and have an estimated population of 1,900,000 inhabitants [67], with a population density of three inhabitants per square kilometre [66]. Comparably, the average population density in Colombia a

hree inhabitants per square kilometre [66]. Comparably, the average population density in Colombia as of 2020 is 46 people per square kilometre of land area [68]. This low population density and geographical isolation make it technically and economically non-viable to connect these NIZ areas to the SIN [1]. Furthermore, as seen in Fig. 2, the density of the NIZ varies. Some parts of the NIZ have a much lower population density than three inhabitants per square kilometre, making it even harder to

much lower population density than three inhabitants per square kilometre, making it even harder to connect these areas to the SIN [66]. The people with energy in Colombia’s rural areas typically depend on diesel gen-sets that face an insecure supply [71]. Currently, 95 % of people with energy access in Colombia’s NIZ use diesel, and the remaining 5 % use renewable energy, driven by the high subsidies provided by the national government to fuel suppliers, reaching more than USD 33 million in 20

idies provided by the national government to fuel suppliers, reaching more than USD 33 million in 2018 [1]. The Colombian government aims to address this and has, since 2015, created an enabling environment for non-conventional renewable energy technologies [1]. Colombia has two relevant legislations supporting the diffusion of renewable energy technologies in NIZ – Article 185 of the National Development Plan (2014–2018) (Law 1753 (2015)) [27] and Law 1715 (2014) [28]. elopment Plan (2014–2018) (Law 1753 (2015)) [27] and Law 1715 (2014) [28]. The first legislation sets up a fund for developing Colombia’s Pacific Coast [27]. The second legislation promotes the diffusion and use of renewable energy nationally, including in NIZ [28]. Where the desert meets the sea This study focuses on the rural indigenous Wayuu communities that inhabit Northern Colombia’s La Guajira peninsula, as seen in Fig. 3 [35]. Although Colombia has abundant and diverse resources, La Guajira is an extremely arid region that is only abundant in energy and minerals [35]. With some of Colombia’s and the world’s best solar and wind energy conditions, La Guajira has the potential to provide reliable, su

world’s best solar and wind energy conditions, La Guajira has the potential to provide reliable, sustainable energy to its entire population [36,37]. Consequently, large-scale renewable energy projects, defined as renewable energy facilities larger than 10 MW, and their associated transmission lines have started to be built in the region on Wayuu territories but only connect to larger cities [38]. A major reason for this is economic; large-scale renewable energy farms are supported by multinati

. A major reason for this is economic; large-scale renewable energy farms are supported by multinational companies and the gov- ernment and have the potential to provide a vast energy supply to Colombia’s cities, which propose high economic rewards [38]. How- ever, these high economic rewards often come at the expense of the local Wayuu population, who do not receive the additional energy created on, or close to, their territory [39]. In addition, since many Wayuu people live in rural conditions

on, or close to, their territory [39]. In addition, since many Wayuu people live in rural conditions spread out over La Guajira, connecting each home to a centralised grid is inherently hard [39]. Theoretical framework and factors determining successful microgrid diffusion in developing countries This study employs a modified version of diffusion theory as its theoretical underpinning to grasp the enablers and barriers of microgrid adoption [45]. The focus of diffusion theory is to determine the factors making the diffusion of an innovation successful. The theory consists of fo

to determine the factors making the diffusion of an innovation successful. The theory consists of four main elements: the social system where the innovation ought to be diffused, the innovations factors, communication channels facilitating ural materials in La Guajira (authors own photo). 4 I. Granit or hindering the diffusion, and time. This research centres around the social system, innovation aspect, and the communication channels used, as seen in Fig. 5. The time dimension and the entire ado

spect, and the communication channels used, as seen in Fig. 5. The time dimension and the entire adoption curve of diffusion theory is not elaborated upon extensively due to the time limitations of this study. Adequately including the time dimension would require a longitudinal study of how the adoption curve changes over time within the Wayuu communities which is out of scope of this study. This study defines innovation following Roger’s [45] definition as “an idea, practice or object that is p

study defines innovation following Roger’s [45] definition as “an idea, practice or object that is perceived as new by an individual or other unit of adoption.” The innovation elements are often the most crucial underpinning factor determining whether the diffusion will be successful or not and covers five main characteristics. The first one is an innovation’s relative advantage [45]. In developing countries, especially in low-income communities, the relative economic advantage of an innovation

g countries, especially in low-income communities, the relative economic advantage of an innovation is particularly important to consider [46]. With low pur- chasing power and limited financial opportunities, diffusing an energy innovation amongst potential adopters in such contexts is impossible if the innovation cannot provide an economic advantage to the recipients [23]. This is often one of the major barriers to renewable energy microgrid diffusion since such technologies often have high ini

e major barriers to renewable energy microgrid diffusion since such technologies often have high initial in- vestment costs [13]. Due to the high initial costs, potential recipients must have the extra capital to invest in the innovation and be provided with sufficient information about the full cost of adopting a renewable energy microgrid, especially its low maintenance cost compared to fossil fuel-driven systems which can encourage diffusion [24]. Other impor- tant relative advantages in deve

uel-driven systems which can encourage diffusion [24]. Other impor- tant relative advantages in developing contexts are the microgrids’ ability to provide reliable energy and their ability to save time for the adopter [13,16]. An innovation’s compatibility with the potential adopters’ norms, values, and needs is the second factor [45]. Microgrids, although of- fering clear benefits, often face many barriers due to failing to consider the recipients’ views, the inclusion of their norms in the dif

y barriers due to failing to consider the recipients’ views, the inclusion of their norms in the diffusion pro- cess, and a lack of understanding from the recipients’ side of why the energy innovation is needed [14,15]. Another important factor to consider when attempting to diffuse an energy innovation in indigenous communities is involving them in the planning process [21]. Neglection of the community’s needs and views often creates resistance to energy initiatives, leading to significant diff

munity’s needs and views often creates resistance to energy initiatives, leading to significant difficulties in the diffusion process and Fig. 5. The elements 5 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e101(2023)103132 failure of the diffusion attempt [22]. Previous microgrid diffusion at- tempts in developing countries have failed due to these reasons. For example, in India, an attempt to diffuse microgrids in a new context failed to include the views of the recipients about the in

pt to diffuse microgrids in a new context failed to include the views of the recipients about the innovation, who claimed that microgrids provided fake energy that was insufficient to meet their energy needs, leading the initiative to fail [14]. To avoid such scenarios, the involvement of the target recipients in the early stages of microgrid implementation is essential [15], especially to increase awareness of the system’s benefits [19]. In low-income communities in developing countries, such u

e awareness of the system’s benefits [19]. In low-income communities in developing countries, such understanding is easiest transferred through direct observation and intrapersonal communication methods, leading to a higher probability that microgrid innovations are diffused [20]. A crucial aspect to consider when aiming to diffuse innovations using the sun and the wind for energy is the cultural worldview of such resources. For the Wayuus, the sun and wind are important elements holding sig- ni

worldview of such resources. For the Wayuus, the sun and wind are important elements holding sig- nificant cultural value, which might significantly impact their view about technologies that use the resources to create energy [22]. The third innovation element is how complex the innovation is [45]. Training to acquire the necessary skills to use the innovation is crucial when trying to diffuse it on a wider scale within a social system [17,18]. This aspect is particularly important in developing

on a wider scale within a social system [17,18]. This aspect is particularly important in developing countries, especially in low-income communities, where information is often provided by people from the outside with different ways of learning [24]. Creating an understanding and providing training on how to use an innovation is therefore a significant aspect to focus on in such contexts, especially for renewable energy innovations that might be unheard of in such a sce- nario [47]. The last two

ly for renewable energy innovations that might be unheard of in such a sce- nario [47]. The last two innovation factors determining the rate of diffusion are observability and trialability. For an innovation to be effectively diffused in a social system, the potential adopters must be able to observe how it functions or try it out to determine its benefits fully [45]. The Wayuu people have strong networks and communal ties, therefore, the ability to have seen how microgrids functions in other co

networks and communal ties, therefore, the ability to have seen how microgrids functions in other communities are likely to be high if their neighbours have access to such systems [48,49]. According to diffusion theory, opinion leaders, the most influential individuals within a social system, are on the higher end of the social hierarchy and can greatly impact diffusion by promoting or disputing an innovation [45]. Materials and methods This study draws on diffusion theory and follows a qualitative comparative case study app

nd methods This study draws on diffusion theory and follows a qualitative comparative case study approach. The comparison is between Wayuu people without energy access, with solar driven microgrids, and that use windmill systems. A comparative study is especially effective to apply in circumstances that rely on multiple sources of evidence that need data triangulation, copes with a particular technical context with numerous variables, and where prior frameworks and theories guide the study desig

nical context with numerous variables, and where prior frameworks and theories guide the study design, data collection and analysis [50]. Three different research methods are used to underpin the comparative approach, namely ob- servatory research, semi-structured interviews, and focus group dis- cussions (FGDs). In total, 33 Wayuus from five distinct communities took part in this study – 17 participants in the semi-structured interviews and 16 different participants in the FGDs, as seen in Appe

cipants in the semi-structured interviews and 16 different participants in the FGDs, as seen in Appendix A. Since semi-structured interviews allows for questions to be amended during the interview process and helps create and direct a fluid con- versation while keeping the participants’ focus on the research topic it was chosen as the primary research method [53]. Furthermore, espe- cially important when working with communities with different access to energy, semi-structured interviews allow f

nt when working with communities with different access to energy, semi-structured interviews allow for possible varied views and knowledge amongst the participants to be included [51]. The in- terviews lasted approximately 20 to 50 min. Twelve were conducted in- person and five were conducted via phone. Seven men and 10 women participated in the interviews, as seen in Appendix A. All respondents were asked the same questions; however, relevant follow-up questions and elaborations by the particip

ere asked the same questions; however, relevant follow-up questions and elaborations by the participants were included for clarification purposes when needed [51]. Six questions about the participants char- acteristics were asked in the beginning to start the interview. A total of 12 to 23 questions, including follow-up questions, were asked relating the reliability of the systems in providing energy, usage, relationships with other people using energy, how the system was acquired, cultural aspe

rgy, usage, relationships with other people using energy, how the system was acquired, cultural aspects, opinions, benefits, and the system’s relative advantage. The FGDs were chosen as an additional research method since it allows for more of an informal discussion to take place by departing from topics and discussion questions similar to the ones used during the semi-structured interviews [49]. Results and discussion The individuals representing the group with solar microgrids expressed strong relative advantages since they had acquired access to the innovation. Prior to the solar microgrids, they used to access water through either the older windmill systems or manually by throwing a bucket down a well and pulling it up. All solar energy users stated that the ne

nually by throwing a bucket down a well and pulling it up. Introduction The global aspiration to keep global temperature rises below 2, but preferably 1.5 degrees, calls for a profound transformation of prevailing socio-economic structures [1]. Over the past decades, scholarly litera- ture has designated these necessary transformations as sustainability or low-carbon transitions [2–4]. In

has designated these necessary transformations as sustainability or low-carbon transitions [2–4]. In essence, these transitions aim to sub- stitute carbon intensive energy resources and technologies for low- carbon variants. However, one of the key challenges related to low- carbon transition policies relates to their potential to induce social in- justices. As such, civil society, activists, non-governmental organisations and scholars occupied with this challenge are demanding climate justice p

governmental organisations and scholars occupied with this challenge are demanding climate justice pathways and just transitions [5–12]. Against the backdrop of justice concerns, an increasing amount of scholarly literature is directed at a deeper understanding of low-carbon transition policies and their social outcomes. This paper addresses this topic by underlining three individual literature reviews on transition policies and social outcomes [13–15]. Because the concept of low- carbon transit

Climate justice, just transitions and inequalities Climate justice (CJ) and just transition (JT) discourses both share origins in the environmental justice discourse (EJ). While justice is a plural concept which varies across contexts, EJ typical

l justice discourse (EJ). While justice is a plural concept which varies across contexts, EJ typically treats injustices as “distributive inequity, lack of recognition, disenfranchisement and exclusion, the undermining of basic needs, capabilities and functioning of individuals and communities” ([20], p. 360) and in relation to envi- ronmental hazards and natural resources. Most scholars trace the beginning of EJ to the 1982 resistance against toxic waste disposal in Warren Country, North Caroli

beginning of EJ to the 1982 resistance against toxic waste disposal in Warren Country, North Carolina; a poor and majority African-American community (see e.g. [21–24]). Hence, environmental justice (EJ) is traditionally seen as a merger between civil rights movements and environmental groups in the USA.1 By the late twentieth and twenty-first centuries, EJ was adopted by urban environmental groups, indigenous land movements and socio-economic justice movements that rejected the conception of t

ps, indigenous land movements and socio-economic justice movements that rejected the conception of the environment as wilderness and entirely void of race and class issues ([29], pp., 22, - 23, [30,31]. Indeed, EJ is known to incorporate traditional and structural inequalities such as class, gender, race and ethnicity in their analyses of wider environmental injustices ([32], p. 80). In the Global North, the EJ discourse was predominantly preoccu- pied with a) marginalised communities that were

North, the EJ discourse was predominantly preoccu- pied with a) marginalised communities that were disproportionately affected by environmental risks and b) advocacy for green spaces and parks ([20], p. 360). In the Global South, EJ has been associated with the various organisations and movements expressing resistance to the entanglement of capitalist accumulation and environmental degrada- tion, e.g. deforestation in Brazil and hydrocarbon extraction in the Niger Delta [33]. CJ can be seen as

e.g. deforestation in Brazil and hydrocarbon extraction in the Niger Delta [33]. CJ can be seen as the natural outgrowth of environmental justice in the face of increasing evidence for anthropogenic global warming and ultimately represents the solidification of climate change activism in the 1990s [20,34,35]. The concept was first introduced to the international political arena in the Bali Principles of Climate Justice defined by Global South and North actors at the 2002 United Nations World Su

ples of Climate Justice defined by Global South and North actors at the 2002 United Nations World Summit on Sustainable Development in Johannesburg [36]. Early iterations of JT can be seen as a labour-oriented version of EJ 1 Other scholars indicate earlier instances of EJMs such as the United Farmworkers of America's resistance against community and worker exposure to pesticides in the late 1960s and 1970s [25,26] and the Italian experience with “class ecology” in relation to occupational healt

and 1970s [25,26] and the Italian experience with “class ecology” in relation to occupational health hazards in the 1970s [27,28]. 2 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c ia l S c i e n ce97(2023)102974 adopted by trade unions who were concerned about the impact of environmental regulations and standards on jobs [37]. In the context of climate change, JT recognises trade-offs between competing needs, e.g. employment and access to energy vis-a-vis climate priorities [7]. Ac- cording to McCauley &

s, e.g. employment and access to energy vis-a-vis climate priorities [7]. Ac- cording to McCauley & Heffron, JT then refers to “the development of principles, tools and agreements that ensure a fair and equitable tran- sition for all individuals and communities” ([10], p. 2). Trade-unions and labour organisations following the JT discourse are joining con- versations on climate change in order to promote the labour-friendly transformations of productive systems into low-carbon variants (Mor- ena

promote the labour-friendly transformations of productive systems into low-carbon variants (Mor- ena et al. in: Velicu & Barca [38]). Before discussing the approach to inequalities in the CJ and JT dis- courses, a brief introduction to inequality is conducive. Inequality generally refers to the difference between individuals or social groups in terms of their command over resources and opportunities [39,40]. es [39,40]. Many studies focus on differences in income and wealth, or economic inequality, since social well-being in capitalist societies is typically determined by one's purchasing power [41]. llows this line of thought and argues that the analytical consideration of inequalities as an outcome of C&LCTP is limiting in two ways. Advancing the discourse on ITPs Closely related to ITPs discussed in Section 3.3, are Transitional Assistance Policies (TAPs) introduced by Green & Gambhir [78]. The key difference between ITPs and TAPs pertains to the latter'

introduced by Green & Gambhir [78]. The key difference between ITPs and TAPs pertains to the latter's emphasis on the distributive and welfare impact of C&LCTP on a predefined group of agents (states, corporations, workers, communities and consumers) and disregard for “the manner and extent to which affected groups are recognised and included in processes and procedures” ([78], p. 11) – a consideration which is imperative for ITPs (see Section 3.3). TAPs are supposed to allow transitions to occu

ation which is imperative for ITPs (see Section 3.3). TAPs are supposed to allow transitions to occur in a more just, equitable and ‘smooth’ fashion and can be designed according to a specific objective and scope. A distinction is made between conservative and adaptive policy objectives. The former are backward looking and static. They are centered around the stabilisation of an agent's or group's interest over time by either partially or fully restoring the condition of said agent or group prio

interest over time by either partially or fully restoring the condition of said agent or group prior to transition-induced structural changes. Alternatively, adaptive objectives are forward-looking and dynamic. This means that agents and groups are facilitated in their adjustment to new circum- stances instead of stabilised. ion, particularly regarding the destination of tax revenue recycling. sed on the legal exemption of some agents or groups from transition laws (e.g. related to climate change mitigation). stance to individuals and groups. The adjustment assis- tance is conditional in that they are aimed at facilitating the adaptation or transformation of agents and groups to new economic conditions. conditional in- kind transfers are usually related to relocation, support and extension of training to workers who have lost jobs in hydrocarbon industries. GESI in energy planning Gender equality and social inclusion are human rights, and a vital requirement for equitable societal development [14]. With its focus on both gender equality and social inclusion, the GESI concept encom- passes more than

th its focus on both gender equality and social inclusion, the GESI concept encom- passes more than the equal rights, responsibilities, and opportunities for all genders but includes considerations of the needs and perspectives of young people, the elderly, people living with disabilities (PWD), ethnic minorities and nomadic communities, religious minorities, refugees, internally displaced people, remote-living people, the queer and intersex community, groups with less education, and low-income

remote-living people, the queer and intersex community, groups with less education, and low-income communities [38]. In this paper, these are referred to as people who are marginalised. 2 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c ie n c e 130 (2025) 104432 The concept is integral to many SDGs, including SDG5 on ‘gender equality’ and SDG16 on ‘inclusive societies’. With the SDGs calling to leave no one behind, GESI acts as a prerequisite to achieve all other SDGs. It is vital to also recogni

ve no one behind, GESI acts as a prerequisite to achieve all other SDGs. It is vital to also recognise that aspects of marginalisation may intersect, for example when a woman is disabled or elderly, or both [21]. This intersectionality implies that every person embodies different so- cial characteristics regarding their gender, race, sexuality, which do not operate in silos but are interdependent and affect a person’s life in their combination [7,39]. In the energy sector, GESI is important for

and affect a person’s life in their combination [7,39]. In the energy sector, GESI is important for the process of creating equitable and inclusive energy systems across the world. Every person’s way of accessing and using energy is individual, which creates a di- versity of needs that should be considered in planning [40]. The concept of GESI is often framed as a continuum comprising different ‘tiers’ (see Fig. 1), which indicate the degree to which gender equality and social inclusion are ackn

iers’ (see Fig. 1), which indicate the degree to which gender equality and social inclusion are acknowledged and integrated into policies, programmes and projects [41]. GESI-neutral approaches are seen to ignore existing inequalities, assuming interventions affect everyone equally, often reinforcing exclusion. For example, electrification projects that do not consult affected communities in their planning may overlook access barriers faced by marginalised groups. GESI-aware or -sensitive ap- pro

planning may overlook access barriers faced by marginalised groups. GESI-aware or -sensitive ap- proaches recognise inequalities but do not actively respond to or address them. For instance, a clean cooking initiative may acknowledge gender imbalances but not design the financing models or ownership structure to enable women to participate on an equal footing. GESI-responsive initiatives intentionally adapt to the different needs of marginalised groups to reduce disparities, such as ensuring tha

ally adapt to the different needs of marginalised groups to reduce disparities, such as ensuring that information and technologies on energy solutions are accessible to people with disabil- ities and information campaigns reach all people in rural areas as well as urban areas. At the most advanced end, GESI-transformative approaches seek to challenge and change the underlying power structures and norms that produce inequality. This may entail energy policy reforms that mandate representation of

norms that produce inequality. This may entail energy policy reforms that mandate representation of marginalised groups in energy planning and target structural barriers to energy access. In many LMICs, GESI in energy has gained attention among re- searchers and international networks, such as ENERGIA, over the last 30 years [42]. Despite these efforts and the widespread recognition of the importance of GESI importance for just energy transitions, the uptake of GESI-responsive policies remains s

nce of GESI importance for just energy transitions, the uptake of GESI-responsive policies remains slow [43,44], as does their imple- mentation [35]. Given this context, this paper adopts the GESI- responsive tier as the primary point of analysis along the GESI contin- uum. While impact begins at the GESI-sensitive tier where approaches may include consultation processes and an awareness of structures of marginalisation, such approaches may not result in structural or sus- tained transformation.

ures of marginalisation, such approaches may not result in structural or sus- tained transformation. GESI-transformative approaches, however, aim to challenge power dynamics and the roots of inequality. Between these two tiers, the GESI-responsive tier represents the minimum threshold at which positive impact can be meaningfully achieved by enabling in- clusive participation and decision-making. It is therefore a pragmatic and realistic benchmark, especially considering the current stage of poli

t is therefore a pragmatic and realistic benchmark, especially considering the current stage of policy and institutional development in the energy sectors of many LMICs. 2.1. of national level institutions, as well as the 47 county governments. The Constitution of Kenya 2010 devolved certain powers and re- sponsibilities from the national government to the newly-established 47 county governments to allow for more localised approaches to planning [18]. The Act requires county govern- ments to prepare their own County Energy Plans (CEPs) as part of the Integrated National Energy Planning (INEP) Framework [56]. amework [56]. However, minimal guidance has been provided to counties on the tools and planning approaches to assess county energy demand [57]. Due to their limited development, CEPs are not yet able to inform country-level en- ergy planning from the bottom up [13]. Furthermore, a lack of gender- disaggregated data in county-level energy planning has been found [58]. an important element of Kenya’s en- ergy policy, and the country has made significant progress in providing a growing population with access to energy. Challenges to further increasing electricity access in Kenya include high connection and supply costs, especially in rural and sparsely populated areas, inade- quate incentives for private investment, and insufficient capacity to meet demand [8,23,60]. GESI and energy in Kenya The constitutional and legislative foundations for GESI were estab- lished by the 2010 Constitution, which articulated equali

gislative foundations for GESI were estab- lished by the 2010 Constitution, which articulated equality principles in Articles 10, 27, and 53–57 that mandate affirmative action for margin- alised groups. Other legislative instruments followed, such as the Per- sons with Disabilities Act 2003 (amended 2023), which established protection frameworks subsequently referenced in energy access pro- grams. National policy frameworks have demonstrated evolving so- phistication in GESI approaches. The Nati

National policy frameworks have demonstrated evolving so- phistication in GESI approaches. The National Gender and Development Policy 2000 represented early gender mainstreaming efforts but remained largely gender focused. Its 2019 revision showed greater awareness of intersectionality, explicitly addressing women and girls, men and boys, PWDs, youth, older persons, and marginalised commu- nities [64]. The Ministry of Energy’s Gender Policy 2019 marked the energy sector’s first comprehensive GE

[64]. The Ministry of Energy’s Gender Policy 2019 marked the energy sector’s first comprehensive GESI framework. In its preface the Principal Secretary stated the intention of the policy “…to strengthen institutional capacities for gender mainstreaming as a basis for gender-sensitive project formulation, implementation, and impact evaluation of all energy projects in Kenya” (p.ii). While the policy defines gender equality broadly to include “absence of discrimination based on a person’s sex, ag

cy defines gender equality broadly to include “absence of discrimination based on a person’s sex, age, social class, religion, colour, race, or political affiliation” (p.40), implementation in- dicators focus primarily on women’s participation metrics rather than broader social inclusion [19]. Nonetheless, the policy remains pioneer- ing in efforts to mainstream gender across the energy sector. Introduction The world is at a crossroads as it must adapt to the evolving needs of a sustainable economy. This global shift demands concerted efforts to consolidate green energy and environmental regulations. In turn, Italy is no exception and is making every effort to achieve environmental tar- gets aligned with the UN 2030 Agenda (SDGs 13

is making every effort to achieve environmental tar- gets aligned with the UN 2030 Agenda (SDGs 13 & 8) to address the high unemployment rate of 7.8 % (Eurostat, 2023) and skill mismatches in the labour market. With the soaring momentum of the green transition around the globe, Italy has actively adopted renewables and reduced its carbon footprint. TR) trends from 1992 to 2020. A notable non-linear surge from around 8000 to 26,000 (KTOE) indicates a significant shift towards green energy adoption in Italy. Limited research has explored the relationship between employment dynamics and renewable energy adoption (Bali Swain et al., 2022; Azretbergenova et al., 2021; Fragcos and Paroussos, 2018; Hanson, 2023). The discourse regarding the impact of environmental policies on employment is circulating in liter- ary circles, driven by the surge in the global green shift. es on this matter. This study contributes to the existing literature in the following ways: (i) While many studies have considered one or two channels, this study offers a broader and integrated multidimensional linkage through exploring the empirical association between GETR, EPS and skill-biased labour demand in Italy, with a set of macroeconomic indicators

bour market. The present study fills this gap by examining the differing effects of environmental regulations and green energy transition on labour market segmentation (Niggli and Rutzer, 2020). Empirical underpinning The impact of environmental regulations on skill-biased labour is paramount in the contemporary global scenario. erved different relationships depending on the type of regulation, the industry, and the geographical context. Non-linear dynamics showing a U-shaped rela- tionship between environmental regulation and employment is reported by literature with gradual improvement of regulations, where the initial inhibitory effect transformed into a promotion effect once the inflexion point of a U-shaped curve is crossed, showing potential for dual dividend (Li & Du, 2021; Huang et al., 2023; Qin et al., 2022; W

crossed, showing potential for dual dividend (Li & Du, 2021; Huang et al., 2023; Qin et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2020). The literature further evidenced that rigorous ER induces innovation motivation in firms under regulatory control, leading to employment effects (Shi et al., 2024; Sun & Kim, 2019). alysing employment. applies the latest econometric techniques, including the novel Dynamic ARDL (DYARDL) and Frequency Domain Causality (FDC) approaches. data, and methodology 3.1. Model construction Our study is conducted within the theoretical framework of the green growth paradox, as introduced by Sinn (2008). This theory states that environmental policies that are intended to improve environmental sustainability may have short to mediumterm unintended negative impact on labour markets, especially for low-skilled labour. Stringent environmental regulations and a clean energy shift can lead to the transformation of sectors. This may result in

regulations and a clean energy shift can lead to the transformation of sectors. This may result in job losses in emission-intensive sectors and job creation in green-intensive sectors. Though the green energy transition aims to decouple economic growth from environmental hazards, these short to mediumterm repercussions for labour markets could be asymmetrical and exclusionary (Riom & Valero, 2024; Bluedorn et al., 2023). This approach is legitimate in understanding the diverse impact of EPS and GETR on employment prospects, wages and skill requirements in the labour force. Literature classified labour sk

loyment prospects, wages and skill requirements in the labour force. Literature classified labour skills by two methods (Vona et al., 2018; Marin & Vona, 2019; Zhong et al., 2021; Walker, 2011). The first criterion is the level of educational achievement. Labour that has received advanced education (from vocational colleges, universities, and graduate schools) is considered highly skilled Labour. On the other hand, individuals who have completed their schooling at high school, secondary vocation

On the other hand, individuals who have completed their schooling at high school, secondary vocational school, middle school, or primary school are classified as low-skilled labourers. The second criterion is technician achievement. Labour comprising technicians employed by science and technology-focused public interest organisations, pro- fessionals, or state-owned enterprises (SOEs) is regarded as highly trained workers (Marin & Vona, 2019; Song et al., 2019). These tech- nicians with higher e

as highly trained workers (Marin & Vona, 2019; Song et al., 2019). These tech- nicians with higher education are typically involved in R&D and tech- nologically advanced work. However, the number of technicians employed by state-owned enterprises (SOEs) and public interest orga- nisations is just a tiny portion of a country’s overall high-skilled labour force. Therefore, it cannot accurately reflect the actual employment of high-skilled Labour. Scholars opted for educational achievement with var

ct the actual employment of high-skilled Labour. Scholars opted for educational achievement with varying levels of expertise as a benchmark to categorise Labour (Hao & Fan, 2025; Zhong et al., 2021). This research, however, classifies HSL and LSL according to the International Standard Classification of Edu- cation (ISCED). Labour with tertiary education is considered HSL, and Labour with high school, secondary vocational school, middle, or pri- mary school completion who have not pursued furthe

Independent variables Environmental Policy Stringency Index (EPS): EPS is used by this study as a key explanatory variable by considering consistent attributes of industries. Due to the unavailability of direct environmental regulatory indices, researc

ributes of industries. Due to the unavailability of direct environmental regulatory indices, researchers resort to using a single indicator, such as energy price, carbon taxes, and treatment level of specific pollutants, to gauge the intensity of environmental regulation (Marin & Vona, 2019; Aldy, 2015; Levinson, 1996). However, this approach is overly limited and does not reflect the actual progress of environmental conditions. Therefore, this research employs a composite index of environmental

ess of environmental conditions. Therefore, this research employs a composite index of environmental policy stringency (EPS) by the OECD, offering a more comprehensive approach. It not only considers market and non-market-based regula- tions but also caters to tech-based regulations (See Fig. 4). The magni- tude of various policy measures (taxes, emission limitations, subsidies, etc) is assessed by the Environmental Performance Score. The score scale ranges from 0 to 6, where 0 counts for the le

y the Environmental Performance Score. The score scale ranges from 0 to 6, where 0 counts for the least strict and 6 for the most stringent. It combines quantitative and qualitative data to quantify the severity of environmental policy. For further details on EPS computa- tion, consult Kruse et al. (2022). Green energy transition (GETR): This study further opts for green energy transition (GETR) as a key explanatory variable, as it is backed by recent literature as a dependable and thorough meas

s a key explanatory variable, as it is backed by recent literature as a dependable and thorough measure of sustainable energy (Appiah et al., 2022). GETR is quantified based on the proportion of primary energy obtained from different renewable sources (Wind, Solar, Hydro, and other renewable energy sources). It aims to curb GHG emissions by promoting more green sources of energy, e.g., low-carbon power generation. 3.2.3. Control variables Control variables are often used to align work with relat

ometric strategy The empirical association of EPS, GETR, GTI, EG, INV, and TO on HSL and LSL is determined by this investigation. Econometric strategy mainly consists of five stages. Issues of potential heteroscedasticity are resolved by the conversion of central variables in logarithmic form. First, the stationarity of data is verified by conducting unit root tests (Dickey & Fuller, 1979; Phillips & Perron, 1988; Zivot & Andrews, 2002) to avoid spurious regression. Stationarity is checked at I

s & Perron, 1988; Zivot & Andrews, 2002) to avoid spurious regression. Second, the Pesaran et al. (2001)bound test is applied to determine the stability of long-term cointegration among the variables. the key technique of dynamic autoregressive distributive lag (DYARDL) by (Jordan & Philips, 2018) is used in this investigation to gauge actual short and long-term variations in response variables caused by explan- atory variables (Javed et al., 2025; Sarkodie et al., 2019; Khan et al., 2019). ying lag lengths for depen- dent and explanatory variables, in contrast to other cointegration tests, which often require identical lag lengths (Engle & Granger, 1987; Johansen & Juselius, 1990). Dynamic ARDL simulations The cuurnet study adopts a novel technique of dynamic autore- gressive distributive lag (DYARDL) (Jordan & Philips, 2018) to address the limitations of mainstream ARDL. Prior Literature used this technique in econometric and time series analysis (Ullah & Lin, 2024; Javed et al., 2023). It captures both short-term and long-term dynamics of the vari- ables, thereby providing a detailed picture of how variables interact immediately and over extended periods

thereby providing a detailed picture of how variables interact immediately and over extended periods (Javed et al., 2025; Villan- thenkodath & Pal, 2024; Ashfaq et al., 2024; Ali et al., 2022; Abbasi et al., 2021a,b,c; Pata & Isik, 2021; Islam et al., 2022). This technique is further advantageous for its proactivity in evaluating, simulating, and projecting plots for significant variations in regressors and their impact on dependent variables. essors and their impact on dependent variables. The DYARDL approach tested five thousand simulations of multivariate parameters using the standard distribution. Frequency domain causality test Finally, we used Breitung and Candelon’s (2006) spectral Granger-causality approach to determine the direction of causality. Granger causality has been widely used to study causal relationships despite its limitations. As the traditional Granger causality test (GCT) evaluates single-test statistics throughout time, it cannot investigate causality across frequencies (Go

uates single-test statistics throughout time, it cannot investigate causality across frequencies (Gorus & Aydin, 2019). When variables are correlated more often than once, traditional GCT fails to capture the causal relationship (Masset, 2008). For this reason, the current study used Granger causality in the frequency domain to show short, medium, and longterm causal linkages. This method helps forecast variable re- sponses at specific time frequencies (Abbasi et al., 2021a,b,c). In fre- quency

recast variable re- sponses at specific time frequencies (Abbasi et al., 2021a,b,c). In fre- quency domain analysis, the spectral density function reveals periodic oscillations in a data series (Gorus & Aydin, 2019). However, this method cannot estimate a model with an endless horizon due to its timing limitation (Abbasi et al., 2021a,b,c). Introduction Amid the pressing global urge to combat climate change, the Renewable Energy (RE) sector has experienced significant expansion [1]. However, this growth has not been universally beneficial; women’s participation in formal employment within this rapidly developing field significantly trails that of their male counterparts [2–5]. Furthermore, women in the sector are predominantly employed in lower-paid administrative

rts [2–5]. Furthermore, women in the sector are predominantly employed in lower-paid administrative and non-technical roles, rather than in higher-paid po- sitions that require advanced training and experience [3,6,7]. Conse- quently, there is growing concern that without prioritizing gender diversity, the RE transition could perpetuate and even exacerbate gender inequality rather than alleviate it [4,5,8]. Despite evidence suggesting that diversity fosters innovation and creativity, there has b

t [4,5,8]. Despite evidence suggesting that diversity fosters innovation and creativity, there has been minimal focus on promoting the inclusion of women within the RE workforce [5,9]. Background Evidence suggests that women perceive and experience spaces in ways that differ significantly from men [16–19]. Consequently, geog- raphy plays a crucial role in shaping women’s mobility patterns and behavioral choices, which in turn affects their job preferences and op- portunities [20,21]. For instance, the availability and characteristics of services, amenities, goods,

rtunities [20,21]. For instance, the availability and characteristics of services, amenities, goods, and industries in areas where women reside creates vital opportunities for their inclusion and retention in the workforce [21,22]. As observed by Sorenson and Dahl [23], if locations are more suitable for men’s employment than for women’s, this mismatch will result in lower wages for women in that region. In light of this, understanding how geography and spatial features influence women’s career

region. In light of this, understanding how geography and spatial features influence women’s career progression and employment opportunities is essential for developing policies and programs aimed at reducing the gender gap. For example, Rosenbloom [24] argues that understanding women’s travel patterns is crucial for uncovering the barriers that certain loca- tions may pose to their job access. Consistent with this argument, evi- dence worldwide has shown that women typically travel shorter dist

nsistent with this argument, evi- dence worldwide has shown that women typically travel shorter distances and spend less time commuting compared to men [21,25]. This difference in travel patterns is largely attributed to the nature of women’s trips, which are characterized by multiple stops [26–29]. In contrast, men’s trips tend to be more linear, often involving direct routes to a single destination [30]. The multi-stop nature of women’s trips is often attributed to the imbalance in household r

ion [30]. The multi-stop nature of women’s trips is often attributed to the imbalance in household responsibilities, including the frequent need to accompany children or elderly family members and to perform errands such as grocery shopping [21,27,31]. Additionally, recent studies have linked this tendency to economic disparity, which often makes women more reliant on public trans- portation or active transport [32]. These differences underscore the distinct daily logistical challenges and const

tive transport [32]. These differences underscore the distinct daily logistical challenges and constraints faced by women, reflecting broader societal roles and expectations that undoubtedly in- fluence their ability to access employment opportunities. This imbalance is even more pronounced in developing contexts, where limited infra- structure, inadequate transportation systems, and deeply entrenched societal norms exacerbate these challenges [33,34]. Furthermore, some studies even suggest that

ched societal norms exacerbate these challenges [33,34]. Furthermore, some studies even suggest that women are more likely than men to accept lower wages in exchange for shorter commutes, enabling them to fulfill household responsibilities [35]. This tendency contributes to an increased wage gap and fewer employment opportu- nities for women, often restricting them to jobs closer to home [36]. Similarly, when childcare options are limited, costly, or of poor quality, women are less likely to wor

ilarly, when childcare options are limited, costly, or of poor quality, women are less likely to work and more likely to hold low-quality jobs 2 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 120 (2025) 103917 [37]. As a result, the scarcity of essential services such as reliable public transportation, nearby childcare facilities, grocery stores, and health clinics often limits women’s access to high-paying or full-time employ- ment opportunities [15,21]. In turn, women are more likely

cess to high-paying or full-time employ- ment opportunities [15,21]. In turn, women are more likely to choose workplaces that require less commute time and where they can accommodate their needs as primary caregivers in the home [38]. Additionally, women often exhibit greater concerns about safety compared to men [39–41]. For instance, research, such as Ode Sang et al.’s [42] study in Gothenburg, Sweden, and Carli’s [43] analysis across 40 major cities in China, suggest that safety significantly

Sweden, and Carli’s [43] analysis across 40 major cities in China, suggest that safety significantly in- fluences women’s mobility and use of public spaces. Similarly, Mayen Huerta and Utomo [40] highlight that women in Latin America often experience heightened feelings of insecurity when navigating public spaces. Valentine [44] described this phenomenon as the “geography of fear,” highlighting how feelings of vulnerability shape women’s choices, mobility, and engagement with public spaces. The

w feelings of vulnerability shape women’s choices, mobility, and engagement with public spaces. The geography of fear compels women to adjust their behavior and travel patterns to avoid spaces that do not meet their specific safety and security needs, thereby influencing their job prospects [45]. This geography of fear is even more pronounced in countries where conflict and violence are persistent [46], intensifying the barriers women face in participating fully in the workforce. Evidence from s

[46], intensifying the barriers women face in participating fully in the workforce. Evidence from studies conducted worldwide has linked characteris- tics such as lighting, cleanliness, maintenance, order, comfort, and aesthetics of public spaces to women’s safety perceptions, with women tending to prioritize these attributes more than men [19,47,48]. For example, survey results from a study in Carmona, Spain, revealed that women place a higher value on the quality and safety features of public

armona, Spain, revealed that women place a higher value on the quality and safety features of public spaces [49]. Likewise, evidence from Londrina, Brazil, found that women often regulated their activities and limited their use of public spaces to certain times of the day due to perceived safety concerns, especially in poorly lit and poorly maintained areas [50]. Accordingly, women are more likely to utilize areas perceived as safer and of higher quality [49]. Given the significant impact of geo

to utilize areas perceived as safer and of higher quality [49]. Given the significant impact of geography on women’s mobility patterns and behavioral choices, a more nuanced understanding of how geography affects job opportunities requires the inclusion of spatial considerations in the analysis. This is particularly important when analyzing women’s employment and business opportunities in the RE sector, as its potential production and diffusion have a spatial dimension [51,52]. Understanding th

sector, as its potential production and diffusion have a spatial dimension [51,52]. Understanding the reasons behind the mismatch between po- tential RE sites and environments that encourage greater participation by women is crucial for bringing more women into leadership roles in the sector, thus ensuring more balanced opportunities. This is particu- larly important in developing contexts, where poor infrastructure, safety concerns, and deep-rooted cultural constraints often hinder women’s acce

poor infrastructure, safety concerns, and deep-rooted cultural constraints often hinder women’s access to remote job sites and professional development opportunities, negatively affecting their economic prospects [53–55]. Reducing the gender gap in these regions is crucial to ensuring a just and inclusive energy transition, empowering women to participate fully and equitably in the growing RE sector [56]. or [56]. From a policy standpoint, integrating geospatial data with sex- disaggregated economic and social statistics can significantly enhance the formulation of gender-sensitive policies and programs [57,58]. Such integration is crucial for accelerating RE deployment and fostering low- carbon economic growth [59]. Furthermore, incorporating spatial in- dicators into an employment assessment framework can significantly improve the evaluation of opportunities by providing a comprehensive underst

ework can significantly improve the evaluation of opportunities by providing a comprehensive understanding of the spatial dynamics that shape employment land- scapes [21]. This approach can enable the development of targeted in- terventions to promote gender equality in the workforce. 3.
