Mayen Huerta et al. small island de

be used as a case study for three key reasons. First, as a 2 C. Mayen Huerta et al. small island developing state (SIDS), St. Lucia is highly susceptible to the impacts of climate change, including sea-level rise, increased storm in- tensity, and other related hazards that could severely damage its infra- structure and economy [60–62]. Moreover, like many other middle- income SIDS, St. Lucia relies heavily on imported fossil fuels and is consequently vulnerable to the price volatility and geopo

es heavily on imported fossil fuels and is consequently vulnerable to the price volatility and geopolitical issues associated with these energy sources [63]. Therefore, transitioning to RE and ensuring that women are actively involved in the planning and implementation processes of this transition are both crucial for the country’s resilience and sustainable economic growth. This transition is urgent to reduce the country’s carbon footprint, enhance energy secu- rity, and mitigate the impact of

ent to reduce the country’s carbon footprint, enhance energy secu- rity, and mitigate the impact of volatile fossil fuel costs [63,64]. Second, Saint Lucia was chosen as a case study due to its challenging data landscape, allowing us to test the analytical framework’s applica- bility in countries with limited data availability. This aims to demon- strate that even in countries where comprehensive public geospatial data is not available, simple analyses can still be performed to better understand

public geospatial data is not available, simple analyses can still be performed to better understand the configuration of the environment. Moreover, by select- ing a country where many features remain unmapped, we can demon- strate the framework’s effectiveness and applicability to other low- and middle-income countries, including those that frequently encounter data completeness challenges. Third, Saint Lucian society has traditionally adhered to patriarchal norms, where women are expected to m

, Saint Lucian society has traditionally adhered to patriarchal norms, where women are expected to manage household responsibilities and caregiving roles [65,66]. These expectations can hinder women’s ability to pursue full-time employment or professional careers, espe- cially when jobs are far from home or essential services supporting their caregiving duties are unavailable. Therefore, understanding the specific factors needed in areas where RE jobs are available is crucial to increasing women

ding the specific factors needed in areas where RE jobs are available is crucial to increasing women’s participation in the field. Moreover, in Saint Lucia, the tourism sector dominates, offering women job opportunities pri- marily in hospitality and service industries [67]. However, these roles are often characterized by low wages, seasonal work, and limited po- tential for advancement [68]. To reduce the gender wage gap, it is essential to improve the conditions that enable women to access and

educe the gender wage gap, it is essential to improve the conditions that enable women to access and thrive in higher paying, more stable careers. Thus, a study of this nature within this context becomes highly relevant. Notably, in recent years, Saint Lucia has commenced its first large- scale clean energy project, a solar plant located near the international airport [69]. With a peak electricity demand of approximately 60 MW, this project represents a significant advancement for the island’s e

demand of approximately 60 MW, this project represents a significant advancement for the island’s energy infrastructure [69]. As such, this research will focus on analyzing the spatial barriers that limit job prospects around this major project, since the smaller solar power plants on the island provide fewer employment opportunities. As is the case in many Caribbean countries, women in Saint Lucia are scarcely involved in efforts to advance the energy tran- sition, which perpetuates existing i

ia are scarcely involved in efforts to advance the energy tran- sition, which perpetuates existing inequalities [4]. The ongoing energy transition offers a valuable opportunity to advance justice and address gender equality issues in the island [4]. If gender-blindness persists within energy systems, achieving these objectives may take considerably longer. 4. Methodology To address objective 1, we developed the Geospatial Women’s Employment Analytical Framework (GeoWEAF), which identifies key sp

developed the Geospatial Women’s Employment Analytical Framework (GeoWEAF), which identifies key spatial factors that foster a supportive environment for women to secure formal employment or business opportunities in the renewable energy sector. This framework was informed by an extensive literature review exploring the intersection of geography and women’s employment. Notably, we focused on labor markets in the fields of Science, Tech- nology, Engineering, and Mathematics (STEM), as this resea

r markets in the fields of Science, Tech- nology, Engineering, and Mathematics (STEM), as this research aims not only to assess the environment for women to access job opportunities but also to secure higher-paying positions that require advanced com- petencies. Additionally, we consulted three World Bank experts—a geospatial expert, a gender labor markets expert, and an energy sector 3 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 120 (2025) 103917 expert—to ensure the selected factor

y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 120 (2025) 103917 expert—to ensure the selected factors were appropriate and accurately reflected the spatial barriers faced by women in the RE sector. It is important to emphasize that the GeoWEAF was specifically developed with low- and middle-income contexts in mind, as the bar- riers women face in these settings are distinct from those in higher- income environments. In conducting the review, we identified rele- vant studies in low- and middle-in

ncome environments. In conducting the review, we identified rele- vant studies in low- and middle-income countries using targeted search terms and Boolean operators such as “spatial analysis AND women’s employment,” “geography AND renewable energy sector,” “gender eq- uity AND labor market access,” “spatial inequality AND employment opportunities,” “women’s economic empowerment AND spatial acces- sibility,” and “spatial distribution AND labor market participation” in databases like Google Schola

sibility,” and “spatial distribution AND labor market participation” in databases like Google Scholar, Scopus, and Web of Science. These search strategies ensured that we captured a wide range of literature addressing the spatial dimensions of women’s employment. The literature review followed a saturation approach, meaning that once key themes began to recur without the emergence of new insights, we deemed the review complete [70,71]. At that point, we categorized and classified all iden- tifie

we deemed the review complete [70,71]. At that point, we categorized and classified all iden- tified themes for further analysis. We identified 15 key spatially varying factors, which we categorized into three distinct dimensions based on their nature (see Fig. 1). Factors in the Contextual Dimension (CD) of the GeoWEAF pertain to the laws and policies that influence workplace gender discrimination, financial autonomy, and overall gender empowerment [72–76]. Factors in the Accessibility Dimensi

on, financial autonomy, and overall gender empowerment [72–76]. Factors in the Accessibility Dimension (AD) relate to women’s daily mobility and travel patterns. These factors consider the proximity from women’s homes to essential services, which is a crucial indicator of accessibility [77–79]. Finally, factors in the Place-Characterization Dimension (PD) pertain to the attributes of geographical locations or environments. These include walkability, safety, and vulnerability to natural disasters

locations or environments. These include walkability, safety, and vulnerability to natural disasters, all of which significantly impact women’s choices and freedom [45,80]. 4.1. Factors and rationale The following section outlines the GeoWEAF factors within each dimension, providing a brief rationale for their inclusion in the framework. 4.2. Contextual dimension 4.2.1. Workplace discrimination Gender biases and stereotypes in recruitment and workplace cultures often hinder women’s career progre

nder biases and stereotypes in recruitment and workplace cultures often hinder women’s career progression in male-dominated fields, such as the RE sector, by perpetuating gender prejudices [81,82]. Such discrimination can manifest in various forms, from hiring practices to professional development opportunities, impacting women’s employ- ment outcomes and job satisfaction [83]. Scholars like Emmons Allison et al. [3] argue that, particularly in the RE sector, the societal under- estimation of wo

s Allison et al. [3] argue that, particularly in the RE sector, the societal under- estimation of women’s competencies in technical fields, coupled with women’s justified expectations of mistreatment and harassment, present formidable obstacles to entry. 4.2.2. Regulatory frameworks (RF) Genuine equal opportunity relies on the effective legal protection of women’s employment rights and interests [84]. 84]. As such, variations in childcare support, parental leave, and other gender equality policies across regions can either enable or restrict women’s access to the workforce [85]. For instance, a study conducted in the U.S. analyzing the impact of different state maternity leave policies on mothers’ employment found that mothers in states with expanded access to job- protected leave were less likely to resign from their jobs [86]. Simi- larly, a study examining the impact of job protection poli

ely to resign from their jobs [86]. Simi- larly, a study examining the impact of job protection policies on gender differences in participation and wages found that such policies signifi- cantly increase the participation of mothers with children under the age of 6 [87]. ment and their ability to invest in training or business opportunities [6,72]. This issue is partic- ularly pronounced in sectors that often require substantial initial in- vestments, such as the RE sector [3]. As such, laws ensuring equal access to credit and banking services are critical in addressing disparities and empowering women to fully participate in economic opportunities. 4.2.4. Accessibility dimension 4.2.4.1. Women’s travel patterns (WTP). This factor addresses the unique travel beh

bility dimension 4.2.4.1. Women’s travel patterns (WTP). This factor addresses the unique travel behaviors and constraints faced by women, which are often influenced by their roles as caregivers and household re- sponsibilities [27,31]. Women’s travel involves multi-stop journeys for family and household-related errands, which are usually conducted during off-peak hours [26–29]. These errands often encompass visits to grocery stores, childcare services, parks, and pharmacies [90]. There- fore, t

n encompass visits to grocery stores, childcare services, parks, and pharmacies [90]. There- fore, the proximity of these services is crucial for effectively accommo- dating the daily needs of women. 4.2.4.2. Access to public transport. Particularly in low- and middle- income countries, due to reduced car ownership, the reliance on pub- lic transportation is more significant for women than men, with the availability and quality of such services playing a crucial role in their ability to access j

th the availability and quality of such services playing a crucial role in their ability to access jobs, education, and other essential services [15,45,91]. Dobbs [92] argues that the lack of high-quality, safe public transport can result in the social exclusion of women, particularly in terms of accessing employment opportunities outside of their neighborhoods. As such, having walking access to public transportation stops is crucial for increasing women’s participation in the labor market [93,9

ublic transportation stops is crucial for increasing women’s participation in the labor market [93,94]. 4.2.4.3. Access to education and training facilities. In developing contexts, women are more likely than men to rely on active transport, particularly walking, as their primary mode of transportation [45,104]. High walkability and well- connected streets can significantly facilitate women’s access to employment by providing safe and efficient travel options [105]. access to a diverse mix of land uses increases the likelihood that women will walk or cycle [104]. On the other hand, poorly maintained, unsafe, or non-walkable environ- ments—characterized by inadequate street lighting, lack of pedestrian paths, absence of ramps, and unsafe crossing points—often deter women’s mobility [45]. cial when assessing the physical barriers women face in accessing jobs. 4.2.5.2. Safety. which has repercussions at a macro level, including women’s ability and willingness to work. 4.2.5.3. Fragility, conflict, and violence (FCV). There is no doubt that political violence, conflict, inequality, and displacement are gendered, disproportionately affecting women and girls, and exposing them to higher levels of violence and exclusion [108,109]. es toward women pursuing STEM careers [115]. ion and communication technologies are increasingly important for accessing job opportunities, training, and financial services [117,118]. it facilitates the search for opportunities that are better suited to their skills and preferences [119]. Digital inclusion also enables women to effectively recruit qual- ified candidates for their businesses, expand their professional networks, and access remote work opportunities, further enhancing their partici- pation in the economy [120]. 4.2.5.6. Water and sanitation. The availability of clean water and sanitation facilities is a fundamental aspect of community health and well-being, aff

lean water and sanitation facilities is a fundamental aspect of community health and well-being, affecting women’s time allocation and their capacity to engage in employment outside the home [121]. Since women and girls in developing contexts are often tasked with domestic duties, they pre- dominantly bear the responsibility for water collection in areas where water and sanitation are limited [80]. As such, the availability of clean water and sanitation facilities is vital for promoting women’s

. As such, the availability of clean water and sanitation facilities is vital for promoting women’s empowerment. 4.2.5.7. Environmental hazards. Environmental and climatic conditions can impact the availability and stability of jobs, especially in sectors sensitive to environmental changes like agriculture and RE [122,123]. natural disasters and are significantly more vulnerable to their effects, including food insecurity [109,124]. Contextual dimension Since policies and laws are generally applied uniformly across a country, the data used to evaluate the factors in the CD were collected at the national level. The scores for all three CD factors were derived from the World Bank’s Women, Business, and the Law (WBL) study, which assesses various criteria on a scale from 0 to 100 [125]. These scores were then standardized to a scale of 0 to 5, where 0 represents the lowest score (0) and 5 represents the highest (100). 4.3.1.1. Workplace discrimination. This factor was assessed using the “Workplace” rating (score = 100) from the 2024 WBL report, which evaluates four key criteria to measure workplace discrimination in a country: (1) whether a woman can secure employment on the same terms as a man, (2) whether the law explicitly prohibits employment discrimination based on gender

e terms as a man, (2) whether the law explicitly prohibits employment discrimination based on gender, (3) whether there is legislation addressing sexual harassment in the workplace, and (4) whether there are criminal penalties or civil remedies for such harassment [125]. 4.3.1.2. Alongside the rapidly growing discourse on maximising opportunities from renewable energy expansion for Indigenous peoples, there is a concomitant need to build understanding of the value proposition of Indigenous co-ownership. This is a crucial step, given that current knowledge of the types and nature of present or planned equity partic- ipation of Indigenous groups globally remains sparse. Our paper addresses this gap by documenting patterns and trends of Indigenous peoples' co-ownership th

r paper addresses this gap by documenting patterns and trends of Indigenous peoples' co-ownership through the development and analysis of an original bespoke registry of large renewable energy projects globally. It poses three key questions: first, what trends and patterns of Indigenous co-ownership are emerging in the renewable energy sector? Second, where do local and national conditions drive, enable or equity in renewable energy projects? Third, in what areas is further research needed to bu

enable or equity in renewable energy projects? Third, in what areas is further research needed to build a collective, globally relevant understanding of the motivations for and risks of co-ownership as a means to economic, intergenerational independence for First Nations peoples? Our first-of-a-kind project registry highlights insights that are a necessary first step in understanding emerging modes of Indigenous peoples' engagement with the global energy transition. s of Indigenous peoples' engagement with the global energy transition. The paper ad- vances scholarship in this topic area by collating, curating, and critically reflecting on Indigenous co-ownership patterns and trends in renewable energy projects at a macro scale. In doing so, it offers foundational, evidence-based knowledge and a much-needed baseline that underlines gaps between on-the-ground realities and long-term aspirations for Indigenous self-determination and economic reconciliation. Policy context for First Nations' engagement with low-carbon energy projects The development and adoption of key international standards, such as the International Labour Organisation's (ILO) Convention 169, the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP), and the United Nations Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, has led to some progress in advancing respectful engagement w

inciples on Business and Human Rights, has led to some progress in advancing respectful engagement with Indigenous groups and organisations [11–14]. While non-binding, they offer guidance on the minimum standards essential for the survival, dignity and well-being of all Indigenous peoples. To navigate renewable energy developments on their lands and territories, Indigenous peoples are asserting their individual and collective rights to influence progress by demanding the right to give or withhol

eir individual and collective rights to influence progress by demanding the right to give or withhold free, prior and informed consent, and negotiate conditions for development that support safeguarding their way of life, health, and cultural existence [15]. In Australia, for example, a small minority of Indigenous groups who hold native title rights and interests in their lands have the legal ability to veto a renewable energy project on their lands. This effective veto power provides authority

lity to veto a renewable energy project on their lands. This effective veto power provides authority to First Nations peoples to demand potentially binding cultural heritage and environmental safeguards [16]. For the vast majority of Indigenous peoples in Australia, however, who have weak or no land rights, the need for the renewable energy sector to extend beyond compliance and build respectful relationships with the First Peoples remains urgent [17]. Within this context, governments and the pr

elationships with the First Peoples remains urgent [17]. Within this context, governments and the private sector show some progress in supporting systems and mechanisms that enable Indigenous peoples' economic participation in the low-carbon energy transition [17–19]. There is growing international policy consensus that First Nations peoples “must be enabled to self-determine how they lead, participate in, and benefit from the clean energy transition” [18]. ipate in, and benefit from the clean energy transition” [18]. participation, underpinned by its 2021 UNDRIP Act [21]. Methodological approach and empirical novelty This study began with the intention to develop an original global registry of large renewable energy projects with Indigenous co- ownership. The objective was to curate a comprehensive, reliable resource for analysing global and national trends and patterns of Indigenous co-owned projects in the renewable energy sector. While Indigenous-led organisations in some na

nous co-owned projects in the renewable energy sector. While Indigenous-led organisations in some nations such as Australia and Canada have invested in maintaining a database of renewable energy projects with Indigenous equity [50,51], these are scale-agnostic, ranging from small community-scale micro grids to medium and large scale projects, and include different modes of Indigenous participation that extend beyond co-ownership. These individual data sources also have a clear in-country focus,

that extend beyond co-ownership. These individual data sources also have a clear in-country focus, and follow their own methodological approach to project compilation. In the absence of a comprehensive, publicly shared global dataset, the project registry was curated and meticulously reviewed over a 14-month period between February 2024 and April 2025 to fill a critical gap in an area of emerging significance: First Nations' participation in large energy transition projects across regions. The

significance: First Nations' participation in large energy transition projects across regions. The research was approved by the University's Human Research Ethics Committee and adhered to high standards of research integrity throughout data collation, curation, and analysis. We focused on projects larger than 10 MW to capture renewable energy developments that reflect significant commercial engagement, thereby excluding smaller, community-scale projects [52]. Primary data sources included publi

ement, thereby excluding smaller, community-scale projects [52]. Primary data sources included publicly available information sourced online from formal project documentation, corporate and organisational websites, media releases, published reports, and peer-reviewed academic litera- ture. While efforts were made to identify relevant projects in all geog- raphies, including using searches in other languages, only one project was found in the Global South. Projects were included in the registry i

languages, only one project was found in the Global South. Projects were included in the registry if they met three criteria: i) they involved low-carbon energy technologies (wind, solar, hydro, geothermal, hydrogen, or “combined” including battery storage and/or transmission infrastructure); ii) they had an announced or operating capacity greater than 10 MW; and iii) they had a documented reference to Indigenous equity or co-ownership, including by First Nations, Aboriginal peoples, Traditiona

nce to Indigenous equity or co-ownership, including by First Nations, Aboriginal peoples, Traditional Owners, or specific Indigenous groups). The registry included projects across various stages of development: announced, in development, operational, or cancelled. Where possible, data was cross-referenced across multiple sources to validate accuracy. For example, to ensure we hadn't overlooked any projects, we conducted a cross-check of our dataset against existing (and continually updated) proj

projects, we conducted a cross-check of our dataset against existing (and continually updated) project lists maintained by country-based Indige- nous-led organisations. In cases of ambiguous or conflicting informa- tion, priority was given to the most recent and direct sources (e.g., project documentation was prioritised over media articles). The resulting registry includes 61 large renewable energy projects in Australia, Canada, the US, New Zealand and Argentina. This is a new registry, develo

rgy projects in Australia, Canada, the US, New Zealand and Argentina. This is a new registry, developed with the intent to address the objectives of this paper; as such, we do not claim that the registry is exhaustive. Given the highly dynamic nature of the sector, regular policy changes, commercial confidentiality, and the time-bound nature of data collection, our reg- istry may not capture all existing large projects or reflect their latest status beyond April 2025 (the end date for our data s

xisting large projects or reflect their latest status beyond April 2025 (the end date for our data search). There are additional limitations worth noting: the registry offers a macro-level view only, and the approach does not involve ground-truthing with First Nations communities and non-Indigenous actors. Our dataset of 61 projects provides a foundational resource for comparative and quantitative analysis. standardised analytical framework designed to map and understand: i) the diversity in project scale and type, ii) the nature of Indigenous ownership across different jurisdictions, and iii) spatial and temporal trends and patterns. Key variables included project location, size, development stage, primary technology, the year of commissioning (actual or planned), and ownership structure. These include a focus on i) the type of equity stake most commonly observed; ii) the heterogeneity of institutional, geographic, and market contexts; iii) the motivations of non-Indigenous project partners; and iv) the fast- paced nature of the energy market. Each of these is discussed below. 5.1. Engagement with large projects but … with a minority stake Our analysis demonstrates trends and patterns suggestive of growing Indigenous participation in the co-ownership of large (>500 MW) renewable

rns suggestive of growing Indigenous participation in the co-ownership of large (>500 MW) renewable energy projects. However, the results indicate that minority Indigenous equity is the most common form of co-ownership across large projects. This is unsurprising given that large projects are complex, involving significant human and financial resources and long-term time commitments, with returns on investment often materialising only after several years, if not decades. Consequently, they often rely on large public and private organisations to raise the bulk of project finance, provide loan guarantees, and absorb unforeseen risks. These factors enable private investors and large cor- porations to retain majority ownership stakes in large projects. Spectrum of contexts, policies and capabilities The results from our dataset point to a diverse spectrum of Indige- nous co-ownership patterns in the renewable energy space. These pat- terns highlight three specific strands of heterogeneity within national boundaries, bas

. These pat- terns highlight three specific strands of heterogeneity within national boundaries, based on renewable energy resource availability, socio- economic capabilities and institutional support. fferences manifest most strongly when comparing remote communities with little experience of, and exposure to, industrial activities on Indigenous peo- ples' lands and territories with a small number of relatively well- resourced Aboriginal corporations formed in the backdrop of large mining enterprises operating on Indigenous lands. ups are able to ca- talyse a unifying ambition to pursue pathways to self-determination. The Yindjibarndi Nation's foray into renewable energy project co- ownership in Western Australia's Pilbara region is a case in point [16]. As one of the first groups in the iron-ore rich Pilbara to be granted native title rights to exclusive and non-exclusive possession in 2017 (extending over 13,000 km2), the Yindjibarndi led early to identify appropriate partners to build and control renewable energy proje

Yindjibarndi led early to identify appropriate partners to build and control renewable energy projects on their lands. At the time of founding, the Yindjibarndi Energy Corporation (YEC), a partnership between ACEN and Yiyangu (100% owned by the Yindji- barndi people) was one of the largest Indigenous-led renewable energy initiatives in Australia. With a 25-50% equity across all projects, mandatory approval by the Yindjibarndi for project siting, preferred contracting and economic opportunities

y approval by the Yindjibarndi for project siting, preferred contracting and economic opportunities for the locals, and an MoU with Rio Tinto [57], a prospective downstream partner seeking renewable energy to decarbonise its iron-ore operations in the Pilbara, the Yindji- barndi equity project is widely considered a leading practice case in Australia. the YEC's experience is commonplace or readily replicable across Australia. Most Aboriginal organisations remain consistently under-resourced and lack the policy and financial support required to translate renewable energy resources on their lands and territories into sustainable social and eco- nomic development outcomes [56]. Likewise, diversity in policy support often shapes Indigenous groups' participation in large infrastructure project developments through co- ownership. Ontario in Canada

participation in large infrastructure project developments through co- ownership. Ontario in Canada demonstrates well the role of policy in enabling Indigenous co-ownership. es [48], established transmission infrastructure, a relatively high population density, and the presence of organised First Nations Groups (such as the Pic River First Nation). rojects [58]. Literature review 2.1. Theoretical background A wide range of studies has investigated the role of media in influ- encing people's perceptions e.g., [23,24]. In this regard, communication theories (such as agenda-setting or framing theories) help to explain how media can influence public opinio

s (such as agenda-setting or framing theories) help to explain how media can influence public opinion by raising individual awareness. Agenda-setting theory [9] posits that the media plays a central role in shaping public priorities by selecting which issues to highlight in the news. When the media chooses to include certain themes and not others in its coverage, it can elevate them from private awareness to public concern. Numerous studies have shown that sustained media attention toward specif

wareness to public concern. Numerous studies have shown that sustained media attention toward specific themes can make the audience particularly involved in those themes [25]. Repetitive media coverage of specific topics in- fluences how the public prioritizes those issues [25,26]. At the same time, media theories suggest that not only what the media talks about influences public perceptions, but also how it is dis- cussed. In this regard, framing theory argues that “frames” (defined as interpre

also how it is dis- cussed. In this regard, framing theory argues that “frames” (defined as interpretive structures that emphasize particular aspects of a theme) guide audience understanding and emotional responses [10]. In the energy domain, how a theme is framed (whether as an economic benefit, cost, or a matter of national investment) shapes how the public and policymakers interpret its relevance and implications [27]. 2 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c ia l S c i e n c e 128 (2025) 104345

ce and implications [27]. 2 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c ia l S c i e n c e 128 (2025) 104345 To summarize, according to agenda-setting and framing theories, the media can shape public opinion and influence how people perceive is- sues. News coverage acts as a sociocultural driver, shaping perceptions of urgency, legitimacy, and feasibility. Drawing on these theories, our aim is twofold. First, we want to understand the level of importance that leading news sources in seven European count

rst, we want to understand the level of importance that leading news sources in seven European countries assign to the topic of energy. This helps us assess whether the media elevates energy concerns to the level of a public issue. Second, we want to examine how this topic is portrayed. In other words, when energy is discussed, we want to un- derstand which aspects receive the most attention (economic, implementation-related, or the types of energy sources). omic, implementation-related, or the types of energy sources). This helps us understand how the framing of the issue influences public perception. 2.2. Methodology To measure and compare the semantic importance of different energy-related themes (ERTs) in media discourse across countries, we selected widely read and well-established online newspapers for each country in our sample. ell-established online newspapers for each country in our sample. Our selection covers seven languages, English, Italian, Swedish, Spanish, German, French, and Greek, and includes some of the most important newspapers in terms of circulation. Our focus is not to capture the full diversity of viewpoints across the media

terms of circulation. Our focus is not to capture the full diversity of viewpoints across the media landscape but to analyze the energy discourse within the most widely circulated and consumed news sources. This choice reflects our specific objective: to examine the agenda-setting potential of dominant media outlets. Therefore, the results should be interpreted with this in mind. Evaluating semantic importance After filtering the articles, we leveraged the Semantic Brand Score (SBS) to quantify the importance of ERTs in their content. The SBS is a metric for capturing the importance and memorability of words and concepts within large text corpora [41]. This indicator encompasses three key dimensions – prevalence, diversity, and connect

t corpora [41]. This indicator encompasses three key dimensions – prevalence, diversity, and connectivity – briefly summarized in this section. According to these metrics, the importance attributed to a word is high when (i) it has frequent mentions, denoting prevalence; (ii) it exhibits diverse and more distinctive textual associa- tions, reflecting diversity; and (iii) it can act as a bridge across different discourse themes (connectivity). Prevalence measures the frequency with which a specif

oss different discourse themes (connectivity). Prevalence measures the frequency with which a specific word or set of words appears in a corpus. A higher prevalence suggests that a term is more frequently featured in public discourse, increasing its visibility. Frequent exposure to certain words enhances recognition and recall, making these concepts more salient in the reader's mind. Diversity assesses the variety and distinctiveness of the terms linked to each word/concept under analysis, while

esses the variety and distinctiveness of the terms linked to each word/concept under analysis, while connec- tivity gauges a word or concept's capacity to bridge various discourse topics. The formulas for calculating diversity and connectivity draw inspiration from centrality metrics within social network analysis, spe- cifically distinctiveness centrality and weighted betweenness centrality, respectively [42,43]. These calculations necessitate an initial trans- formation of the text documents i

ectively [42,43]. These calculations necessitate an initial trans- formation of the text documents into a network of co-occurring words after text preprocessing, carried out using the SBS BI web app [44]. Text preprocessing involves multiple steps to standardize and clean up the text, including the removal of stopwords (common words such as ‘and’ or ‘the’ that carry little semantic value), punctuation, and special characters. The text is also converted to lowercase to ensure uniformity and avoid

tion, and special characters. The text is also converted to lowercase to ensure uniformity and avoid duplicate entries due to case differences. Additionally, words are stemmed – a process that reduces words to their root form (e.g., ‘running’ to ‘run’) – to consolidate variations of the same term [45]. While root stemming is commonly used for English, we acknowledge that it may be less effective in capturing morphological nuances in other languages within our corpus, such as Italian, German, Fre

n capturing morphological nuances in other languages within our corpus, such as Italian, German, French, and Spanish. In these cases, lemmatization or dictionary-based stemming could offer greater linguistic precision. However, they typically require more computational resources and language-specific tools. Given that we did not observe substantial differences in the final outputs across languages, we used stemming for consistency and efficiency. However, future research could further explore th

, we used stemming for consistency and efficiency. However, future research could further explore these alternatives to assess their potential benefits. Finally, the text is tokenized, meaning it is split into individual words or meaningful units, enabling the construction of a structured network of word co-occurrences. Endogeneity concerns The observed correlation between media importance and renewable energy production could suffer from endogeneity. It is plausible that increases in renewable energy production may lead to more media attention (reverse causality) or that both variables respond to omitted time-varying shocks, such a

attention (reverse causality) or that both variables respond to omitted time-varying shocks, such as changes in national energy policy or in- ternational events. We adopt an instrumental variables (IV) approach to address these concerns. We use the World Press Freedom Index (WPFI) as an instrument for the SBS variables of media importance. The WPFI, published annually by Reporters Without Borders, measures the degree of journalistic freedom in each country [51]. In countries with greater press

, measures the degree of journalistic freedom in each country [51]. In countries with greater press freedom, journalists are more likely to independently report on public-interest issues, including energy-related topics, thus exogenously increasing the visi- bility of such topics in the media [52]. Second, conditional on economic and institutional controls, press freedom should not directly affect renewable electricity production except through its influence on media narratives. We use a two-sta

renewable electricity production except through its influence on media narratives. We use a two-stage least squares (2SLS) approach to estimate the effect of each SBS cluster. In the first stage, we regress the endogenous variable (i.e., the SBS values) on the WPFI scores. We then use the resulting fitted values as explanatory variables in the second-stage regression. In this second stage, the dependent variable is the share of renewable energy, while the main explanatory variables are the fitt

pendent variable is the share of renewable energy, while the main explanatory variables are the fitted SBS values from the first stage, including lags from 0 to 12. To ensure robust inference we compute standard errors using the wild bootstrap method clustered at the country level. This approach provides more reliable inference in the 2SLS setting [53]. 2 Since the SBS metric comes from the aggregation of counting variable, its skewed distribution is expected. The definition of this concept remains contested, as there is no uni- versally accepted consensus on its meaning. Historical perspectives have linked energy poverty to “fuel poverty,” which represents the inability to maintain enough warmth in the home [18]. However, some research

,” which represents the inability to maintain enough warmth in the home [18]. However, some researchers consider the terms synonymous, while others distinguish them [19]. Early studies linked energy poverty to household income, high- lighting the vulnerability of low-income households to afford energy expenditures [20]. This approach was prevalent in developed countries that proposed expenditure-based indicators to compute the energy poverty. Most studies define energy poverty as no access to mo

ased indicators to compute the energy poverty. Most studies define energy poverty as no access to modern energy facilities, like clean cooking, electricity, and commercial energy [21]. This definition is further expanded by Parajuli, who connects it to broader social and economic development goals [22]. From this perspective, energy poverty is seen as a concept with more dimensions: a subjective component based on household members’ perception of inadequate warmth during cold periods and an obje

omponent based on household members’ perception of inadequate warmth during cold periods and an objective component based on the net income of a household after energy ex- penditures falling under the state poverty line and proper energy costs exceeding the average level for that country [23]. Multidimensional factors that influence energy poverty include more indicators on social, economic, and environmental development. Boardman identifies economic and social factors that characterize the hous

nvironmental development. Boardman identifies economic and social factors that characterize the household, energy prices, and efficiency as key multidimensional factors [21]. Recalde et al. emphasize the importance of structural determinants of energy poverty to encompass the multidimensional perspective: economic, labor, and market policies, and energy housing [24]. Nussbaumer et al. proposed an energy poverty index based on the IEA Energy Development Index [25]. This index measures a household

energy poverty index based on the IEA Energy Development Index [25]. This index measures a household’s inability to have energy but also considers economic and social aspects beyond accessibility, affordability, and use of energy for various ne- cessities [26]. Energy poverty is computed by employing three approaches: phys- ical, technological, and economic threshold. The physical threshold approach aligns with the World Bank’s definition of absolute poverty, establishing a minimum level of ener

ch aligns with the World Bank’s definition of absolute poverty, establishing a minimum level of energy use to ensure primary needs [22]. The technological threshold version focuses on the impossibility of reaching energy services, while the economic method establishes the highest percentage of income necessary for an acceptable quantity of energy expenditure. These approaches face limitations, such as the lack of available datasets to measure energy consumption for basic neces- sities, differenc

h as the lack of available datasets to measure energy consumption for basic neces- sities, differences in measuring adequate temperature, and disparities between energy prices and purchasing power. Empirical studies in literature propose various indicators to measure energy poverty depending on data availability. Some studies focus on access to different types of energy, while others focus on access to electricity, modern cooking technologies, modern energy fuels, adequate heating, arrears on en

ss to electricity, modern cooking technologies, modern energy fuels, adequate heating, arrears on energy bills, health issues generated by inadequate heating possibilities, dwelling with leakages and damp walls, and unavailability of other basic necessities. ce tests are crucial tools in panel data analysis because they address a potential issue that can significantly affect the reliability and interpretability of results. For example, spatial dependence and common effects might generate cross-sectional dependence. • Unobserved common shocks: Events like recessions, financial cri- ses, or natural disasters can simultaneously affect multiple in- dividuals in a panel. • Spatial dependence: Individuals residing in close proximity (e.g., neighboring cou

als in a panel. • Spatial dependence: Individuals residing in close proximity (e.g., neighboring countries or states) might be influenced by each other’s economic activities. Amazonia Legal (Legal Amazon) comprises 9 states, 7 of which are in the North region. Most of Brazil's energy comes from hydroelectric power plants in the Amazon and the tropical savannas. ity, and its biome pro- vides continued opportunities for carbon-sink, as well as a buffer against climate change. It stores about 123 billion tonnes of carbon [6] and its trees release 20 billion tonnes of water daily into the atmosphere [7]. By producing rain clouds, cooling temperatures and absorbing carbon, the entire biome performs a vital role not only locally, but for the entire continent, as well as the world. as well as the world. The study explores how the choice of energy solutions under the LPT and MLA programs can be re-focused to meet vital needs of rural and forest Indigenous communities, the majority of whom live in the state of Amazonas, without degrading the ecological system and disrupting local cultures. to conceptualize the potential benefits of distributed generation using renewable energy and achieve the following objectives – to (i) improve health and prospects for self- Fig. 1. y level, (ii) re-think the ecolog- ical footprint of local and regional energy infrastructure (both physical and social) for ecologically compatible trajectories, and (iii) support climate and sustainability agendas primarily at local and regional levels. More generally, the study sheds light on the linkages between carbon- free, off-grid (mini- or micro-grid) solutions and local bioeconomies, where the value of distributed renewable energy generation can be appreciated beyond the Amazonian biom

re the value of distributed renewable energy generation can be appreciated beyond the Amazonian biome. 1.1. Renewable energy for biocultural survival: Leap or a natural fit? Brazil's energy sector is one of the least carbon-intensive in the world, since around 46 % of total energy supply comes from renewables [15]. Besides pledging to reach net-zero emissions by 2050, Brazil committed to achieve zero illegal deforestation target by 2028 [10]. In the electricity sector, current initiatives are un

ero illegal deforestation target by 2028 [10]. In the electricity sector, current initiatives are underway aiming to spearhead the energy transition under a 4D-model - digitalization, decentralization, decarbonization, and democratization. The two-fold research question pursued in the study is, first - do the current approaches to bridging universal access in Amazonia's Indigenous territories include locally co-designed renewable energy systems that are culturally appropriate and ecologically co

ude locally co-designed renewable energy systems that are culturally appropriate and ecologically compatible? Second, under which conditions would distributed generation using renewable technologies make the energy transition more equitable (and democratic) for Indigenous communities? Energy policy and cultural survival are connected in specific ways, which are implicated in the competing priorities evident in different electrification approaches impacting the Amazon biome. First, it is the expa

es evident in different electrification approaches impacting the Amazon biome. First, it is the expansion of the national grid closer to the biome aiming to ensure electricity supply at the national level. Second, it is the expansion of access to SIN through competitive contracting for additional generation primarily benefiting extractive industries (metal and metallurgical sec- tors, chemical and mineral extractives). Third, it is the installed capacity of sistemas isolados (SISOL) (isolated sy

mineral extractives). Third, it is the installed capacity of sistemas isolados (SISOL) (isolated systems not connected to the grid) and the choice of fuel configuration to meet the increase in demand. Fourth, it is reaching universal access and meeting energy needs of communities currently without access, which implies adding new en- ergy infrastructures. All four approaches (particularly first and second), can incentivize economic activities with higher carbon and ecological footprint and resu

nd second), can incentivize economic activities with higher carbon and ecological footprint and result in further degradation of the Amazonian biome on which forest communities depend for their survival. The underpinning priorities therefore need to be coordinated across sectors and scales. Similarly, the choice of LPT and MLA energy solutions and technologies will either come with a higher carbon and ecological footprint (particularly when based on fossil fuel sourcing), or can be co-designed w

and ecological footprint (particularly when based on fossil fuel sourcing), or can be co-designed with local communities in ways that are ecologically and bioculturally appro- priate. To limit the scope of the study, the research focus remains on small-scale innovative energy solutions for under-served communities in the Legal Amazon (i.e., the third and fourth approach) rather than on innovation tasked with accelerating large-scale renewable electricity production (i.e., the first and second a

tasked with accelerating large-scale renewable electricity production (i.e., the first and second approach). 1.2. Structure of the article In the Introduction, the study introduces the research topic, research questions, and aims of the study. Contributions of the study to the field of social science energy research are articulated (Contribution) and limitations stated (Limitations). The Methods section outlines the multi- step literature review methodology, and is followed by Conceptual framew

section outlines the multi- step literature review methodology, and is followed by Conceptual framework, where contextually relevant factors, definitions and cate- gories are explicated. Under Results, current energy policy approaches and energy solutions are presented, mostly framed as techno-economic, leading to a Discussion where the importance of contextual factors, relevant for energy system design in ecologically and culturally unique contexts, is corroborated. Under Conclusions, the most

design in ecologically and culturally unique contexts, is corroborated. Under Conclusions, the most important find- ings and their implications for the future of the Amazon biome and 2 N. Hampl Indigenous cultures are summarized. 1.3. Contribution The study makes the following contributions to the field of social science energy research. First, it clearly articulates a conceptual link between the broadly-agreed upon commitment to conserve the Amazon (the biome and its cultures) and renewable ene

broadly-agreed upon commitment to conserve the Amazon (the biome and its cultures) and renewable energy as a means of achieving it. This perspective builds upon prior energy research that bridges technical and social science concepts to generate new alterna- tives in energy systems design, aiming to benefit both human and ecological systems. In this cross-disciplinary energy domain, renewable energy is situated at the center of hyperlocal bioeconomies and follows culturally appropriate managemen

gy is situated at the center of hyperlocal bioeconomies and follows culturally appropriate management principles of the natural environ- ment. Second, the research contributes insights that address the puzzle as to why local energy demand in the Amazon is still met by imported fossil fuels (rather than locally sourced renewable energy). Third, the recommended approach speaks to the market gap that currently exists in the deployment of context-specific, ecologically-informed distributed- energy s

currently exists in the deployment of context-specific, ecologically-informed distributed- energy systems, with opportunities for local involvement in an inde- pendent, decentralized energy generation and management. In the research context introduced here, the demand addressed is for scaled- down, innovative renewable energy solutions whose objective is to help ensure survival of both the Amazonian Indigenous communities and the biome they occupy. 1.4. Limitations Because of the heterogeneity of both the Amazon biome and its cultures, the study does not propose an off-the-shelf solution, since one universal solution could not possibly match all contexts at micro- regional level. The Amazon biogeographic region expands beyond the larger Amazon River basin and comprises diverse ecosystems including rainforests, seasonal forests, deciduous forests, flooded forests, and sa- vannas. including rainforests, seasonal forests, deciduous forests, flooded forests, and sa- vannas. Within this bioregion, 385 distinct ethnic groups who reside there amount to an estimated 1.5 million peoples. Given the cultural and ecological diversity, it is beyond the scope of the study to discuss the full range of alternative energy solutions for the different cultural and ecological contexts. Legal Amazonia Legal Amazonia refers to a political definition comprising 9 Brazilian states instituted by the Brazilian government in 1953 to promote economic and social development in the region. However, since its founding, development pathways have been contested, as land conver- sion and large-scale development projects re

velopment pathways have been contested, as land conver- sion and large-scale development projects resulted in deforestation, environmental degradation and damage to ecological systems. Social and environmental impacts of hydroelectric development have been well documented over the past 40 years, ranging from earlier accounts of the Balbina dam, to Tucuruí dam, Samuel dam, Sao Luiz do Tapajos dam, Belo Monte and others in the Xingu River Basin [20,21]. Most importantly, the aggregate impacts (e.g

Belo Monte and others in the Xingu River Basin [20,21]. Most importantly, the aggregate impacts (e.g., displacement and livelihoods lost due to an interrupted access to basic provisions such as water and fish) contributed to a reduced social resilience for biocultural survival [22]. Moving forward, new hydropower development projects (i.e., Cahuela Esperanza dam, Guajara Mirim dam) will have trans- boundary effects (Brazil-Bolivia) and generate more of the anticipated adverse outcomes for Indige

- boundary effects (Brazil-Bolivia) and generate more of the anticipated adverse outcomes for Indigenous cultures and the ecological systems. The ecological damage (i.e., ecological incompatibility) thus comes at the cost of wellbeing (i.e., biocultural incompatibility) of local Indigenous communities, expressed in the interlinked social and ecological crises. 3.1.3. Illicit economies Apart from legal economic activities that can act as stressors on Indigenous livelihoods, illegal activities—in some cases taken up by local communities as poverty alleviation strategies—have been expanding in parallel. Compounded effects of both legal and illegal mining driven by high commodity prices (e.g., gold and diamonds) in Legal Amazonia (e.g., by garimpeiros in Yanomamí and other Indigenous lands [28]) include water

in Legal Amazonia (e.g., by garimpeiros in Yanomamí and other Indigenous lands [28]) include water and soil contamination by mercury and metalloids [29], in addition to other undisclosed chemicals that may be released into the environment. ities [30], particularly those living around river basins and river tributaries, and directly consuming untreated water [31]. In these contexts, the energy system should be designed to meet critical community needs (i.e., to provide enough power from renewable sources for water treatment, basic operations of health clinics, and community centers). Carbon emissions For the last three decades, land use, land-use change and forestry (LULUCF) sector in Brazil has been emitting more carbon than it has sequestered – emissions that account for the released underground carbon resulting from deforestation in the Amazon

issions that account for the released underground carbon resulting from deforestation in the Amazon rainforest and the loss of the savannas in the eastern part of the country [32]. Climate scientists monitoring the Amazon biome argue that the combination of deforestation, wildfires and cattle raising has already transformed the Amazon from a carbon sink to a carbon source – in 2021 alone, the Brazilian Amazon captured less than half of its emissions, leaving a surplus of 740 million tons of CO2

ilian Amazon captured less than half of its emissions, leaving a surplus of 740 million tons of CO2 equivalent (42 % of Brazil's net emissions) [33]. In Legal Amazonia (1985–2020), the highest emissions occurred in southern and eastern Para´ (municipality Sao Felix do Xingu) and in the least populated state, Roraima (municipality of Porto Velho), in both cases owing to forest-to-land conversion for cattle and mono- culture (i.e., soybean and corn) farms. r cattle and mono- culture (i.e., soybean and corn) farms. Combined with Altamira (a mu- nicipality in central-east region), the three municipalities emitted over 2.1 billion gross tons of CO2 equivalent (1985–2020) [id.]. The same three were responsible for one fifth of all deforestation in Brazil's Amazon in the year 2020, meaning that encroachment on the forest in the same areas has not been subsiding [34]. Emissions are high in Mato Grosso, Tocantis, Maranha˜o, and in Rondonia, particularly

bsiding [34]. Emissions are high in Mato Grosso, Tocantis, Maranha˜o, and in Rondonia, particularly along the border with the state of Amazonas, and also around BR-319 highway [35], as predicted in prior research [36]. In the Amazonas state, higher emissions are observable in the eastern part and around the capital Manaus, and lower in the western part of the state. ed to higher incidence of wildfires [37,38], the energy sys- tems should support economic activities (local circular bioeconomies) that could outcompete incentives for deforestation. 3.1.7. Past injustices At the local level of encounter with Amazonian Indigenous com- munities, various tactics have been used to coopt communities into projects promoted by extractive companies and the state who treat op- position and territorial struggle as a “technical-managerial issue” [39] instead of an opportu

t op- position and territorial struggle as a “technical-managerial issue” [39] instead of an opportunity for democratization and political empower- ment [40], or an opportunity to improve corporate social responsibility (CSR) and environmental social governance (ESG) [41]. The assumption of the study is that energy futures can only be equitable when past in- equities become dis-embedded from the energy system design and its structural features. When these inequities are identified, the energy sy

nergy system design and its structural features. When these inequities are identified, the energy system design can be re-focused on providing specific equitable features (i.e., ownership structure, fuel sourcing). For example, energy solutions for demarcated Indigenous lands can consider principles of circularity within specific territories and focus on specific biocultural livelihood needs. 3.1.8. Existential imperative There is an added urgency (an existential imperative) [42] to make the right choices about energy solutions in Brazil's Amazon, since the multi-scalar approaches in energy planning and their infrastructures will produce specific trajectories that will either enable or disable culturally and ecologically compatible livelihoods, with high stakes beyond the Amazon. The core assumption is that “business-as-usual” solutions will only speed up the pace at which adverse outcom

assumption is that “business-as-usual” solutions will only speed up the pace at which adverse outcomes will be delivered. Put differently, by replicating the same flaws in the energy system design (e.g., not accounting for critically important local/regional factors), further destruction of the biome and its cultures will be enabled rather than prevented – in contradiction to climate, energy, and biodiversity targets [43]. 6 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c ie n c e112(2024)103491 4

Renewable energy in Brazil Brazil has five macroeconomic regions, four electricity regions, 27 federal levels (26 states and the Federal district Brasília), and 5570 municipalities. At the national level, the National Interconnected System (SIN) integrates primarily hydro-wind-thermal-biomass sources and extends to all Brazil's states except Roraima, and supplies electricity

thermal-biomass sources and extends to all Brazil's states except Roraima, and supplies electricity to about 98 % of Brazil's population [4]. The installed generation capacity in the SIN comprises mainly hydroelectric plants in 16 hydro- graphic basins within different regions of the country. Contingent upon hydrological conditions, thermal power plants near load centers dispatch power and manage reservoir water levels during dry or rain seasons. Over the past 15 years, the participation of biom

reservoir water levels during dry or rain seasons. Over the past 15 years, the participation of biomass in electricity generation has nearly doubled to reach 13.7 % of the installed capacity in electricity matrix (25 GW as of 2023), where sugarcane bagass is the primary biomass source [44]. Equitable energy policies Law No. 10438 of 2002 guarantees universal access to public elec- tricity services. Enacted by Normative Resolution No. 414 (ANEEL), all urban areas today have electricity access, as well as rural areas in the South, Southeast, and most states in the Northeast and Midwest region. s in the South, Southeast, and most states in the Northeast and Midwest region. Regulation No. 482 makes it possible for consumers to generate their own electricity and negotiate the supply of generated surplus to local distribution network. rated surplus to local distribution network. Distributed energy resources (DERs) comprise a combination of distributed generation, storage and digitalization that allow households to generate, consume and reduce their electricity consumption, with partial or no participation of other agents (e.g., utility companies). 4.3.1. and community water wells is included in the program. Institutional protections and territorial rights Territorial rights are directly linked to the notion of equitable and democratic energy transition, as they set the framework for neg

inked to the notion of equitable and democratic energy transition, as they set the framework for negotiations and power dynamics in local—extra-local interactions. Strong land rights are negatively co-related to deforestation and forest degradation (i.e., the stronger the land rights granted to Indigenous peoples, the less deforestation was observed) [41,75–77]. In practically all the Amazo- nian countries of South America, Indigenous and tribal territories have a lower average deforestation tha

countries of South America, Indigenous and tribal territories have a lower average deforestation than forests not managed by Indigenous peoples [25]. Forests under common tenure are also associated with good outcomes from the forest conservation and livelihood perspectives, benefitting both community wellbeing and forest conservation [75–77]. However, some claimed Indigenous lands—the largest landmass in the state of Amazonas—have not yet been titled nor awarded territorial rights, which has in

mass in the state of Amazonas—have not yet been titled nor awarded territorial rights, which has in turn incentivized licit and illicit extractivist eco- nomic activities in the contested areas [78–80]. trus- ted actors, choice of energy technologies and infrastructures, right of refusal, benefit-sharing, but also, an improved physical security [81,82]. g universal access in Amazonia's Indig- enous territories include locally co-designed renewable energy systems that are culturally appropriate and ecologically compatible? 5.1.1. Centralized energy systems Though centralized systems are still promoted, they were not identified as an optimal way of reaching universal access in remote areas of the Amazon. SIN, or by extending gas pipeline networks into the Amazonian biome, tested energy solutions include carbon-free microgrids using local renewable energy sources [88,89]. The empirical evidence presented under Results demonstrates that decentr

t DER more quickly [90,91]. eaningful participation in the system design by the communities themselves is necessary to meet the demand for self-governance and self-determined development in TIs [58,92,93]. 5.1.2. Distributed energy systems Recent energy research considers distributed energy systems sourc- ing renewable energy in energy modeling in remote regions [44,61,62]. rimarily intended to optimize a planning process and identify the cheapest alternative—do not factor in all contextually relevant aspects (e.g., value/worth of the Amazon biome, value of the unique Amazon bioculture [94], or territorial governance in Indigenous lands). Without “pricing-in” the non-monetary value of the biome, the existence of abundant reserves of fossil fuel resources will always price out renew- ables due to their higher initial capital costs. What could emerge as an optimal so

price out renew- ables due to their higher initial capital costs. d as local access to fossil fuels solving the issue of costly imports, and in this way boosting social resilience, all while precipitating the destruction of the biome. nd generate the most culturally appropriate, and ecologically compatible energy solutions. Commons theory Since community renewables involve issues related to shared land property and natural resources, this study is framed within the context of the commons theory. This theory began with Hardin's “The Tragedy of the Commons,” which describes the problems of overuse and degrada- tion of natural resources [18]. Elinor Ostrom's book Governing the Commons [19]and her article [20]proposed eight desi

sources [18]. Elinor Ostrom's book Governing the Commons [19]and her article [20]proposed eight design principles for the governance of common-pool resources: (1) clearly defined bound- aries; (2) rules tailored to local circumstances; (3) participatory decision-making; (4) monitoring; (5) graduated sanctions for those who abuse the commons; (6) accessible conflict resolution; (7) the com- munity's right to self-organize; and (8) nested, larger networks [19,21]. Since then, Cox et al. [22]has ap

s right to self-organize; and (8) nested, larger networks [19,21]. Since then, Cox et al. [23]applied the commons framework to renewable energy and examined the factors that foster community engagement in wind power cooperatives in Denmark, Germany, Belgium, and the United Kingdom, whil

ity engagement in wind power cooperatives in Denmark, Germany, Belgium, and the United Kingdom, while Wade and Ellis [24]used wind rent in conjunction with Ostrom's institutional analysis. Andersson [25] employed the Institutional Analysis and Development (IAD) framework to identify which institutional factors influence the likelihood of suc- cessful governance. This research aligns with Bauwens et al. [23] and Wade and Ellis [24], which view wind as a common-pool resource. We analyze three case

t al. [23] and Wade and Ellis [24], which view wind as a common-pool resource. We analyze three cases of Jeju Island in South Korea, where commercial wind turbines are installed on shared pastureland managed by village committees. Led by the government or commercial wind power companies, compensation is provided to the village committees, which then manage the community funds. These case studies are consistent with the commons theory described in Ostrom [21], as they involve clearly defined vill

re consistent with the commons theory described in Ostrom [21], as they involve clearly defined village boundaries, community-specific rules, local decision-making, and the right to self-organize. This research explores the institutional and so- cioeconomic factors that influence the likelihood of managing wind farms through the lens of commons theory. 2.2. Conceptual theory of benefit sharing mechanism The benefit sharing mechanism (BSM) is conceptualized as a system that distributes direct and

hanism The benefit sharing mechanism (BSM) is conceptualized as a system that distributes direct and indirect benefits derived from power gener- ation projects among stakeholders or local communities, given diverse terms such as “benefit payment” or “compensation to the community”. Comparative analyses have further explored the distinctions between corporate-led and resident-driven power generation models, contrib- uting to a nuanced understanding of BSM dynamics [15]. Studies have explored dive

n models, contrib- uting to a nuanced understanding of BSM dynamics [15]. Studies have explored diverse aspects, including resident attitudes toward wind power projects, barriers to benefit sharing, and the role of BSM in enhancing local acceptability [16]. Since community-engaged renewable energy became more prevalent in the early 2000s, the BSMs began to be studied in the United Kingdom, especially in Scotland [16,26–29]. Following these studies, more coun- tries have adopted community-based r

lly in Scotland [16,26–29]. Following these studies, more coun- tries have adopted community-based renewable energy generation, and research has been conducted primarily in European countries such as Sweden [30], France [31], Denmark [32,33], Germany [34], and the Netherlands [35], while some studies have also examined community- based approaches to renewable energy projects globally, such as in Tunisia [37]and Australia [36]. South Korea has also been exploring the benefit-sharing approach sinc

unisia [37]and Australia [36]. South Korea has also been exploring the benefit-sharing approach since the late 2010s [11,38]. Prior work spans specific technologies on wind power [28,39], solar power [40,41], and hydropower [42], as well as mixed wind–solar contexts where community engagement is salient [43–45]. Empirical work reports that community ownership is associated with higher local acceptance in Germany [15] and Scotland [44], and qualitative interviews in South Korea suggest similar patterns [11,38]. ownership embeds residents in the project's governance and risk–return structure (e.g., equity and voting rights), whereas BSM primarily concerns the distribution of project benefits (e.g., community funds or agreements) without implying ownership. Benefit-sharing Benefit-sharing can take various forms, including direct or indirect investment, project financing, scholarship funding, educational pro- grams, energy price reductions, village fund formation, local employ- ment, benefit distribution, development fund establishment, shared ownership, community tax refunds, and electr

it distribution, development fund establishment, shared ownership, community tax refunds, and electricity tariff reductions. In this study, benefit-sharing is categorized into the following types: (1) village funds provided by developers to residents, often managed by village institutions, local governments, or community organizations, and used for infrastructure or vulnerable groups [51,52]; (2) compen- sation for potential or realized damages [53]; (3) benefits-in-kind, such as non-financial s

compen- sation for potential or realized damages [53]; (3) benefits-in-kind, such as non-financial support including food or educational programs [54]; (4) local employment during the development, construction, and oper- ation [41]; (5) energy price reductions, offering discounted electricity rates to residents [55,56]; and (6) indirect social benefits, such as tourism reputation effects [16,54]. Based on prior work, village funds, compensation, local employment, and energy price reductions are

Based on prior work, village funds, compensation, local employment, and energy price reductions are among the most frequently reported types. In this paper, the term compensation is used in a broad sense to encompass direct and indirect benefits provided to community mem- bers. When used in a narrow sense, it is limited to cash payments from companies to residents as direct compensation for damages. Where we refer specifically to this narrow usage, we explicitly indicate “direct cash compensatio

Community engagement Community engagement in wind projects refers to how project de- velopers or public entities involve community members in decision- making processes (e.g., information, consultation, committee-based deliberation) [56,60]. Related arrangements, such as benefit-sharing (e.g., community funds or agreements) and community ownership (eq- uity stakes), may co-occur but are

e.g., community funds or agreements) and community ownership (eq- uity stakes), may co-occur but are treated as distinct concepts in this paper. At the global level, many wind and other renewable energy projects are developed and owned by companies; community engage- ment in such projects concerns how local residents are involved in governance and how benefits are distributed, rather than implying resident-only development [57]. The degree and form of engagement vary across mechanisms (e.g., inf

resident-only development [57]. The degree and form of engagement vary across mechanisms (e.g., information disclosure and consultation, community consultative com- mittees, and community benefit agreements or funds) and across project phases (planning, development, operation) [57,60]. In Australia, Howard [57]proposed a framework highlighting consultative commit- tees and contributions to local infrastructure. infrastructure. and community engagement around wind power [65] and solar energy projects [45]. Case study description Drawing on field investigations and in-depth interviews, the case studies show that wind turbines were installed on shared pasture. Particularly, the wind power sites in Haengwon-ri, Samdal-ri, and Gasi- ri were originally village communal pastures, which are regarded as common-pool resources collectively owned and managed by residents. T

pastures, which are regarded as common-pool resources collectively owned and managed by residents. Thus, decisions to install wind power projects on the shared pasture required local resident's agreement and their self-governance based on funds provided by the wind power companies. The village committee is composed of residents recognized as full members of the community, a status that in Jeju Island's long- established villages is generally reserved for long-term inhabitants. Newly relocated in

and's long- established villages is generally reserved for long-term inhabitants. Newly relocated individuals, even if officially residing within the vil- lage's administrative boundaries, often require a significant period before being regarded as full members eligible to serve on the com- mittee. As a result, committee members are often long-time residents who grew up in the village, most of whom are farmers over the age of 60. We addition- ally conducted in-depth interviews with survey respondents to explore findings that were not fully captured through the surveys. The questionnaire utilized in this study was meticulously designed to encompass inquiries concerning various aspects, including the types of benefit-sharing, the degree of local accept

iries concerning various aspects, including the types of benefit-sharing, the degree of local acceptance toward wind turbines, and the level of residents' environmental awareness. Additionally, de- mographic information such as age, duration of residence, type of resi- dency, presence of a village committee, and occupation was incorporated into the questionnaire. Structured closed-type items and optional questions formed the framework of the questionnaire. Data were collected through face-to- fa

ptional questions formed the framework of the questionnaire. Data were collected through face-to- face interactions. Drawing from literature research and prior in- terviews, the structured questionnaire was constructed based on the Likert 5-point scale. The section of the questionnaire about local acceptance encompassed opinions before and after wind turbine installation, evaluations of the pros and cons of wind turbines, and as- sessments of village perception post-installation (See Table 3). P

nd cons of wind turbines, and as- sessments of village perception post-installation (See Table 3). Percep- tion measures were collected on 5-point Likert scales. Specifically, X1 (benefit-sharing) uses item A3, which asks respondents to rate the extent to which benefit-sharing is provided in their village, and X2 (community engagement) uses item A5, which asks respondents to rate the extent of village-level participation in decision-making (e.g., public hearings/ consultations, committee process

lage-level participation in decision-making (e.g., public hearings/ consultations, committee processes) as perceived by residents. Consequently, higher scores indicate a more positive perception of advantages, whereas lower scores indicate a more negative view of disadvantages associated with wind turbines. sulta- tion, and pilot testing to address ambiguities or errors. Given the study's focus on BSM, local acceptance, and environmental awareness, we assessed validity and reliability. Factor analysis with principal compo- nent extraction and orthogonal varimax rotation was used to identify latent structure. Factor retention followed the eigenvalue >1.0 criterion. piro-Wilk test; results indicated p- values between 0.00 and 0.02 across all survey items, suggesting non- normal distributions. 3.5. Data analysis Hypothesis 2-1examines the level of awareness about wind turbines. Questions B9–1 to B9–5 ask whether wind turbines improve air quality, provide infinite energy sources, increase energy independence, offer economic benefits to the village, and contribute to village revitalization. All responses show positive scores from residents of Samdal-ri, Gasi-r

te to village revitalization. All responses show positive scores from residents of Samdal-ri, Gasi-ri, and Haengwon-ri. Questions B10–1 to B10–5 assess perceptions of the disadvantages of wind turbines (noise, land damage, landscape damage, rand animal/vegetation damage, and psychological damage), using reverse-coded versions of the positive questions.
