sis, so lower scores indicate fewer negative ef- fects, representing a more positive outcome. Upon completion of data collection, the acquired data underwent rigorous analysis to assess validity and reliability, utilizing Python. First, given the non-normal distribution of all variables, the Kruskal-Wallis nonparametric test method was chosen, treating the dependent vari- able as continuous. Second, correlation analysis was conducted to explore potential relationships among the variables under c

d, correlation analysis was conducted to explore potential relationships among the variables under consider- ation. Finally, multi-linear regression analysis was employed to examine associations among benefit-sharing, community engagement, local acceptance, and environmental awareness. For community engagement, the two binary indicators were summed to form index (0–2), rescaled to 0–1, and standardized (z-score) prior to modeling. dized (z-score) prior to modeling. Kruskal-Wallis test result As shown in Table 5, we conducted the Kruskal–Wallis nonpara- metric test to examine Hypotheses 1–1, and 1–2, followed by post-hoc multiple comparisons to identify differences among the villages. The results revealed statistically significant differences (p <0.05) in local acceptance levels (H1(cid:0) 1), whereas no significant differences were observed in envir

in local acceptance levels (H1(cid:0) 1), whereas no significant differences were observed in environmental awareness (H1(cid:0) 2). Post-hoc analysis indi- cated that the most pronounced difference in local acceptance occurred between Gasi-ri and Haengwon-ri, with Gasi-ri showing notably higher scores. In contrast, Samdal-ri's scores did not significantly differ from those of either Gasi-ri or Haengwon-ri. This pattern suggests that local acceptance may be shaped not only by the presence of be

aengwon-ri. This pattern suggests that local acceptance may be shaped not only by the presence of benefit-sharing or ownership schemes but also by contextual factors such as past experi- ences with wind projects, perceptions of fairness in benefit distribution, and the nature of interactions between developers and the community. Since these elements could not be fully captured through survey data alone, additional field visits and interviews with wind farm village committee leaders were conducte

alone, additional field visits and interviews with wind farm village committee leaders were conducted to explore these underlying factors in greater depth. 4.3. it-sharing tend to be accompanied by more favorable evaluations of wind turbines and their contribution to the village. The aim of this paper is to understand the position and situation of women in employment related to the energy transition economic ac- tivity. For that purpose, we firstly use data for the European Union (EU) and specifically for Spain to quan

For that purpose, we firstly use data for the European Union (EU) and specifically for Spain to quantify existing gaps and analyse their patterns. Secondly, we focus on Spain and apply logit models and decomposition techniques for those models to analyse the gender dif- ferences in the probability of accessing employment in the sector, con- trolling for different personal and environmental characteristics, including a detailed analysis of the factors influencing the observed differences. eristics, including a detailed analysis of the factors influencing the observed differences. In this study, energy transition economic activities are defined as those aligned with the transformation of the energy system towards net- zero carbon emissions, including renewable energy generation, energy efficiency, clean transportation, and related industrial and consulting services. This definition is operationalized using the National Classifi- cation of Economic Activities (NACE Rev. 2) [25], de

is operationalized using the National Classifi- cation of Economic Activities (NACE Rev. 2) [25], detailed in the Methodology section. Our working hypothesis is that energy transition employment suffers from negative biases towards the employment of women, both in terms of their entry into the labour market and in relation to horizontal segregation. These biases are related to the social construction of the role of women in the private sphere, as presented in the theoretical framework. This pape

ion of the role of women in the private sphere, as presented in the theoretical framework. This paper contributes to the literature in two ways. Firstly, by providing a comprehensive and operational delimitation of the energy transition sector in terms of economic activities and employment. of a delimitation of economic activities and employment related to energy transition, both from a conceptual and operational perspective. In this sense, we design an applied delim- itation of the activities related to the energy transition using the NACE Rev. 2, that can be applied to any country that uses NACE, including the European Union. pirical evidence and thus, to the progress in comparative studies based on a broad and consistent definition. Methodology and database Despite the attention received from the policy perspective, there is no consensus about the delimitation of energy transition from the con- ceptual and applied perspective. Thus, one of the challenges of this work has been the design of that delimitation from an applied perspective. The objective, as with other concepts such as green economy, is to obtain an ad hoc delimitation of the activities related to the energy transition in terms of the eco

obtain an ad hoc delimitation of the activities related to the energy transition in terms of the economic sectors defined at the NACE Rev. 2, in order to use labour statistics to characterize employment. The approach used in the delimitation relies on the activities that are at the core of the policies focused on energy transition. For the Spanish case, as mentioned previously the NIECP serves as the national strategic planning instrument that synthesizes energy and climate policies aiming to at

the national strategic planning instrument that synthesizes energy and climate policies aiming to attain the decarbonization objectives established by the European Union. As such, it encompasses a comprehensive set of actions and in- vestments, both public and private, to be developed along the upcoming decade. Consequently, it serves as a pertinent guide to address the pri- mary inquiry: which subsectors or activities will concentrate employ- ment generation associated with the energy transitio

bsectors or activities will concentrate employ- ment generation associated with the energy transition. This is particularly noteworthy given the substantial investments anticipated in these subsectors, as indicated by the impact analysis within the NIECP itself. Following an exhaustive examination of the array of actions outlined in the NIECP across its five axes of operation (Decarbonization, including renewable energies; Energy efficiency; Energy security; In- ternal energy market; and Researc

luding renewable energies; Energy efficiency; Energy security; In- ternal energy market; and Research, innovation, and competitiveness), and building upon advancements in the realms of the green economy 3 R. labour force statistics in terms of the disag- gregation provided for economic activities2 [49]. not specific to the energy transition but intricately connected to it, as they provide intermediate consumption or inputs to the core subsectors). The source of data used for the analysis of the employment within energy transition activities in Spain is the Labour Force Survey for the European Union, with specific focus in Spain at the econometric section, with data for different years. The quantitative strategy relies firstly on the analysis of the employment to identify existing patterns of employment and the gender gaps in those patterns, providing a framework for studying d

g patterns of employment and the gender gaps in those patterns, providing a framework for studying differences between men and women entering the labour market in energy transi- tion activities. Subsequently, a logit regression model is employed to further examine variations in the probability of being employed in the energy transition sectors. The model allows the analysis of the influ

ility of being employed in the energy transition sectors. The model allows the analysis of the influence of different factors, including sex as the key variable, to reflect possible different patterns. P=Pr[y=1/X]=F(X'β) (1) where P is the probability of being employed in the analysed sectors, X is a vector of explanatory or control variables, and β is a vector of co- efficients to be estimated. Moreover, F(X'β) is the cumulative distribu- tion function of the logistic function: F(X'β)=[exp(X'β)

oreover, F(X'β) is the cumulative distribu- tion function of the logistic function: F(X'β)=[exp(X'β)]/[1+exp(X'β)] (2) The dependent variable is a dichotomous variable, which takes value 1 if the worker is employed at energy transition sectors and 0 if he or she is employed in other sectors. The estimated coefficients using logit models cannot be directly interpreted as the magnitude of the change in the outcome probability that is associated with a one-unit change in the corresponding explanato

in the outcome probability that is associated with a one-unit change in the corresponding explanatory variable, and thus they only represent the direction of the relationship between the dependent variable and the explanatory variables. Therefore, the sign of the coefficients indicates the direction of the change in the likelihood or probability of entering the labour market. To allow for a better interpretation of the results, the marginal effect at the mean (MEM) of each estimated coefficient

r interpretation of the results, the marginal effect at the mean (MEM) of each estimated coefficient is calculated. In our model, the marginal effect indicates the change in the probability of being employed at the energy transition sectors as the corresponding explanatory variable increases in one unit, holding all other variables at their mean. We consider three groups of explanatory variables: individual, job and establishment characteristics. The set of individual characteristic variables in

individual, job and establishment characteristics. The set of individual characteristic variables includes the sex of the worker, age as a proxy of work expe- rience and its squared value, nationality, and the educational level divided into four categories (below primary education, primary and lower secondary education, upper secondary and professional educa- tion, university education). The sex of the worker is included by means of a dichotomous variable (female) which takes a value of one if t

f the worker is included by means of a dichotomous variable (female) which takes a value of one if the worker is female and zero otherwise. Among the set of job characteris- tics, we included information on occupation aggregated at the nine major groups according to the ISCO-08, the type of contract (permanent or fixed-term), and the type of working day (full-time or part time). The set of establishment characteristics includes the type of ownership (public or private) and the geographical locat

shment characteristics includes the type of ownership (public or private) and the geographical location at the NUTS-3 level. The sign of the coefficient for the female variables indicates whether female workers have a lower/greater probability of being employed at 4 R. Santero-Sa´nchez et al. the energy transition sectors. The corresponding marginal effect is the difference in the probability of being employed at the energy transition sectors between females and males. Additionally, we apply the

eing employed at the energy transition sectors between females and males. Additionally, we apply the methodology proposed by Fairlie [50] for decomposing differences in logit models to conduct a detailed analysis of the factors influencing the differences between females and males in the probability of being employed in transition energy sectors. This approach draws upon the decomposition technique initially formulated by Blinder and Oaxaca [51,52] for linear models, which has found extensive ap

e initially formulated by Blinder and Oaxaca [51,52] for linear models, which has found extensive application in labour market studies for studying wage dif- ferentials among distinct demographic groups. The methodological in- volves several steps. Initially, a baseline logit model for probability of being employed in transition energy sectors is estimated using pooled data from the two groups, females, and males. Afterwards, the fitted probability is calculated for each observation in the datas

males, and males. Afterwards, the fitted probability is calculated for each observation in the dataset using the estimated coefficients from the baseline model. Then, the mean proba- bility of being employed in transition energy sectors is computed sepa- rately for females and males. Lastly, the difference in mean probabilities between the two groups is decomposed into components attributable to differences in observed characteristics (age, education, occupation…), which constitute the so-called

ifferences in observed characteristics (age, education, occupation…), which constitute the so-called explained component, and differences in the coefficients of the explanatory variables, which conform the so- called unexplained component. The unexplained component can be closely related to the presence of gender discrimination, but also to the possibility of omitted important variables when explaining the proba- bility of being employed in energy transition sectors. 4. Introduction The intersection of energy poverty, the social energy tariff scheme,1 and health outcomes is a critical area of research with significant im- plications for public health, social policy, and a fair transition. Energy poverty —defined as the inability to maintain homes at a comfortable temperature—affects more than 40 millio

—defined as the inability to maintain homes at a comfortable temperature—affects more than 40 million Europeans (Eurostat, 2024), leading to adverse health outcomes and exacerbating social inequalities (Champagne et al., 2023; Ballesteros-Arjona et al., 2022). Research has consistently shown that energy poverty is closely linked to poor health outcomes (Pan et al., 2021; Katoch et al., 2024). instance, studies have found that individuals living in energy-poor households are more likely to suffer from respiratory diseases, cardio- vascular problems, and mental health issues (Xu et al., 2022; Bentley et al., 2023). Tackling energy poverty through policy intervention In recent years, the commitment of the European Union and its member states to reducing energy poverty has reached levels that were scarcely imaginable just over three decades ago (European Commission, 2023), w

ched levels that were scarcely imaginable just over three decades ago (European Commission, 2023), when the first specialized literature was being established with the seminal contribution of British scholar Boardman (1991). The UK researcher laid the groundwork for a progressive understanding and scope of energy poverty. In fact, energy poverty was not incorporated into EU energy policy until 2009, with the introduction of the Electricity Directive (Directive, 2009/72/EC) (European Parliament a

9, with the introduction of the Electricity Directive (Directive, 2009/72/EC) (European Parliament and the Council of European Union, 2009). Initially, the European approach to dealing with energy poverty was limited to promoting energy affordability through price regulation, direct income support systems, and disconnection prohibition mecha- nisms. However, since the publication of the European Green Deal in 2019 (European Commission, 2019), which addresses the current climate crisis and suppor

reen Deal in 2019 (European Commission, 2019), which addresses the current climate crisis and supports a socially just and inclusive energy transition, and more recently, the European "Fit for 55″ plan, a new comprehensive vision has emerged for tackling the causes of energy poverty, com- plementing social policy. Nowadays, energy poverty is granted significant importance in Eu- ropean legislation and is recognized as a form of deprivation that must be systematically controlled and remedied by t

and is recognized as a form of deprivation that must be systematically controlled and remedied by the member states. In fact, current regulations require member states to address energy poverty in their National Energy and Climate Plans by quantifying the number of households experiencing energy poverty, defining national reduction targets, specifying timelines for achieving these targets, and deter- mining policies and measures to tackle energy poverty. Despite the European Union’s strong comm

ter- mining policies and measures to tackle energy poverty. Despite the European Union’s strong commitment to advancing the mitigation of this issue, the fight against energy poverty at the political and social 2 E n e r g y P o li c y 206 (2025) 114762 levels is at varying stages of development within the European frame- work, reflecting the different institutional and territorial contexts of each country (Stojilovska et al., 2022). The final choice of implemented measures, as well as their int

h country (Stojilovska et al., 2022). The final choice of implemented measures, as well as their intensity and scope, rests with national gov- ernments, resulting in fragmented policies that exacerbate vulnerability differences within the European Union (Bouzarovski et al., 2012; Cos- ta-Campi et al., 2020). Therefore, the extent of energy poverty varies among member states (Eurostat, 2024). In Spain, the situation is even more concerning, with 20.8 % of the population facing difficulties in mai

ain, the situation is even more concerning, with 20.8 % of the population facing difficulties in maintaining their homes at a comfortable temperature (Eurostat, 2024). This makes Spain, along with its neighbor Portugal (20.8 %), the country with the highest incidence of energy poverty in the European Union. Energy poverty in Europe and, therefore, Spain is not a new or temporary issue resulting from the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic and the current energy crisis. On the contrary, it remains a

om the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic and the current energy crisis. On the contrary, it remains a significant concern following the severe impact of the 2008 financial crisis and is closely linked to the economic cycle (Costa-Campi et al., 2024). This situation underscores the need for legislative measures to protect the most vulnerable segments of the population, as well as an evaluation of the implemented policies to determine whether the problem is being mitigated or the measures need adju

e implemented policies to determine whether the problem is being mitigated or the measures need adjusting to enhance their effectiveness. As a response to tackle energy poverty in Spain, Royal Decree-Law 6/ 2009, dated April 30, 2009 (Spanish Government, 2009), was enacted to establish the social energy tariff. This instrument was initially designed to provide a discount on electricity bills for vulnerable households enrolled in the Voluntary Price for Small Consumers scheme (PVPC). Since its in

ulnerable households enrolled in the Voluntary Price for Small Consumers scheme (PVPC). Since its inception, the social energy tariff has undergone several mod- ifications aimed at improving its benefits and reaching a broader vulnerable population.3 These changes include revised income eligi- bility criteria, increased annual electricity consumption limits for dis- counted rates, and the inclusion of additional vulnerable groups, such as single-parent families and individuals with level II or I

n of additional vulnerable groups, such as single-parent families and individuals with level II or III dependency. In 2018, the tariff was expanded to also provide an annual financial allowance for heating, hot water, and cooking. Today, the social energy tariff covers both electricity and thermal energy needs. Even though, in recent years, new or improved instruments have been created specifically aimed at mitigating energy poverty, the liter- ature focused on examining the efficacy of governme

y aimed at mitigating energy poverty, the liter- ature focused on examining the efficacy of government tools and re- sponses to energy poverty is limited (Bienvenido-Huertas, 2021; Pillai et al., 2023). One of the early studies on the impacts of the social energy tariff policy in Spain was conducted by the researchers García-Alvarez and Tol (2021). Using a sample of households from 2008 to 2011 and employing a difference-in-differences approach, their work confirms that the initial version of th

In light of this, while various pro- grams such as Spain’s social energy tariff, the UK’s Warm Home Discount Scheme, and the US’s Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program have provided partial relief from energy poverty, their long-term effectiveness remains debated due to implementation chal- lenges, limited reach, and behavioral barriers. ementation chal- lenges, limited reach, and behavioral barriers. Integrated approaches, such as those combining subsidies with energy efficiency measures, appear more promising for sustainable impact. Beyond economic and energy consumption outcomes, recent literature underscores the importance of addressing energy poverty as a social determinant of health. There is growing recognition that inadequate energy access af- fects both physical and mental health, particularly among vulnerable populatio

ate energy access af- fects both physical and mental health, particularly among vulnerable populations. For instance, Rodrigo et al. (2024)analyzed the UK’s financial sup- port schemes, finding that direct energy subsidies to low-income households significantly reduced health risks associated with thermal discomfort. Similarly, Oliveras et al. (2020)emphasized that integrating health into energy policy design can reduce healthcare utilization and associated costs. These findings are consistent w

olicy design can reduce healthcare utilization and associated costs. These findings are consistent with recent European Union reports (O’Connor et al., 2024), which highlight the dual benefit of energy efficiency upgrades: they improve household energy security and contribute to better overall health outcomes. However, most existing studies focus on isolated policy effects —either on energy consumption or on health— without fully exploring the complex interrelation between energy poverty, target

ption or on health— without fully exploring the complex interrelation between energy poverty, targeted subsidy schemes like the social energy tariff, and their health impacts. Bridging this gap is essential for designing more effective, equity-oriented policies. 2.2. Energy poverty and health Energy poverty has significant implications for health through various interconnected mechanisms. These impacts manifest across physical, mental, and psychosocial dimensions and arise from inade- quate livi

pacts manifest across physical, mental, and psychosocial dimensions and arise from inade- quate living conditions, financial strain, and the inability to access essential energy services. Energy poverty is strongly associated with a range of adverse phys- ical health outcomes through which energy poverty affects health and thermal stress due to inadequate heating or cooling in homes (Oliveras et al., 2020; Llorca et al., 2020; Churchill and Smyth, 2021; Ballester- os-Arjona et al., 2022; Bentley

l., 2020; Llorca et al., 2020; Churchill and Smyth, 2021; Ballester- os-Arjona et al., 2022; Bentley et al., 2023; Charlier and Legendre, 2023; Katoch et al., 2024). Many households facing energy deprivation rely on solid fuels such as wood, coal, or biomass for cooking and heating, often in poorly ventilated environments. This reliance contributes to signifi- cant indoor air pollution, exposing residents to harmful particulate matter and toxic gases. The resulting health consequences include re

esidents to harmful particulate matter and toxic gases. The resulting health consequences include res- piratory diseases such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, asthma, and lung cancer, alongside cardiovascular conditions linked to prolonged exposure to air pollutants. Moreover, poor air quality com- promises the immune system, increasing vulnerability to respiratory infections. For instance, Katoch et al. (2024)found that energy poverty increases the risk of poor physical condition, maln

e, Katoch et al. (2024)found that energy poverty increases the risk of poor physical condition, malnutrition, respiratory disease, depression, asthma exacerbations and other negative effects on health. Homes affected by energy poverty are often poorly insulated and prone to dampness, condensation, and mold growth. These living con- ditions create an environment that exacerbates respiratory infections, allergies, and asthma. Chronic exposure to such conditions can lead to long-term impairments in

ons, allergies, and asthma. Chronic exposure to such conditions can lead to long-term impairments in respiratory function and overall health. This association is consistent with findings from Polimeni et al. (2022), who reported that energy poverty in the European Union is linked to higher incidences of both acute and chronic health conditions. Research by Mohan (2021) further underscores the negative impacts of energy poverty on children’s health. The study found that children living in 3 E n e

tive impacts of energy poverty on children’s health. The study found that children living in 3 E n e r g y P o li c y 206 (2025) 114762 energy-poor households are more likely to suffer from developmental issues and chronic illnesses, which can have long-term consequences on their overall well-being. Similarly, Oliveras et al. (2020)highlight that energy poverty homes lead to increased rates of asthma and overweight among children. Kose (2019) found the same type of effects in other vulnerable po

hma and overweight among children. Kose (2019) found the same type of effects in other vulnerable population subgroups such as the elderly. Financial stress may also result from energy poverty, particularly when households face high energy costs relative to income, thus rep- resenting another pathway linking energy poverty to health. Households facing high energy costs frequently divert resources away from essential needs such as food and healthcare to pay for utilities. This trade-off can lead

away from essential needs such as food and healthcare to pay for utilities. This trade-off can lead to nutritional deficiencies and delayed or neglected medical care, exacerbating existing health issues. The psychological strain of man- aging financial insecurity adds another layer of mental health burden, often contributing to anxiety, depression, and chronic stress. This financial burden disproportionately affects low-income families, who are unable to invest in energy-efficient technologies o

ortionately affects low-income families, who are unable to invest in energy-efficient technologies or home improve- ments that could lower their energy costs over time. Consequently, they remain trapped in a cycle in which high energy expenses consume a significant share of their income. The mental health implications of energy poverty are equally significant. Xu et al. (2022) demonstrated that households experiencing energy poverty are at a higher risk of depression, anxiety, and other mental h

useholds experiencing energy poverty are at a higher risk of depression, anxiety, and other mental health disorders. This is corrob- orated by Churchill and Smyth (2021), who found a strong correlation between energy poverty and mental distress in Australia, and the sys- tematic review of Katoch et al. (2024), which highlights the relevant effect on mental health and education. In addition, two papers have explored the effects of energy poverty on physical and mental health. McTague and Trujillo

pers have explored the effects of energy poverty on physical and mental health. McTague and Trujillo-Baute (2025) analyzed the effect of energy poverty on physical and mental health outcomes among individuals aged over 50 in the United States, showing that energy-poor elders have poorer health, higher body mass index, and higher depressive symptoms. Oliveras et al. (2020)also found effects on mental health in vulnerable subgroup populations, such as children. The impacts of energy poverty are fu

al health in vulnerable subgroup populations, such as children. The impacts of energy poverty are further compounded by inequities that disproportionately affect vulnerable populations. Older adults face heightened mortality risks due to extreme temperatures, while children exposed to cold, damp, or polluted living conditions may experience impaired physical and cognitive development. Individuals with dis- abilities often struggle to meet their specific energy needs for assistive devices, reduci

with dis- abilities often struggle to meet their specific energy needs for assistive devices, reducing their quality of life and independence. Furthermore, Charlier and Legendre (2023) explored the broader psychosocial impacts of energy poverty, revealing that the constant financial strain and social isolation resulting from energy deprivation contribute to deteriorating mental health. The study also highlighted that these mental health effects are not uniformly distributed but rather more prono

lso highlighted that these mental health effects are not uniformly distributed but rather more pronounced among low-income households and marginalized communities. Social isolation is another critical dimension of energy poverty, both as a cause and a consequence. People living in energy poverty often avoid social interactions out of embarrassment over their inadequate living conditions, such as poorly heated or cooled homes. This avoidance reduces social engagement and increases feelings of lon

oorly heated or cooled homes. This avoidance reduces social engagement and increases feelings of loneliness, particularly among vulnerable groups like older adults. Financial constraints also limit participation in social activities, com- munity events, or even essential trips, such as medical appointments, further deepening isolation. This sense of disconnection can exacerbate feelings of exclusion and reduce opportunities for social support, creating a feedback loop between energy poverty and

on and reduce opportunities for social support, creating a feedback loop between energy poverty and social isolation. This issue is particularly significant for older adults and individuals with limited mobility, who often rely on social connections to maintain emotional health. 2.3. Linking energy poverty alleviation policies with health outcomes The relationship between energy poverty and health has garnered increasing attention in recent years, prompting researchers and 3 E. Jov´e-Llopis et a

has garnered increasing attention in recent years, prompting researchers and 3 E. Jov´e-Llopis et al. policymakers to explore whether energy-related interventions can yield measurable public health benefits (Liddell and Morris, 2010; Navarro et al., 2010; Llorca et al., 2020; Ballesteros-Arjona et al., 2022; European Public Health Alliance, 2024). While energy poverty is widely recog- nized as a social determinant of health, most mitigation strategies have focused narrowly on financial relief m

social determinant of health, most mitigation strategies have focused narrowly on financial relief mechanisms, such as subsidies or price regulations (European Public Health Alliance, 2024; Herna´ndez, 2016). Although these tools are essential in the short term, their po- tential to improve health outcomes depends not only on the extent to which they alleviate energy deprivation but also on their ability to address the structural vulnerabilities that underpin poor health among energy-poor househ

ability to address the structural vulnerabilities that underpin poor health among energy-poor households. Among these policy instruments, Spain’s social energy tariff offers a valuable case study to examine the intersection between targeted energy subsidies and population health. As a price-based intervention, the so- cial energy tariff aims to reduce the economic burden of electricity consumption for low-income households, thereby enabling them to maintain thermal comfort and access basic energ

for low-income households, thereby enabling them to maintain thermal comfort and access basic energy services. Theoreti- cally, this should mitigate health risks associated with cold or over- heated living environments, including respiratory and cardiovascular conditions, as well as mental health disorders triggered by financial stress and energy insecurity. However, empirical research suggests that the actual health benefits of such schemes may be modest and uneven, limited by implementation c

hat the actual health benefits of such schemes may be modest and uneven, limited by implementation challenges, eligibility restrictions, low up- take rates, and insufficient integration with other social or health ser- vices (García-Alvarez and Tol, 2021). Evidence from Spain and other European countries indicates that policy design plays a critical role in determining health outcomes (Fan et al., 2024; Lena Heller et al., 2024). For example, while direct subsidies can reduce immediate hardship,

2024; Lena Heller et al., 2024). For example, while direct subsidies can reduce immediate hardship, they may not significantly improve long-term health unless they are coupled with complementary measures such as energy efficiency improvements, insulation upgrades, and tailored health and social care services. In countries like Germany and the UK, integrated models that combine income support with structural housing interventions have shown greater promise in reducing both energy poverty and its

structural housing interventions have shown greater promise in reducing both energy poverty and its health consequences. These findings underscore the need for holistic approaches that move beyond affordability to address the broader determinants of health vulnerability in energy-poor households. To enhance policy effectiveness, there is a growing consensus that health indicators should be systematically incorporated into the design, monitoring, and evaluation of energy poverty interventions (E

atically incorporated into the design, monitoring, and evaluation of energy poverty interventions (European Commission, 2025). This would allow for a better understanding of the causal pathways linking energy deprivation to health outcomes and would help optimize resource allocation across sectors. Furthermore, establishing robust evidence on the health impacts of energy in- terventions could strengthen the case for intersectoral collaboration between energy, housing, and health authorities, pro

ngthen the case for intersectoral collaboration between energy, housing, and health authorities, promoting a more equitable and efficient response to energy poverty. In this context, the Spanish social energy tariff represents not only a financial tool but also a potential lever to generate co-benefits in health, provided it is integrated into a broader, multidimensional policy framework. 3. Methods 3.1. Data and empirical strategy To analyze how the social energy tariff affects energy poverty a

ds 3.1. Data and empirical strategy To analyze how the social energy tariff affects energy poverty and health status in Spain, this study uses data from the Living Conditions Survey (LCS), provided by the Spanish Statistical Office (INE). The database includes approximately 50,000 Spanish households in 2022. The main advantage of the LCS for this study is that it provides detailed, harmonized, and nationally representative data on household income, housing characteristics, energy-related depriva

nationally representative data on household income, housing characteristics, energy-related deprivations, and self- perceived health, enabling a comprehensive analysis of the link be- tween energy poverty and health outcomes. In particular, the 2022 wave 4 E n e r g y P o li c y 206 (2025) 114762 of the LCS includes, for the first time, a specific module on health and quality of life, along with a new question directly related to energy assistance: "Did the household benefit from any subsidies

a new question directly related to energy assistance: "Did the household benefit from any subsidies or discounted tariff to cover expenses for electricity, heating, gas, etc.?" This new in- formation is highly relevant for the purposes of our study, as it allows us to directly identify households receiving energy-related subsidies. As these variables are only available in the 2022 wave, the analysis is necessarily cross-sectional. Despite these advantages that contribute to advancing knowledge,

is is necessarily cross-sectional. Despite these advantages that contribute to advancing knowledge, it is important to acknowledge that the LCS is not without limitations. Specifically, as a cross-sectional dataset, it does not allow for tracking households over time. Although a longitudinal design would have been ideal for assessing the effects of a policy such as the social energy tariff on energy poverty and health outcomes, the available cross-sectional data still offer valuable insights whe

gy poverty and health outcomes, the available cross-sectional data still offer valuable insights when interpreted with appropriate caution. In this study, we apply a recursive bivariate probit model, which enables us to estimate two binary outcome variables that may be interrelated, allowing for the possibility that one of the outcomes might influence the other. In this context, the “recursive” aspect means that the outcome of one equation affects the outcome of the other, creating a causal rela

pect means that the outcome of one equation affects the outcome of the other, creating a causal relationship between the two. The recursive bivariate probit model is suitable for this analysis as it accounts for the endogeneity of energy poverty in estimating its effect on health. Energy poverty is considered endogenous in this context due to the bidirectional rela- tionship between health and energy poverty: poor health can lead to increased energy needs and financial strain, potentially causin

rgy poverty: poor health can lead to increased energy needs and financial strain, potentially causing energy poverty, while energy poverty can negatively impact health through inadequate heating or cooling and the inability to maintain a healthy living environment (Thomson and Snell, 2013; Liddell and Morris, 2010; Healy and Clinch, 2004; Herna´ndez, 2016; Grey et al., 2017). This simultaneity necessitates a model that can appropriately handle the endogeneity to avoid biased and inconsistent est

essitates a model that can appropriately handle the endogeneity to avoid biased and inconsistent estimates, using two equations, one for the endogenous variable (energy poverty) and the other for the output variable (health status). 3.1.1. The explanatory variables (Xi)used in the first equation to under- stand the drivers of energy poverty are grouped into four main cate- gories, in line with the empirical literat

rivers of energy poverty are grouped into four main cate- gories, in line with the empirical literature (Costa-Campi et al., 2024; Burguillo et al., 2025): sociodemographic characteristics of the house- hold reference person, the household’s economic profile, dwelling characteristics, and climate-related factors reflecting extreme tempera- tures. The “Sociodemographic” dimension includes variables such as One-person household, Single-parent family, and gender, with the variable Female identifyin

such as One-person household, Single-parent family, and gender, with the variable Female identifying individuals of the female gender. individuals—given that income plays a crucial role in explaining energy poverty, as widely documented in the literature (Boardman, 1991; Bouzarovski et al., 2012). Another factor that exacerbates energy poverty is the type of housing tenure (Rent). Households that rent their homes are often discouraged from seeking solutions to improve the condition and energy efficiency of the dwelling. Additionally, higher education is included as a proxy for human capital and informational access, since educa

lly, higher education is included as a proxy for human capital and informational access, since educational attainment has been found to be inversely associated with the risk of energy poverty and may also correlate with greater awareness of energy-saving practices (Healy and Clinch, 2004). ial energy losses associated with different housing structures, which may shape the likelihood of experiencing energy poverty (Grey et al., 2017). For instance, the variable Apartments may be negatively associated with energy poverty, since these types of dwellings tend to have lower surface-to-volume ratios and shared walls, which reduce heat loss and improve energy efficiency. In contrast, the number of Bedrooms is expected to positively influence energy poverty as larger dwellings generally r

umber of Bedrooms is expected to positively influence energy poverty as larger dwellings generally require more energy for heating and cooling, increasing the risk of energy deprivation. the opposite is true. By applying this recursive bivariate probit model, we aim to provide a robust analysis of the causal relationship between energy poverty and health, offering a nuanced understanding of the factors contributing to health disparities within the context of energy affordability, focusing on the role of the social e

health disparities within the context of energy affordability, focusing on the role of the social energy tariff. This methodological approach allows for a comprehensive assessment of both direct and indirect effects, thereby informing targeted policy interventions and improving both energy access and health. The recursive bivariate probit model is particularly well-suited for situations with potential endogeneity where both the treatment and the outcome variables are binary, as in our case. Unl

ntial endogeneity where both the treatment and the outcome variables are binary, as in our case. Unlike instrumental vari- able methods or the Lewbel 2SLS approach—which either require strong external instruments or rely on heteroskedasticity conditions that can be difficult to verify—the recursive bivariate probit model allows for a more direct modeling of the joint determination of the two binary 6 E n e r g y P o li c y 206 (2025) 114762 outcomes. This approach accounts for unobserved factors

y 6 E n e r g y P o li c y 206 (2025) 114762 outcomes. This approach accounts for unobserved factors that simulta- neously affect both equations by estimating the correlation between their error terms. As a result, it enables us to capture potential endo- geneity without the need to rely on external instruments, which are often hard to justify in cross-sectional data and especially challenging in the context of social policy evaluations. Moreover, while alternative methods such as the special re

the context of social policy evaluations. Moreover, while alternative methods such as the special regressor approach are useful under certain conditions, they generally require the presence of a continuous, exogenous regressor with a large support and additional distributional assumptions, which are not easily met in our data. The recursive bivariate probit model provides a more robust and internally consistent framework given the nature of our variables and the structure of the dataset. 3.2. De

Descriptive statistics The descriptive statistics presented in Table 1 provide a useful overview of the sample’s characteristics and allow us to identify relevant patterns related to health and energy poverty. In terms of health, 69.5 % of the sample reports good, very good or excellent health, while 79.7 % express overall life satisfaction above the midpoint of the scale. How- ever, 32.2 % of indiv

79.7 % express overall life satisfaction above the midpoint of the scale. How- ever, 32.2 % of individuals experience limitations in daily life, and only 21.8 % report no difficulties remembering or concentrating. These fig- ures suggest that while general well-being and satisfaction are relatively high, a significant share of the population faces physical or cognitive limitations, which may be associated with socioeconomic or environ- mental stressors. In relation to energy deprivation, 16.5 %

sociated with socioeconomic or environ- mental stressors. In relation to energy deprivation, 16.5 % of the sample is classified as energy poor based on inadequate home temperature conditions, and 7.7 % report having experienced delays in energy bill payments. These relatively low but non-negligible rates indicate that energy poverty af- fects a meaningful portion of the population. Notably, only 7.8 % of households receive the social energy tariff, which suggests that either the eligibility crit

.8 % of households receive the social energy tariff, which suggests that either the eligibility criteria are restrictive or that the coverage of the subsidy is insufficient given the presence of energy deprivation. 4. Results and discussion In this section, we present the results from the recursive bivariate probit model, structured in three types of effects: social energy tariff on energy poverty, energy poverty on health, and health drivers including the indirect effect of social energy tariff

iew of how distributive, procedural, recog- nition, and capability interpretations of environmental justice are incorporated into renewable energy policy. Studies of environmental justice have also been performed for many individual countries, such as Bulgaria [17], Mexico [18–20], the United States [21,22], Austria [23], Brazil [24], Malawi [25], Morocco [26], and Benin, Senegal, and Togo [27]. However, there exist few examples in the literature of developing a cross-national perspective on RET conflicts linked to environmental injustice, in which past and present injustices guide recommendations for intervention and future prevention. Background: from global acceptance of renewable energy to environmental justice

nmental justice. 2. Background: from global acceptance of renewable energy to environmental justice challenges Renewable energy is gaining increased attention for its potential to reduce fossil fuel dependency on fossil fuels [34] and to mitigate climate change impacts [35,36]. This attention has led to rapid RET project installation worldwide. Global installed wind energy capacity increased from 198 GW in 2010 to 837 GW by 2021 [37], contributing 16.4 % of electricity utilized in Europe, 12.8 %

198 GW in 2010 to 837 GW by 2021 [37], contributing 16.4 % of electricity utilized in Europe, 12.8 % in China, and 9.2 % in the United States [38]. A report by the Global Wind Energy Council showed that over 341,000 wind turbines had been built globally as of 2022 [39]. Solar energy has seen similarly rapid growth, with the International Energy Agency [40] reporting that global installed solar photovoltaic 2 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 114(2024)103609 capacity reached

tovoltaic 2 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 114(2024)103609 capacity reached 820 GW in 2021. Geothermal energy, while less widely adopted than wind or solar, has potential to provide a consistent and reliable power source as demonstrated in Iceland and the Philippines [39]. Biomass, particularly that derived from wood and crop waste, provides a significant renewable energy contribution to rural and less industrialized areas. Adoption of biomass as a RET in these areas re-

contribution to rural and less industrialized areas. Adoption of biomass as a RET in these areas re- quires a less complex transition away from infrastructure powered by liquid fuels [41]. Despite renewable energy’s potential to make significant contribu- tions to global energy demand, large expanses of land are often required. For example, the United States’ target of meeting 20 % of 2030 elec- tricity demand with onshore wind energy would require more than 50,000 km2 of land use change [42]. H

ricity demand with onshore wind energy would require more than 50,000 km2 of land use change [42]. High land use requirements mean that RETs have been associated with various negative impacts, such as limited land use for local agriculture and community subsistence [43], biodiversity losses [44], visual impacts arising from changes in land- scapes [45], and claims of negative human health impacts caused by noise of operation and shadow flickering [46]. There also exist non- physical and social i

caused by noise of operation and shadow flickering [46]. There also exist non- physical and social impacts associated with RETs, such as a lost sense of place, culture, livelihood, and historical norms as communities are forced to reconcile with changes to their surroundings [10,47]. Addi- tionally, property values may decline due to negative perception of new RET projects, though the causal direction and the reality of this rela- tionship are both disputed [48,49]. Although these social impacts

tion and the reality of this rela- tionship are both disputed [48,49]. Although these social impacts are less tangible than impacts like biodiversity loss, their consequences for local RET acceptance [28] also represent environmental injustice to host communities. The exacerbation of such environmental injustice will, according to Fitzgerald [50], require building coalitions between justice activists as the global renewable energy transition accelerates. Environmental justice discourse became po

sts as the global renewable energy transition accelerates. Environmental justice discourse became popular in the late 20th Century for identification and assessment of the impacts of proposed or existing infrastructures on poor, marginalized, and racialized commu- nities [51]. The United States Environmental Protection Agency defines environmental justice as “the fair treatment and meaningful involvement of all people regardless of race, color, national origin, or income with respect to the deve

lvement of all people regardless of race, color, national origin, or income with respect to the development, implementation, and enforcement of environmental laws, regulations, and policies” [52,53]. This definition arguably places more emphasis on distributive environmental justice, which has necessitated recent studies exploring other environmental justice dimensions, such as procedural involvement and restorative healing of social and environ- mental damage to identifiable victims [54,55]. En

ent and restorative healing of social and environ- mental damage to identifiable victims [54,55]. Environmental justice also provides a means of understanding and strategizing decolonization efforts [56], with many non-Western and Indigenous perspectives now published that theorize previously disregarded or silenced pathways to environmental justice [57,58]. The prevailing consensus in the literature is that instances of exclusion, unfair treatment, or marginalization of individuals in decision-

ure is that instances of exclusion, unfair treatment, or marginalization of individuals in decision-making processes related to land use, in this case of RET development, may be indicative of environmental injustice. Mueller and Brooks [11] reported three main theoretical explanations for environmentally unjust development of infrastructures or projects: economic, social, and racial. The economic explanation poses that pur- suit of profit and return on investment drives industries to develop che

explanation poses that pur- suit of profit and return on investment drives industries to develop cheaper land that is often populated by poorer communities [59]. The social explanation is associated with the power of resistance, such that communities lacking resources to resist are more likely to be targets for development [60], a particular problem in countries with authoritarian governments that choose administrative efficiency over justice [61]. Finally, the racial explanation poses that infr

choose administrative efficiency over justice [61]. Finally, the racial explanation poses that infrastructural development is disproportionately located in places with majority Black, Indigenous, and People of Color (BIPOC) populations. As a result, majority-BIPOC communities are often exposed to higher energy-related health risks, such as pipeline spillages [62] and respiratory illnesses [63], while simultaneously subject to high rates of energy poverty [64]. Ensuring that RETs, or any infrast

hile simultaneously subject to high rates of energy poverty [64]. Ensuring that RETs, or any infrastructural development, do not cause environmental injustice (whether economic, social, or racial) necessi- tates thinking about how injustices can be avoided in the first place. To 2 C.C. Nsude et al. that end, we present Critical Restoration Geography (CRG) as a frame- work for how previous efforts to restore communities impacted by environmental justice to an equitable state can guide future rest

to restore communities impacted by environmental justice to an equitable state can guide future restoration and prevention efforts. We define CRG here as an applied effort to un- derstand the environmental, social, and political dimensions of restoring communities impacted by environmental injustice. Emerging examples of CRG thinking exist across the literature, particularly those focused on how restoration projects can reproduce or challenge existing power dynamics, such as those of race, gende

estoration projects can reproduce or challenge existing power dynamics, such as those of race, gender, and class inequality [65,66]. Doyle and Havlick [67] expanded the scope of CRG beyond physical and environmental features to extend to human elements such as economics, ethics, and politics. Additionally, Anderson and Woelfle-Erskine’s [68] study on pedagogies of CRG reported that the intertwined ecological and social dimensions of restoration should go beyond ecosystem repair, extending to the

ecological and social dimensions of restoration should go beyond ecosystem repair, extending to the support of human lives and well-being. Avoiding environmental injustice due to RET development would thus require an understanding of how humans interact with the land prior to develop- ment, as well as how the restoration of land to a sustainable and/or equitable state may coexist alongside RETs. Smith [69] similarly described restoration as requiring equal placement of value in humans and the e

Smith [69] similarly described restoration as requiring equal placement of value in humans and the environment in their interactions. As such, CRG’s emphasis on pre-emptively avoiding future environmental injustice must accommo- date ideas beyond simply restoring the land to a prior state (which might not have initially been just), such as new RET developments being coexistent with justice served. 3. Data and methods 3.1. Data source – global atlas of environmental justice (EJAtlas) We used EJAt

3. Data and methods 3.1. Data source – global atlas of environmental justice (EJAtlas) We used EJAtlas to collect documented cases of environmental injustice associated with conflicts in RET development from around the world. EJAtlas is an online tool created by the Rosa Luxemburg and Heinrich Bo¨ll Foundations in 2011, which allows users to explore and visualize global environmental justice issues, whether of agricultural practice, conservation, industrial activity, or renewable energy devel-

es, whether of agricultural practice, conservation, industrial activity, or renewable energy devel- opment [70]. EJAtlas contains details concerning over 3800 environ- mental conflicts and cases from more than 120 countries, with evidence for each case collected from news articles, academic papers, non- governmental reports, and other sources [71]. An in-house editorial team confirms the robustness of each source and updates the database frequently. EJAtlas allows users to search for environment

s of each source and updates the database frequently. EJAtlas allows users to search for environmental conflicts by location, type of conflict, and industry, while also filtering the data by year, country, and region. One of EJAtlas’ key advantages is its ability to highlight patterns of environmental injustice, since EJAtlas can illus- trate certain regions or communities that are disproportionately affected by specific conflicts. For instance, EJAtlas’ many documented examples of industrial an

ely affected by specific conflicts. For instance, EJAtlas’ many documented examples of industrial and utility-related injustices in China are consistent with the nation’s ongoing challenge to control air and water pollution [72], the same being true of ore mining injustices illustrated across nations in the Andes mountains [73]. This information can be used by academics, activists, and policymakers to support advocacy efforts and policy changes targeted at addressing environmental injustice [53]

s to support advocacy efforts and policy changes targeted at addressing environmental injustice [53]. It is also worth mentioning that conflicts documented in EJAtlas are generally grounded in objective verification and justified by empirical evidence, rather than being solely reliant on subjective perceptions. While EJAtlas can highlight patterns and convey overarching reasons for why RETs have historically caused environmental injustice, there are limitations to applying its database for our r

istorically caused environmental injustice, there are limitations to applying its database for our research purposes. One of the key limitations is EJAtlas’ scope, since its database relies on publicly available information that may not capture the full extent and frequency of environmental conflicts and protests. This limitation results in the underrepresentation of certain RETs in the database and limited exam- ples of conflicts in certain countries. Another limitation is that some communities

nd limited exam- ples of conflicts in certain countries. Another limitation is that some communities may also be less likely to report or document environ- mental injustices, which can lead to further underrepresentation in the 3 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 114(2024)103609 database. Furthermore, EJAtlas’ focus on conflicts and protests means that underlying causes of associated environmental injustice are not directly conveyed, such as structural inequalities, power i

sociated environmental injustice are not directly conveyed, such as structural inequalities, power imbalances, and environmental racism, hence the value of CRG for contextualizing the importance of the database’s contents [71]. All of these reasons place limits on the robustness of statistics derived using EJAtlas, as well as the global applicability of CRG to understanding RET conflicts and their associated injustices. 3.2. Data preprocessing - extraction, variables, and analysis We extracted g

associated injustices. 3.2. Data preprocessing - extraction, variables, and analysis We extracted global examples of environmental (in)justice associ- ated with RETs manually, starting from the year 2001 (EJAtlas’ earliest recorded RET conflict) through to 2023. Most of these extracted data were recent conflicts and reflect current global realities of injustices in RET development. Throughout the extraction process, we sought data about conflicts caused by four specific RET types: wind, solar,

extraction process, we sought data about conflicts caused by four specific RET types: wind, solar, biomass and geothermal. Given this study’s interest specifically in renewable energy resources, which are “replenished at a rate faster than they are consumed” [74], nuclear energy’s dependence on finite reserves of fissile metals [75,76] justifies its exclusion. Moreover, despite being an RET, we have excluded hydroelectricity due to existing studies of global hydroelectricity injustices using EJ

ave excluded hydroelectricity due to existing studies of global hydroelectricity injustices using EJAtlas [77,78]. EJAtlas documents environmental (in)justice pertaining to each of the four chosen RETs using 21 different variables, which we have summarized below in Table 1 under five broader categories (see Supplementary Material for RET conflict details extracted from EJAtlas): We also computed descriptive statistics to summarize documented RET conflicts and to consequently identify trends of g

escriptive statistics to summarize documented RET conflicts and to consequently identify trends of global environ- mental (in)justice in RET development and siting. Computed statistics include the frequency of conflicts per RET type reported in EJAtlas, presented as annual and cumulative numbers of RET conflicts, along with the country, geographical distribution, the affected population, conflict intensity, project status, and outcome of each conflict. While these statistics serve to identify gl

intensity, project status, and outcome of each conflict. While these statistics serve to identify global trends in when and where RET conflicts have been associated with environmental (in)justice, EJAtlas does not contain every single conflict to have ever occurred across the planet, and some of the available conflicts are not fully documented. However, the documented cases presented here still provide context for how CRG can assist in developing pre-emptive strategies for avoiding future enviro

ovide context for how CRG can assist in developing pre-emptive strategies for avoiding future environmental injustice. 3.3. Steps to critical restoration geography (CRG) policies We developed the CRG framework presented here from collective readings prepared for a symposium by CritRest, a forum of scholars and activists whose intersectional research cuts across racial, disability, and environmental justice movements [79]. We used this reading list, sum- marized below in Table 2, as a foundation

l justice movements [79]. We used this reading list, sum- marized below in Table 2, as a foundation for how the CRG framework was developed and could address issues relating to environmental jus- tice, transcending its prior applications to studies of land degradation and water pollution. Another goal of the reading list is to connect the past and future in the present through restoration geography, since repairing social and environmental damage and striving for justice both require presently m

y, since repairing social and environmental damage and striving for justice both require presently making restorative efforts through applying CRG [55]. Our CRG framework thus has important policy implications for resolving RET conflicts and thus ensuring environmental (specifically restorative) justice, including: • Consideration of the socio-environmental consequences of RET projects, thereby addressing potential conflicts and ensuring sus- tainable RET siting practices [80]. • Prioritizing eq

dressing potential conflicts and ensuring sus- tainable RET siting practices [80]. • Prioritizing equity through fair benefits and engaging with local communities, thus ensuring stable and responsible access to essential materials [81]. 3 C.C. Our CRG framework is similar to Sovacool and Dworkin’s [87] pre- sentation of a decision-making tool for identifying economic, cultural, environmental, and sociopolitical problems caused by energy use/pro- duction. We apply CRG

ultural, environmental, and sociopolitical problems caused by energy use/pro- duction. We apply CRG in a similar manner, though with the explicit intent of pre-emptively avoiding future cases of global environmental injustice caused by RETs. Our work achieves this goal by using CRG’s principles to explain successes and failures at achieving environmental justice documented in EJAtlas. We subsequently propose how decision- makers and actors engaged in RET development could use these same principl

tly propose how decision- makers and actors engaged in RET development could use these same principles to ensure that future RET projects do not perpetuate similar injustices before, during, or after development. We have derived seven principles that together comprise the CRG framework, and in doing so we have engaged with recommendations for performing good energy social science, such as generalizability of each principle at a global scale and grounding each principle in evidence from the liter

zability of each principle at a global scale and grounding each principle in evidence from the literature [88]. It should also be acknowledged that a framework that attempts to gener- alize pathways to environmental justice encounters issues of cross- national research, since prevailing causes of RET conflict may be country-specific, or because of differing national progress in making renewable energy transitions [89]. Nonetheless, each principle describes its own means of pre-emptively addressi

ergy transitions [89]. Nonetheless, each principle describes its own means of pre-emptively addressing potential and actual envi- ronmental injustices of RET development, thus providing planning and implementation guidance throughout an RET project’s life cycle. These principles are: 1. Recognize the role of power dynamics: Power dynamics are deeply embedded in RET development, especially in decision-making, ac- cess to resources, and in shaping priorities and narratives within RET siting. For e

ion-making, ac- cess to resources, and in shaping priorities and narratives within RET siting. For example, Heynen et al. [90] emphasized that power im- balances influence not only who participates in projects but also the distribution of costs and other benefits associated with the project. As such, understanding the flow of power dynamics by critically examining how power is exercised, negoti- ated, and contested throughout an RET projec

by critically examining how power is exercised, negoti- ated, and contested throughout an RET project’s life cycle is important in addressing any form of injustice and ensuring inclusive and effective governance [92,93]. 2. Acknowledge the interconnectedness of social and ecological systems: RETs have often been depicted as an ecological solution, though this platitude often neglects the social dynamics of RET development [94]. The CRG framework emphasizes that social and ecological components a

namics of RET development [94]. The CRG framework emphasizes that social and ecological components are inseparable and must be considered together for RET development. There must be explicit acknowledgement that human society is deeply intertwined with ecosystems, and that ecosystems do not exist in isolation from human activities [95]. For example, human actions such as land use change and resource extraction both have profound impacts on biodiversity and the behaviour and ecol- ogy of animals

ce extraction both have profound impacts on biodiversity and the behaviour and ecol- ogy of animals and plants, with changes to that same ecosystem exerting feedback and on food availability and general human well- being. 3. Adopt a place-based approach: In siting RETs, it is important to un- derstand, recognize and respect the unique characteristics, histories, and dynamics of chosen development sites [96]. By adopting a place- based approach, developers and project proponents must recognize th

sites [96]. By adopting a place- based approach, developers and project proponents must recognize that ecological processes, social dynamics, and cultural heritage are distinct and not generalizable. Questions such as “What are the his- torical, social, and environmental factors that have shaped the community?” and “What are the needs, challenges, and opportu- nities of the community?” all need to be explored. Considering a community’s values, aspirations, and priorities will help ensure that RE

be explored. Considering a community’s values, aspirations, and priorities will help ensure that RET development aligns with a community’s self-concept of place [97]. Furthermore, a place-based approach to RET development may facilitate a sense of place, connection, and stewardship between communities and all relevant actors. With this approach comes recognition of the relevance of cultural identity, cultural continuity, and a sense of belonging associated with the land [98]. 4. Incorporate mul

ity, cultural continuity, and a sense of belonging associated with the land [98]. 4. Incorporate multiple knowledge systems: This principle recognizes that RET development is not limited to scientific knowledge alone. Explicit value must be placed in the contributions and impact of Indigenous and local knowledge, which have a long-standing rela- tionship with the land [99]. Integrating scientific, local, and Indig- enous knowledge into RET development enhances the ecological and social well-bein

local, and Indig- enous knowledge into RET development enhances the ecological and social well-being of the community through the consequent incor- poration and empowerment of diverse perspectives on best practices [100]. Also, engaging multiple knowledge systems means developers can create a platform for dialogue, collaboration, co-learning, and a common shared decision-making forum among different knowledge holders. RET development can thus benefit from a more compre- hensive understanding of

ent knowledge holders. RET development can thus benefit from a more compre- hensive understanding of Indigenous and local knowledge, which often convey ecological insights, species interaction, ecosystem dy- namics, and traditional land management that modernist approaches to science cannot [101]. 4 C.C. Nsude et al. 5. Prioritize equity and social justice: Most communities have experi- enced some form of social, economic, or environmental injustice in their past that negatively impacted their w

form of social, economic, or environmental injustice in their past that negatively impacted their well-being and ecosystems [102]. Until these injustices are discussed and addressed, it would be unwise to develop an RET project within these same communities. This principle thus recognizes and understands the historical and current injustices that have impacted specific communities and de- mographics, which include but are not limited to the legacies of colonization, systemic discrimination, and

ics, which include but are not limited to the legacies of colonization, systemic discrimination, and the unequal distribution of resources and power. RET developers and project proponents must first understand before development begins how communities have been marginalized, as well as the environmental degradation and ecosystem service losses that have consequently occurred [103]. 6. Promote resilience: As RET development is likely to create distur- bances and disruptions, it is important to de

lience: As RET development is likely to create distur- bances and disruptions, it is important to develop measures that enable communities and ecosystems to adapt, persist, and reconso- lidate in the face of these challenges [104]. This principle emphasizes the need for developers to enhance the capacity of socio-ecological systems to withstand and recover from disruptions and distur- bances arising from RET development [105]. Distribution of environmental injustice cases across the four RETs Our extraction of documented RET conflicts returned 102 reported cases of environmental (in)justice caused by the four chosen RETs. These reported cases covered the years 2001 to 2021 (zero cases were reported for 2022 and 2023; nine of the 102 cases lacked a year of reported conflict), with the largest number pertaining to wind energy projects (48 out of 102

ear of reported conflict), with the largest number pertaining to wind energy projects (48 out of 102 cases); EJAtlas less commonly reported conflicts about solar (24), biomass (24) and geothermal (6) projects. Fig. 1a and b illustrates the cumulative total and annual frequency of these reported cases of RET conflict. While the cumulative total of reported cases has steadily increased, their annual frequency peaked between 2009 and 2012, and all cases until 2005 were for wind energy conflicts. Mo

frequency peaked between 2009 and 2012, and all cases until 2005 were for wind energy conflicts. eporting of RET conflicts to EJAtlas, the latter evidenced by the spatial distribution of reported conflicts in Fig. 2. Affected population and conflict intensity The nature and intensity of the impacts of RET conflicts on com- munities is shown to vary substantially in EJAtlas’ database. Of the 102 relevant conflicts, Table 3 shows that 78 of them (76 %) occurred in rural communities, most of which were wind energy projects (41 out of 78). The remaining conflicts were mostly r

ities, most of which were wind energy projects (41 out of 78). The remaining conflicts were mostly reported in semi-urban areas (21 %), comprised of near-equal numbers of wind (7 out of 21), solar (7) and biomass (6) projects. An interpretation of the global environmental (in)justices of RET using a CRG perspective would thus be most relevant to rural conflicts. This relevance is important given the number of commercial and residential RET projects that have historically been developed in rural

he number of commercial and residential RET projects that have historically been developed in rural communities [109,110]. Interpreting EJAtlas and the attainment of environmental justice Global interest in renewable energy development is largely driven by pressures to meet domestic energy demand and to address the threat of climate change, though EJAtlas reveals that these pressures often lead to inadvertent negative impacts and environmenta

ugh EJAtlas reveals that these pressures often lead to inadvertent negative impacts and environmental injustice in host com- munities. EJAtlas’ database specifically details the geographical condi- tions, consequences, and in some cases potential solutions to various forms of global environmental injustice, with injustices over RET con- flict being the focus of this work. Locations for common RET conflicts are Mexico and the United States, for which many examples exist in EJAtlas for wind and so

conflicts are Mexico and the United States, for which many examples exist in EJAtlas for wind and solar energy conflicts (Fig. 3), Mexico is charac- terized by uneven geographies of energy consumption, thought to contribute to a corresponding unevenness in RET conflicts. For example, affected communities have explicitly contested wind energy develop- ment in Mexico’s windiest regions due to preference to supply energy to several multinational corporations, rather than these local communities [19

ence to supply energy to several multinational corporations, rather than these local communities [19]. Moreover, the largest numbers of biomass energy conflicts have occurred in Kenya, Mozambique, and Sierra Leone (Fig. 3), countries in which extensive palm oil and sugarcane plantations are increasingly being used for biomass energy production, posing a threat to the rich biodiversity in these countries and thus a heightened risk of environ- mental injustice [114]. in achieving justice to thus critique purported efforts and outcomes. Each of these four pillars is summarized below, with examples given of each one in the context of RET conflict: 1) “Intersecting social categories, including race, class, gender, sexu- ality, and species.” This pillar accounts for the human domination of nonhuman species that results in ecocide and natural habitat destruction. Example: the land transformation required to build wind farms and valuing the consequent impacts on b

xample: the land transformation required to build wind farms and valuing the consequent impacts on both avian species and human discomfort. 2) “Multi-scalarity regarding space and temporality”, thereby recog- nizing that environmental injustice can happen over different time- frames and spatial domains, separately and simultaneously. Example: recognizing that biomass production’s impacts range from local biodiversity disturbance to potentially global consequences for atmospheric pollution. 3) “S

m local biodiversity disturbance to potentially global consequences for atmospheric pollution. 3) “State power that reproduces the social inequalities inherent to capitalism,” and the responsibility of state and national governments to recognize their role as actors that have historically and structur- ally enabled these inequalities.
