munity accep- tance, reflecting the need to understand interactions between siting decisions and public engagement. Interestingly, Cowell [237] found many UK gas projects have successfully reinhabited sites of former fossil fuel power stations, whereby developers mobilised arguments around the net environmental benefits of such projects, while encountering minimal local resistance about landscape impacts. At present, UK studies suggest mixed perspectives regarding the community impacts of domest

impacts. At present, UK studies suggest mixed perspectives regarding the community impacts of domestic hydrogen [65,78,168]. Van Alstine and Bastin [168] reported moderate to high levels of local acceptance for hydrogen stimulating a green economy in Leeds, but also noted high levels of uncertainty as to whether hydrogen technologies would benefit local communities, as opposed to large businesses (M =3.4).24 Relat- edly, Scott and Powells [65] noted strong concerns over socio-economic risks and

(M =3.4).24 Relat- edly, Scott and Powells [65] noted strong concerns over socio-economic risks and distributional injustice among survey respondents in the North of England, while focus group respondents from Manchester and Bir- mingham expressed reservations over exacerbating energy vulnerabil- ities [78], which has also been echoed in the Australian context [72]. Given the potential synergies between industrial and residential sectors in the context of the UK hydrogen transition (see Section

etween industrial and residential sectors in the context of the UK hydrogen transition (see Section 1), community acceptance should be examined in relation to place-specific dynamics [75,114,244]. While some initial insights on community acceptance have been generated, few follow-up studies have been con- ducted to validate pre-COVID 19 results. Moreover, there is limited evidence regarding the equity aspects of the hydrogen switchover [72, 78,176]. Similarly, efforts to explore the spatial dyna

uity aspects of the hydrogen switchover [72, 78,176]. Similarly, efforts to explore the spatial dynamics [245–247] and justice implications [224,248,249] of the energy transition continue to lag [75]. To help reverse this trend, Sections 5.7 and 5.8 engage with community acceptance through the lens of perceived benefits, costs and risks, in addition to spatial dynamics and energy justice. 3.4. sehold acceptance: temporary disconnection from the gas grid Should the domestic hydrogen transition receive policy support in the upcoming years [29], the switchover process would require house- holds to be temporarily disconnected from the gas grid while appliances are changed or upgraded [250]. Disruption levels will depend on the duration of disconnection, which may range from several hours to a few days [78,162,185]. A nationally representative UK survey (N =2000) conducted by the Decarboni

a few days [78,162,185]. A nationally representative UK survey (N =2000) conducted by the Decarbonised Gas Alliance [162] provides the only available quantita- tive evidence on this aspect, finding 39 % of respondents were ’uncon- cerned’ about being without gas ‘for a few days in summer’ compared to 34 % expressing ’concern’. Additionally, the researchers [162] con- ducted four focus groups (N =32) split between the North of England and Greater London, which suggested concerns over short-term

32) split between the North of England and Greater London, which suggested concerns over short-term disruption could be mitigated and appeased in most cases. groups (N =39), representing consumers from different housing segments in Manchester and Bir- mingham, Grey et al. [78] specified a hypothetical scenario in which 300 homes would be switched to hydrogen in the summer over a three-to-four-day period. ents expected the disconnection period to last a few hours, which resulted in minimal qualitative evidence on tolerance limits. Integrated mixed-methods multigroup analysis In comparison to strictly qualitative or quantitative analysis [267–269], mixed-methods research offers a more complete approach for engaging with complex societal issues [270]. Traditio

s research offers a more complete approach for engaging with complex societal issues [270]. Traditionally, mixed-methods research is conducted via separate qualitative and quantitative stages prior to integration [269,271], however, a conver- gent method (i.e. bidirectional approach) [272] can also be achieved within a single stage of data collection to merge both ‘strands’ of evi- dence more directly [270]. Through this approach, integrated mixed-methods analysis [273] can support ‘narrative we

ctly [270]. Through this approach, integrated mixed-methods analysis [273] can support ‘narrative weaving’ [269, 274]; whereby qualitative and quantitative results are merged themat- ically [271] to enhance the scope of findings [273]. Focus groups provide a powerful research method for conducting qualitative social science research [270,275], with strong applicability for examining public acceptance of emerging energy technologies [276, 277] and attitudes toward the energy transition [278,279].

nce of emerging energy technologies [276, 277] and attitudes toward the energy transition [278,279]. Notably, Sandri et al. [72] and Smith et al. [256] also employed focus groups to extract insights on different aspects of hydrogen acceptance. Perceptions of purchasing costs Purchasing a new gas boiler is a highly context-specific decision which is often triggered by system breakdown, or usually delayed until the appliance reaches the end of its lifespan (i.e. 12–15 years), especially for low-income earners [183]. Absent of environmental pressures and forthcoming regulations, it follows that a large proportion of the UK housing stock could feasibly continue using their current boile

ws that a large proportion of the UK housing stock could feasibly continue using their current boiler into the mid-2030s. Perceptions of energy suppliers To better gauge their experience with and attitudes towards energy companies, participants were asked to confirm if they had previously changed supplier, and to share any relevant details regarding this de- cision and the process. The rationale for introducing this topic was to ascertain how consumers would feel about potentially changing to

for introducing this topic was to ascertain how consumers would feel about potentially changing to a hydrogen-specific provider in the future, and to invite reflections on recent events in UK energy markets. Previously, it has been shown that “widespread consumer disengagement in the energy market remains an obstacle to net-zero ambitions and climate change targets” [73]. that a high proportion of UK consumers remain disengaged with energy markets [184], especially in terms of switching energy supplier and tariff [203]. Introduction Responsible for only 1.3 % of global greenhouse gas emissions but being the highest per capita producer of emissions in the world due to fossil fuel exports [5], Australia is facing a significant challenge in renewable energy transition. Under the Paris Agreement, Australia initially set a reduction target of 26–28 % below the 2005 levels by 2030. In 2022, t

ent, Australia initially set a reduction target of 26–28 % below the 2005 levels by 2030. In 2022, the Commonwealth Government increased that target by legislation to 43 % (section 10 of the Climate Change Act 2022). If Australia is to meet its emissions reduction target, it will need to generate more than 80 % of its electricity from renewables by 2030 [24]. This will represent a significant increase from the 35 % current elec- tricity generation from renewables in 2023 [6]. Australia is not ye

ease from the 35 % current elec- tricity generation from renewables in 2023 [6]. Australia is not yet on track to meet its 2030 emissions reduction target [25]. Australia is a world leader in domestic solar adoption, with 3.9 million homes as of September 2024 having solar panels [8,87]. Adop- tion of solar is facilitated because most houses are detached, semi- detached or townhouses, with only 16 % being apartments [4]. ion in recent years, 2.8 million owner-occupied houses remain without solar, and two-thirds of new houses are being built without solar [87]. et al. data in Sydney, Australia, over the period 2013 to 2024, we apply fixed effects panel regression alongside Poisson and negative binomial models for installation counts, and Tobit and ordinary least squares models for system capacity. Our results indicate that a 10 % increase in installation costs leads to a 6.69 % decrease in installations, while a 10 % rise in electricity prices is associated with a 10.43 % increase in uptake. Similar patterns are observed for system capacity: a 10 % in

ated with a 10.43 % increase in uptake. Similar patterns are observed for system capacity: a 10 % increase in installation costs reduces capacity by 8.5 %, whereas a 10 % rise in electricity prices boosts capacity by 13.5 %. Conversely, feed-in tariffs are found to have an insignificant effect on both uptake and capacity. A novel contribution of this research is the explicit inclusion of housing affordability metrics—specifically the price-to-income and price-to-rent ratios—as well as a rental a

ffordability metrics—specifically the price-to-income and price-to-rent ratios—as well as a rental affordability metric, the rent-to- income ratio, in the panel analysis. This is supported by a strong theo- retical framework that links housing to energy justice, capital con- straints, and tenure-based exclusion within broader socio-technical and social processes [37,72]. While previous studies on solar uptake widely focus on household income, wealth and cash assets [2,11,14,15], few studies have

solar uptake widely focus on household income, wealth and cash assets [2,11,14,15], few studies have incorporated housing and rental affordability into consid- eration in solar uptake. As housing is the single largest financial asset for many Australian households [70], housing costs (i.e., mortgage or rent payments) play a crucial role in linking household behaviour and technological advancement within socio-technical systems, being an important social justice aspect about the injustices and in

within socio-technical systems, being an important social justice aspect about the injustices and inequalities of sustainable energy transitions [18,66,73]. Our findings reveal that housing affordability is a critical determinant of solar uptake. A 10 % increase in the price-to-income ratio reduces the number of installations by 8.39 % and installed capacity by 5.35 %. Similarly, a 10 % increase in the price-to-rent ratio results in a 6.91 % decline in installation numbers and a 3.95 % reduction

n the price-to-rent ratio results in a 6.91 % decline in installation numbers and a 3.95 % reduction in capacity. In contrast, rental affordability as indicated by the rent-to-income ratio shows no statistically significant effect. This suggests that renters may face structural barriers to solar adoption, as their ability to participate in clean energy transitions is largely determined by landlord decisions rather than their financial capacity or preferences. Given that housing affordability eme

decisions rather than their financial capacity or preferences. Given that housing affordability emerges as a key barrier to house- hold solar adoption—one unlikely to be resolved in the short term—this paper explores practical policy solutions to address it. Even with existing government rebates and financial incentives, many households face financial constraints that limit their ability to cover the upfront costs of solar installation, particularly low-income homeowners [81,82]. Addi- tionally

the upfront costs of solar installation, particularly low-income homeowners [81,82]. Addi- tionally, renters encounter structural barriers, as they lack ownership control over the properties they live in, further hindering their partici- pation in the clean energy transition. To overcome these challenges, we propose a ‘Use It or Lend It’ solar program. Under this scheme, home- owners and investors would have the option to permit the government to install and maintain solar systems on their roof

s would have the option to permit the government to install and maintain solar systems on their rooftops, without bearing any financial or maintenance responsibilities. Our findings provide policy- makers with evidence of the long-term affordability challenges facing solar uptake and underscore the need for innovative, targeted in- terventions to ensure more equitable access to renewable energy. The Small-Scale Renewable Energy Scheme (SRES) The uptake of domestic rooftop solar panels during the sample period (January 2013 – April 2024) was incentivised b

omestic rooftop solar panels during the sample period (January 2013 – April 2024) was incentivised by the SRES. The SRES is an economic instrument created by the Renewable Energy (Electricity) Act 2000 (Commonwealth) and administered by the Clean Energy Regulator (CER), a non-corporate Commonwealth entity established by 2 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 129 (2025) 104335 the Australian Government. The legislation established a renewable energy target, which by the time of

e Australian Government. The legislation established a renewable energy target, which by the time of the sample period had been sepa- rated into a large-scale target and the SRES. Under the SRES, the owners of eligible systems under 100 kW can claim Small-Scale Technology Certificates (STCs). These are usually assigned to a registered installation agent in exchange for a discount on the installation cost. For example, a typical 6.6 kW system may cost $8500, but the consumer only pays approximate

on cost. For example, a typical 6.6 kW system may cost $8500, but the consumer only pays approximately $6200. The $2300 difference is the amount that the retailer receives from a registered installation agent for the assignment of the STCs. Note that the discount received by purchasers of rooftop solar systems is not a government subsidy. Rather, the $2300 discount for the installation of the photo- voltaic system is the market price for STCs reflecting the amount that competitive retailers are

o- voltaic system is the market price for STCs reflecting the amount that competitive retailers are willing to pay to generate the amount of renewable energy required by the legislation. Commonly referred to as a rebate, the SRES created financial incentives for electricity retailers to increase the amount of renewable energy that they sell each year [21,49,58,80]. The renewable energy generation target stated for 2020 in the Renewable Energy (Electricity) Act 2000 was reached a year in advance.

target stated for 2020 in the Renewable Energy (Electricity) Act 2000 was reached a year in advance. A review in 2018 found that the CER was administering the renewable energy target (including the SRES) well [7]. The SRES appears to have achieved its objective, namely of acting as a short-medium-term catalyst for renewables investment until solar becomes the cheapest, longer-term option for those Sydney households able to invest in it [87]. As recently argued by Schuetz [67], there is a research gap in the urban economics literature on the link between housing decisions and climate change, which could inform the timely formulation of policies. context with a time dimension. Our research addresses these gaps by incorporating housing and rental affordability as a central factor, using longitudinal panel data to capture broader trends and behaviours across different postal areas. 3. Empirical estimation strategies 3.1. The models To examine solar panel uptake, we employ a panel data fixed effects regression model, which accounts for unobserved effects that may correlate with explanatory variables. We analyse the number of solar installat

rved effects that may correlate with explanatory variables. We analyse the number of solar installations and solar system capacity separately at the postcode level. Since the number of installations is a count variable, we employ count regression models [e.g., [39,65]], specified as follows: INSit=exp(+β 1Affordability it+Xit+γ i+δ t)+μ jt. (1) where INSit denotes the number of small-scale solar panel installations in postcode i at month t. Affordability represents a set of affordability it meas

anel installations in postcode i at month t. Affordability represents a set of affordability it measures for postcode i at month t, including price-to-income, price-to- rent, and rent-to-income ratios. Xit includes a set of key market de- terminants that may affect solar panel uptake, such as solar installation cost, electricity prices, and feed-in tariffs. γ controls for the postcode i fixed effect, capturing time-invariant local characteristics; δ t captures year and seasonal fixed effect, acc

ct, capturing time-invariant local characteristics; δ t captures year and seasonal fixed effect, accounting for temporal trends. μ is the jt error term. Standard errors are robust and clustered by postcodes. The equation is first estimated using a Poisson regression, assuming that INSit follows a Poisson distribution. However, if overdispersion is detected, i.e., the variance of INSit exceeds its mean, the standard Poisson assumption is violated. In such cases, we use Negative Binomial Maximum L

mean, the standard Poisson assumption is violated. In such cases, we use Negative Binomial Maximum Loglikelihood (NB ML) and Negative Binomial Quasi- Maximum Loglikelihood (NB QML) models as alternatives to relax the overdispersion restriction and the distributional assumption of INSit in the Poisson regression. To avoid potential multicollinearity among the affordability measures, we include them one at a time in the regression analysis. For the installed solar system capacity, the distribution

one at a time in the regression analysis. For the installed solar system capacity, the distribution of capacity observations piles up at zero, which clearly cannot have a conditional normal distribution. For this reason, we employ a censored regression model specified as follows: ln(CAPit)=β 0+β 1Affordability it+Xit+γ i+δ t+ϵ jt. (2) Where CAPit is the sum of small-scale solar system capacity installed in postcode i at month t, and ϵ jt is the error term assuming following a 3 S. Shi et al. no

stalled in postcode i at month t, and ϵ jt is the error term assuming following a 3 S. Shi et al. normal distribution with a mean of zero and constant variance. All other variables are defined as in Eq. (1). Eq. (2)is estimated using a Tobit model, which is suitable for dealing with censored data. Note that the Tobit model estimates the relationship for the latent CAPit*, not the observed CAPit. The Tobit coefficients reflect both the possibility of being uncensored and the expected value of CAP

he Tobit coefficients reflect both the possibility of being uncensored and the expected value of CAPit when uncensored. For comparison purposes, we also estimate a linear Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) model. OLS regressions are applied to both the full sample and a restricted subsample that excludes zero- capacity observations. While OLS regression may produce biased esti- mates in the presence of censoring, it still might be a good approxima- tion for the marginal effects, especially when censor

censoring, it still might be a good approxima- tion for the marginal effects, especially when censoring is not severe, and covariates are centred around their mean [79]. By employing postcode fixed effects in the panel regression, we aim to produce a consistent estimator by removing unobserved locational amenities, housing types (e.g., freestanding vs. apartments), roof suit- ability, and demographic factors that remain relatively stable within each postcode over time. Time-fixed effects are inc

hic factors that remain relatively stable within each postcode over time. Time-fixed effects are included to account for broader economic and environmental conditions, such as inflation, weather patterns, and seasonal variations (e.g., December or January effects). Compared to random effect models, the fixed effect model is preferred, as unobserved postcode characteristics such as locality attri- butes and demographic factors are likely correlated with affordability measures in our analysis. Unl

butes and demographic factors are likely correlated with affordability measures in our analysis. Unlike studies that include postal socioeco- nomic or demographic factors in cross-sectional analyses of solar panel diffusion [e.g., [48,85]], we address the time-varying affordability is- sues using a panel data time series approach, where we rely on postcode fixed effects to account for the slowly evolving demographic charac- teristics in our analysis. Other influences—such as peer or neighbour ef

volving demographic charac- teristics in our analysis. Other influences—such as peer or neighbour effects [38,57], marketing or information campaigns [63], and limits imposed by network companies across suburbs—are generally delin- eated by postcodes and will be controlled for using postcode fixed effects. Endogeneity due to reverse causality—where solar adoption may influence housing prices through green premiums—is unlikely to pose a significant concern in this study. This is because it typica

gh green premiums—is unlikely to pose a significant concern in this study. This is because it typically takes several months or even years for dwellings with new solar installations to be sold and reflected in market prices. As a result, our housing affordability metrics are implicitly lagged in time relative to the solar PV adoption data observed in the same month, which helps mitigate concerns about reverse causation. Similar considerations have been adopted in prior studies, such as Best et a

bout reverse causation. Similar considerations have been adopted in prior studies, such as Best et al. [10], which employ explicit lagged variables to address potential endogeneity. Finally, to account for potential dependence among observations within the same postcode, we cluster the standard errors at the postcode level, allowing for possible serial correlations over time within each postcode. In addition, we employ robust standard errors to further address potential misspecification of the v

In addition, we employ robust standard errors to further address potential misspecification of the variance and distributional assumptions in the analysis [e.g., [19]]. 4. Data 4.1. Introduction Energy justice has become a central concept in the transition to sustainable urban energy systems [1,2]. As cities globally confront the twin issues of climate change and social equity, ensuring fair access to affordable, reliable, and clean energy has never been more critical [3–5]. However, quantifying energy justice remains a complex task, hindering the implementati

itical [3–5]. However, quantifying energy justice remains a complex task, hindering the implementation and evaluation of energy policies within an equitable framework [6,7]. This study aims to address this challenge by proposing an initial set of quantitative metrics to measure energy justice in urban contexts, with a specific focus on Washington, D.C. as a case study. In this context, urban settings refer to densely populated metropolitan areas characterized by concentrated populations, diverse

ngs refer to densely populated metropolitan areas characterized by concentrated populations, diverse housing types (from single-family homes to large apartment complexes), and centralized utility infrastructure. These settings present unique energy justice challenges due to their spatial concentration of socio- economic disparities and complex utility service networks [4,8–10]. * Corresponding author at: George Washington University, 2101 F Street, Wash E-mail addresses: ibrahim.ahmed@gwmail.gwu

Measuring energy justice is essential for identifying and addressing disparities in energy systems, facilitating city comparisons on energy equity progress, and assessing the effectiveness of policies designed to improve justice [11]. Quantitative metrics serve as a bridge between theoretical concepts of energy justice a

ustice [11]. Quantitative metrics serve as a bridge between theoretical concepts of energy justice and practical implementation, providing measurable indicators to track progress. Despite these bene- fits, attempts to quantify energy justice are still in their early stages, largely due to the conceptual and qualitative nature of most discussions on the topic [12]. The multidimensional aspects of energy justi- ce—distributive (focusing on fair allocation of energy resources and burdens), recognit

nergy justi- ce—distributive (focusing on fair allocation of energy resources and burdens), recognition (acknowledging diverse needs and vulnerabilities of different communities), and procedural (ensuring inclusive and fair decision-making processes)—add further complexity [1], as does the lack of standardized metrics for urban settings, which complicates comparative analyses and best practice development [13]. hington, D.C. 20052, United States. nekingsley.effiong@gwmail.gwu.edu (E. This research offers an initial and important step toward overcoming these obstacles by introducing a set of conceptually developed metrics. These metrics include measuring energy burdens across demographics, assessing renewable energy acces

These metrics include measuring energy burdens across demographics, assessing renewable energy access, analyzing service disconnection rates, calculating system reliability, evaluating utility affordability programs, and gauging public participation in energy decision-making [4,14]. While these metrics are not yet definitive, they represent a foundational approach to quantifying energy justice that can evolve with further research and application. Washington D.C. serves as an ideal context for t

t can evolve with further research and application. Washington D.C. serves as an ideal context for this evaluation due to its diverse socio-economic profile, ambitious renewable energy goals, and documented history of racial and economic inequities in energy access and affordability [15]. The city's energy landscape, characterized by a mix of low-income neighborhoods and rapidly gentrifying areas, presents a compelling case for analyzing how energy burdens, afford- ability, and decision-making p

presents a compelling case for analyzing how energy burdens, afford- ability, and decision-making processes intersect. Investigating these dynamics in the District provides valuable insights that can inform strategies to promote equitable energy transitions in other urban areas. By establishing a quantitative framework for energy justice, this study aims to equip policymakers, researchers, and advocates with practical tools to assess and address energy inequities in urban envi- ronments. This a

vocates with practical tools to assess and address energy inequities in urban envi- ronments. This approach ensures that the pursuit of sustainability does not come at the expense of social equity, promoting an inclusive tran- sition to clean energy that benefits all communities [16]. The remainder of this analysis is structured as follows: Section 2 provides a review of the literature on energy justice and existing ap- proaches to its measurement. In Section 3, we describe the research methodol

ustice and existing ap- proaches to its measurement. In Section 3, we describe the research methodology and analytical framework used to evaluate energy justice. Section 4presents the results and discusses the findings of the case study based on Washington, D.C. Section 5 lays out the conclusion, offers recommendations for policy, and suggests future research directions for advancing energy justice. 2. Literature review This section explores the foundational theories and current research on ener

e. 2. Literature review This section explores the foundational theories and current research on energy justice, focusing on frameworks that define equitable energy distribution and decision-making. It first reviews the evolution of energy justice principles, examining how researchers have operationalized distributive, recognition, and procedural dimensions through various metrics. The review then analyzes gaps in existing approaches, partic- ularly the challenges of developing standardized measu

analyzes gaps in existing approaches, partic- ularly the challenges of developing standardized measurements in urban settings and translating theoretical frameworks into practical tools. This analysis connects theoretical insights to our present study, which aims to develop an integrated framework for assessing energy equity in Wash- ington, D.C. 2.1. Theoretical foundations of energy justice The concept of energy justice builds upon the foundation of envi- ronmental and climate justice, extendi

concept of energy justice builds upon the foundation of envi- ronmental and climate justice, extending the discourse beyond basic energy access to include the equitable distribution of energy-related benefits and burdens [1]. The theoretical basis of energy justice is founded on three core principles: distributive, recognition, and proce- dural justice [12]. Distributive justice emphasizes the fair allocation of energy resources and burdens among different societal groups, addressing the questio

r allocation of energy resources and burdens among different societal groups, addressing the question of who benefits from energy access and who bears the costs of energy-related issues like high prices or environmental degradation. Recognition justice involves acknowledging and respecting the diverse energy needs and vulnerabilities of marginalized commu- nities, ensuring that historically disadvantaged groups are not over- looked or further marginalized. Procedural justice emphasizes the impor

antaged groups are not over- looked or further marginalized. Procedural justice emphasizes the importance of fairness and inclusivity in decision-making processes related to energy systems [1,17]. Sovacool and Dworkin [18] expanded on these principles to propose a broader framework that captures availability, affordability, due process, transparency and accountability, 2 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 122 (2025) 103986 sustainability, intergenerational equity, and respon

h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 122 (2025) 103986 sustainability, intergenerational equity, and responsibility. This expanded framework offers a more nuanced approach to understanding the multidimensional nature of energy justice. Over the years, researchers have made efforts to measure different aspects of energy justice [3,17,19,20]. For example, Herna´ndez [4] used energy burden, which represents the proportion of household income allocated to energy expenses, as a key indicator of distributive

the proportion of household income allocated to energy expenses, as a key indicator of distributive justice, particularly in studies related to energy poverty and vulnerability [15,21,22]. Carley and Konisky [14] explored procedural justice by examining public involvement and stakeholder engagement in energy decision-making processes, highlighting the significance of inclusive governance in achieving just energy outcomes. Similarly, Fuller and McCauley [16] focused on procedural and recognition

ving just energy outcomes. Similarly, Fuller and McCauley [16] focused on procedural and recognition justice, evalu- ating how community energy projects can empower marginalized groups and facilitate grassroots energy transitions. Regarding recogni- tion justice, Finley-Brook & Holloman [23] examined how marginalized communities' energy needs are systematically overlooked in energy transition planning, demonstrating how standard metrics fail to capture unique vulnerabilities faced by low-income

ning, demonstrating how standard metrics fail to capture unique vulnerabilities faced by low-income communities and commu- nities of color. The application of energy justice frameworks in urban areas has offered valuable insights into the specific challenges cities encounter in promoting equitable energy transitions. Reames [15] provided one of the most detailed empirical analyses of energy inequities, examining residential energy efficiency disparities based on race and socioeco- nomic status i

ties, examining residential energy efficiency disparities based on race and socioeco- nomic status in U.S. cities. Through spatial analysis of residential heat- ing efficiency in Kansas City, Reames found that predominantly minority and low-income neighborhoods faced significantly higher energy use intensities compared to more affluent, predominantly white neighbor- hoods. Specifically, minority neighborhoods showed 27 % higher energy use intensity compared to predominantly white neighborhoods,

eighborhoods showed 27 % higher energy use intensity compared to predominantly white neighborhoods, even after controlling for income levels and housing age. These disparities were attributed to systemic factors including older housing stock, his- torical patterns of disinvestment in minority neighborhoods, and limited access to energy efficiency upgrades. The study demonstrated that effi- ciency gaps were not merely a function of income, but were significantly influenced by racial segregation p

gaps were not merely a function of income, but were significantly influenced by racial segregation patterns and neighborhood character- istics, emphasizing the need to address historical patterns of segregation and lack of investment when developing energy policies. In a different context, Bouzarovski and Tirado Herrero [24] mapped energy poverty across European cities, demonstrating how political and economic transitions have created distinct spatial patterns of energy vulnerability. Their anal

and economic transitions have created distinct spatial patterns of energy vulnerability. Their analysis highlighted the unique challenges faced by post-socialist cities, where the shift from centralized to market-based energy systems necessitated tailored approaches. These included ac- counting for factors like district heating infrastructure and rapid housing privatization, which are crucial for understanding energy poverty within the broader EU context. Building on these individual studies, K

understanding energy poverty within the broader EU context. Building on these individual studies, Kontokosta et al. [25] attemp- ted to develop a more comprehensive framework by analyzing 107 U.S. cities and establishing a set of energy equity indicators, including energy burden, energy insecurity, and household energy efficiency. Their work represents one of the few attempts to establish a standardized frame- work for assessing urban energy justice across different dimensions. The development

ardized frame- work for assessing urban energy justice across different dimensions. Second, much of the existing literature relies heavily on qualitative methods [26–30]. While qual

s. Second, much of the existing literature relies heavily on qualitative methods [26–30]. While qualitative assessments are valuable, they often lack the easy comparability needed to evaluate energy justice across different cities and time periods [13,31]. Third, the methodological limitations of existing studies are clearly demonstrated in their context-specific nature. For example, while Reames' analysis provided crucial insights into energy efficiency dis- parities in Kansas City, its methodo

analysis provided crucial insights into energy efficiency dis- parities in Kansas City, its methodology was necessarily tailored to that city's particular characteristics, including its patterns of racial segrega- tion, housing stock age distribution, and specific history of neighbor- hood disinvestment [15]. factors unique to those contexts, such as district heating systems and the legacy of centralized energy planning, making direct application to other urban settings problematic [11]. These context-specific approaches, while valuable for understanding particular cases, have created challenges for developing standardized metrics that can be applied consistently across different urban envi- ronments. Even Kontokosta's attempt at a standardized framework across 107 cities faced limitations in accoun

Even Kontokosta's attempt at a standardized framework across 107 cities faced limitations in accounting for local variations in energy systems, housing markets, and socioeconomic conditions [25]. Fourth, the failure to incorporate recognition justice into quantita- tive frameworks is particularly pronounced, with most studies priori- tizing distributive and procedural elements while overlooking specific vulnerabilities of marginalized groups. pecific vulnerabilities of marginalized groups. ies of community energy pro- jects [16] or how energy transition planning tends to overlook low in- come communities and communities of color [23]. Research methodology and analytical framework This study develops a quantitative approach to measure energy jus- tice in Washington D.C.'s urban energy system through an integrated framework of metrics and analysis

ce in Washington D.C.'s urban energy system through an integrated framework of metrics and analysis methods. The framework (Fig. 1), evaluates energy justice through three fundamental dimensions: distributive, recognition, and procedural justice. Each dimension is assessed using specific metrics that capture different aspects of energy equity within the urban context. The framework begins with comprehensive data collection and processing, followed by systematic analysis through the three justice

prehensive data collection and processing, followed by systematic analysis through the three justice dimensions. We used descriptive quantitative analysis, multi-source data synthesis, and urban context adaptation to process the data collected. While each metric provides quantitative measurements, this study does not attempt to combine these metrics into a composite score or establish universal thresholds for energy justice assessment. This choice reflects both data constraints (varying geograph

resholds for energy justice assessment. This choice reflects both data constraints (varying geographic resolutions) and the complex, interconnected nature of energy justice dimensions that resist simple aggregation. oach enables both individual assessment of specific justice aspects and comprehensive understanding of overall energy equity in the District's energy system. While ward-level analysis would be ideal for all metrics to enable consistent spatial comparisons, the current data landscape requires working across different geographic scales. The following sections detail each metric's methodology, data sources, and analytical approach. 3.3. Distributive justice The distributive justice analysis in this

ources, and analytical approach. 3.3. Distributive justice The distributive justice analysis in this study focuses on equitable allocation of energy resources and burdens across Washington D.C.'s population. Two primary metrics are employed to evaluate distributive justice: energy burden and renewable energy distribution. Through these metrics, the study examines both the financial impacts of energy costs on households and access to clean energy resources across different socio-economic groups a

gy costs on households and access to clean energy resources across different socio-economic groups and geographic areas. 3.3.1. Energy burden Energy burden measures the portion of a household's total income spent on energy expenses, is an important indicator of energy afford- ability, and provides a popular metric for assessing distributive energy justice [15]. It highlights the economic disparities across different de- mographic groups. High energy burdens signify that households allocate a dis

es across different de- mographic groups. High energy burdens signify that households allocate a disproportionately large portion of their income to meet basic energy requirements, worsening financial pressure and contributing to broader energy insecurity. A household is deemed to have a high energy burden if its energy expenditure exceeds 6 % and severe if it surpasses 10 % [34,36,37]. This study adopts the methodology used in the American Council for an Energy-Efficient Economy's (ACEEE) energ

adopts the methodology used in the American Council for an Energy-Efficient Economy's (ACEEE) energy burden reports [34,36,38]. The data was sourced from the 2019 American Housing Survey (AHS) for the Washington, D.C., metropolitan statistical area (MSA). The AHS data includes household-level income, energy costs (electricity, natural gas, and other heating fuels), and demographic de- tails, allowing for a comprehensive analysis of energy burden dispar- ities. The energy burden calculations rep

wing for a comprehensive analysis of energy burden dispar- ities. The energy burden calculations represent gross energy costs relative to income, as the dataset captures total household energy ex- penditures regardless of whether households receive assistance through programs like LIHEAP or benefit from solar initiatives. For this study, energy burden calculations excluded households that do not directly pay for their energy bills or report a positive income. While the dataset does not allow us

irectly pay for their energy bills or report a positive income. While the dataset does not allow us to isolate the individual impacts of utility assistance pro- grams or renewable energy benefits, this comprehensive view helps identify areas where targeted interventions are most needed, even as some households may already receive support through various pro- grams. The net impact of these assistance programs is separately analyzed in Section 4.4, providing insight into how existing in- terventio

ce programs is separately analyzed in Section 4.4, providing insight into how existing in- terventions affect household energy burdens. While this metric is widely recognized and easy to calculate, it is important to stress that it does not account for non-monetary aspects of energy insecurity, such as poor housing quality or lack of energy effi- ciency, which may increase consumption and raise costs for low-income households. Thus, while it serves as a critical indicator of distributive injusti

sts for low-income households. Thus, while it serves as a critical indicator of distributive injustice, energy burden should be interpreted alongside other metrics to provide a holistic view of energy inequity. 5 E n e r g y R e s e a r c h & S o c i a l S c i e n c e 122 (2025) 103986 3.3.2. Distribution of renewable energy systems The deployment of solar energy systems, particularly the distribution of solar panel installations across wards, serves as a key metric for evaluating the equity of

tion of solar panel installations across wards, serves as a key metric for evaluating the equity of the clean energy transition [39]. This study uses data on solar panels installations gathered from the District's Depart- ment of Energy and Environment (DOEE) renewable energy reports and the Solar for All (SFA) program documentation to analyze the geographic and demographic spread of solar installations. This approach enables an assessment of renewable energy benefits, such as reduced energy bil

ations. This approach enables an assessment of renewable energy benefits, such as reduced energy bills and improved sustainability, and whether they are being fairly distributed across Washington, D.C. This metric aligns closely with distributive justice, as it highlights whether historically marginalized communities are gaining access to the economic and environmental benefits of solar energy. However, focusing on installations alone may overlook systemic barriers to adoption, such as high upfr

wever, focusing on installations alone may overlook systemic barriers to adoption, such as high upfront costs or lack of ownership in rental housing, which can prevent low-income house- holds from participating in renewable energy programs. Consequently, while solar deployment is an important indicator of progress toward sustainability, it should be evaluated alongside policies and programs aimed at overcoming these barriers. 3.4. Recognition justice Recognition justice focuses on acknowledging

at overcoming these barriers. 3.4. Recognition justice Recognition justice focuses on acknowledging and addressing the diverse needs and vulnerabilities of different community groups within the energy system. This study employs three metrics to evaluate recognition justice: service disconnections, system reliability through the SAIDI index, and participation in utility affordability programs. These metrics collectively assess how well the energy system recognizes and responds to the needs of vul

e metrics collectively assess how well the energy system recognizes and responds to the needs of vulnerable populations. 3.4.1. Service disconnections Service disconnections, referring to the number of households whose energy services are terminated due to non-payment, are used as a direct indicator of energy accessibility. These data were gathered from PEPCO utility provider records submitted to the Public Service Commission's e- docket system. Service disconnections are particularly useful in

the Public Service Commission's e- docket system. Service disconnections are particularly useful in high- lighting acute instances of energy insecurity and can reveal patterns of energy deprivation among vulnerable populations. However, discon- nections are reactive measures, often reflecting the outcome of afford- ability issues rather than chronic systemic inequities. Moreover, variations in disconnection policies across jurisdictions can complicate comparisons, making it challenging to draw g

connection policies across jurisdictions can complicate comparisons, making it challenging to draw generalizable conclusions. Nonetheless, when analyzed alongside other indicators such as energy burden, service disconnections provide a valuable lens to examine recognition justice, as high rates of disconnection among specific pop- ulations may indicate a failure to recognize energy as a fundamental right for all individuals. 3.4.2. SAIDI Index The System Average Interruption Duration Index (SAID

l right for all individuals. 3.4.2. SAIDI Index The System Average Interruption Duration Index (SAIDI) measures the total duration of power outages experienced by an average customer over a specified period, typically reported annually. SAIDI is a robust metric for evaluating energy availability and reliability through a recognition justice lens [40]. A high SAIDI value indicates that residents are experiencing frequent or prolonged power outages, which can severely hinder their ability to meet

e experiencing frequent or prolonged power outages, which can severely hinder their ability to meet basic needs. Data for this metric was compiled from the U.S. Energy Information Administration's annual reports and utility performance reports from the District's primary en- ergy providers. However, SAIDI treats all customers equally, not accounting for the varied impacts outages may have on different socioeconomic groups [2]. For example, frequent outages in low-income neighborhoods can have mo

rent socioeconomic groups [2]. For example, frequent outages in low-income neighborhoods can have more severe consequences, disrupting daily life and increasing vulner- ability. This disproportionate impact on marginalized communities makes SAIDI an important indicator of recognition justice when 5 I.A. Procedural justice 3.5.1. Public participation in energy forums Public participation in energy-related decision-making processes is a core element of procedural justice. This study analyzed attendance re- cords, meeting minutes, and transcripts from the District's

ice. This study analyzed attendance re- cords, meeting minutes, and transcripts from the District's Public Service Commission (PSC) e-docket system, covering the period from January to December 2023, to gauge the level of inclusivity and transparency in energy forums. Meetings were classified based on criteria such as in- formation accessibility, general public access, and attendee diversity. Understanding whether public input was genuinely considered begins with the availability of these meetin

rstanding whether public input was genuinely considered begins with the availability of these meetings to the general public through invitations [41]. sed by examining the avail- ability of meeting minutes or transcripts, recognizing that effective participation requires access to relevant information [42]. ing energy policy, thereby promoting inclusivity and accountability. n public participation can provide insights into procedural justice, they must be interpreted with caution and supplemented with qualitative assessments to capture the true extent of community involvement and depth of their engagement in decision-making forums. 4.
